gmw_00013406.name: J220110 432614
gmw_00013406.overview: J220110 432614 is a galaxy with infrared colors consistent with a quasar [arXiv:1804.11048]. The object is identified as non-stellar and matches an unresolved optical source [arXiv:1804.11048].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J220110 432614 as a quasar-colored WISE counterpart detected across multiple catalogs, classified as non-stellar and matched to an unresolved optical source.

quotes.quote: J220110 432614 matches WISE J220111.10 432614.3 and the colours are consistent with a quasar. This source is also detected in 2MASS, GALEX, Million Optical-Radio/X-ray Associations Catalogue MORX; MORX , the DEep Near-Infrared Survey DENIS; DENIS and X-ray selected AGN in the 6dFGS-RASS catalogue RASS . It is identified as a non-star in the GCS, has a Gaia counterpart Gaia2016 (ID 6570950867938602240) and is present in the Sky-mapper optical database (ID 307655951). It also matches an unresolved optical source in the USNO-B1.0 catalogue.

gmw_00013407.name: J220301 451714
gmw_00013407.overview: J220301 451714 is a non-stellar source that matches an unresolved optical source in the USNO-B1.0 catalogue [arXiv:1804.11048]. It is detected in the GALEX catalogue, and its infrared colors are consistent with a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1804.11048].

sources.summary: The paper reports that J220301 451714 matches a WISE source, is detected by GALEX, has infrared colors consistent with a spiral galaxy, and appears non-stellar in catalogs.

quotes.quote: J220301 451714 matches WISE J220301.66 451715.1 and also detected in GALEX catalogue. Infra-red colours suggests this source is consistent with a spiral galaxy. It is classified as non-stellar source in GSC and also matches an unresolved optical source in the USNO-B1.0 catalogue.

gmw_00013408.name: J220833 453600
gmw_00013408.overview: J220833 453600 is an active galactic nucleus with infrared colors consistent with a quasar [arXiv:1804.11048]. It is a highly variable radio source that exhibits a flat spectrum across widely separated epochs [arXiv:1804.11048].

sources.summary: The paper describes J220833 453600 as a highly variable WISE-matched quasar/AGN with multi-epoch radio variability, flat spectrum, and detections in GALEX, mid-infrared AGN, Sky-mapper, and Gaia catalogs.

quotes.quote: J220833 453600 is a highly variable source with a modulation index m=0.78 between the SUMSS and ASKAP observations on the timescale of 14 years. It was also identified in the archival survey of the Australia Telescope Parkes-MIT-NRAO (ATPMN) at 5 GHz and 8 GHz ATPMN . The source matches WISE J220834.00-453559.4 (Fig. figure:WISE_overlay ) and infrared colours are consistent with a quasar. It is also detected in GALEX and AGN in the Mid-Infrared Secrest2015 catalogues, confirming the source to be an AGN. It is categorised as non-stellar in GSC, detected in the Sky-mapper optical database (ID 308634897) and also has a Gaia counterpart (ID 6567439130879039872).

We observed a 40 change in the flux density of the source at 5.5 GHz from 41 mJy in the ATPMN survey to 23 mJy in our latest ATCA observations. However, the flux density at 8 GHz is consistent with the ATPMN survey within the uncertainties. The spectrum of the source is observed to be flat at widely separated epochs of ATPMN observations (1993 1994) and our recent ATCA observations.

gmw_00013409.name: J221405 460109
gmw_00013409.overview: J221405 460109 is a non-stellar object with infrared colors consistent with a starburst galaxy [arXiv:1804.11048]. It has a SUMSS radio counterpart and is classified in the MORX catalog with a 52 percent probability of being a quasar [arXiv:1804.11048].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J221405 460109 as WISE-matched with starburst-galaxy colors, a non-stellar GSC classification, and a SUMSS counterpart with quasar and galaxy probabilities in MORX.

quotes.quote: J221405 460109 matches WISE J221405.38 460107.1 and is consistent with the infra-red colours of a starburst galaxy. It is identified as non-stellar object in GSC. It also has a SUMSS counterpart with a flux density of 15.9 1 mJy and a 52 probability of being a quasar and 17 probability for a galaxy in the MORX catalogue.

gmw_00013410.name: J215914 471350
gmw_00013410.overview: J215914 471350 is a spiral galaxy identified as a non-stellar, extended source [arXiv:1804.11048]. It has been detected across multiple astronomical surveys, including the 2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey which classifies the object as a galaxy [arXiv:1804.11048].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J215914 471350 as a WISE-matched spiral galaxy, detected in several surveys, classified as a galaxy by 2dFGRS, and matched to an extended Sky-mapper source.

quotes.quote: J215914 471350 matches WISE J215914.38 471350.8 and the colours are consistent with a spiral galaxy. This is also detected in 2MASS, DENIS, the Galaxy List for the Advanced Detector Era catalogue GLADE; GLADE and the 2dF Galaxy Redshift Survey Colless2003 , which classifies this source as a galaxy. This source is identified as non-stellar in GSC. We also searched the Sky-mapper data and found a match with an extended source (ID 308593832).

gmw_00013411.name: GRB 081029 host galaxy
gmw_00013411.overview: The GRB 081029 host galaxy is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.848 [arXiv:1111.6484]. Its optical afterglow spectrum reveals absorption features from hydrogen, neutral elements, and both low and high ionization metallic species [arXiv:1111.6484].

sources.summary: The paper attributes optical-afterglow absorption features to the GRB 081029 host galaxy, listing hydrogen, metal, and fine-structure transitions and assigning a common host redshift of z=3.848.

quotes.quote: The Host Galaxy We detect both hydrogen absorbing features (Ly and Ly ) and several metallic transitions in the spectrum of the optical afterglow. The latter belong both to neutral elements ( O 1 ( 1039) and ( 1302)), low ionization species ( C 2 ( 1334), S 2 ( 1250), ( 1253), and ( 1259), Si 2 ( 1264), ( 1304), and ( 1526), Fe 2 ( 1608), Al 2 ( 1670), Al 3 (1854)) and high ionization species ( N 5 ( 1238) and ( 1242), Si 4 ( 1393) and ( 1402), C 4 ( 1548) and ( 1550), O 6 ( 1031) and ( 1037)). In addition to these features, lines from several fine structure levels of C 2 ( 1334), O 1 ( 1304) and ( 1306), Si 2 ( 1264), ( 1309), and ( 1533), and Fe 2 ( 1618), ( 1621), ( 1631), and ( 1636) are detected. These features are excited by the ultraviolet flux from the GRB afterglow.

Estimates of the typical distance from a GRB to absorption systems suggest distances of 0.1 -1 kpc VLS2007,DFP2009 implying that all these absorption features are due to the GRB 081029 host galaxy. The common redshift of these features is z=3.848 , which we take at the redshift of the host.

gmw_00013412.name: ( RA, Dec ) = (109.7767^ , 23.8854^ )
aliases_external: Likely DECaLS host feature of SN 2020znr
gmw_00013412.overview: The object at ( RA, Dec ) = (109.7767^, 23.8854^ ) is a faint, low-brightness blue dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2201.03887]. It is characterized by low metallicity and is the likely host galaxy of the supernova SN 2020znr [arXiv:2201.03887].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a faint DECaLS feature at SN 2020znr's position as the likely host, a low-metallicity blue dwarf whose light likely does not affect measured supernova polarization, though host dust could.

quotes.quote: The transient is very likely associated to the low brightness feature observed in the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey (DECaLS) Legacy Survey, https://www.legacysurvey.org/ , Data Release 9 (DR9) at position ( RA, Dec ) = (109.7767^ , 23.8854^ ) , J2000, of apparent magnitudes g=23.96, =23.64, z=23.50 mag, i.e. of absolute magnitudes -14.32, -14.64 and -14.78 mag, respectively, assuming it is at the same redshift as the SLSN at z=0.1.

We point out that the object observed in the Legacy survey of magnitude g = 23.96 (see introduction section), very likely the host of SN 2020znr, is very faint with respect to the brightness of SN 2020znr. An intrinsic polarization degree associated to this feature, very likely a blue dwarf galaxy of absolute magnitudes, -14.32 mag in g-band, and -14.64 mag in -band, assuming a redshift z=0.1, is therefore not expected to contribute to the total polarization degree measured on SN 2020znr. On the other hand, the light emitted by SN 2020znr could be polarized by aligned dust grains pervading such a dwarf galaxy, even though it is a low metallicity galaxy. Dust polarization properties of such galaxies are not known and one can not discard the existence of magnetically aligned dust grains in such environments.

gmw_00013413.name: 4FGL J2358.3+3830
gmw_00013413.overview: 4FGL J2358.3+3830 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar blazar located at a redshift of z = 0.200 [arXiv:2003.12629]. The active galactic nucleus exhibits emission lines with an equivalent width greater than 5 [arXiv:2003.12629].

sources.summary: The paper measures z=0.200 for 4FGL J2358.3+3830, detects emission lines with EW above 5, and classifies the WISE counterpart as a BZQ blazar.

quotes.quote: Particularly we successfully measured redshifts for 3 of the 4 BZBs listed in the 4FGL (see Section sec:sample ). They are 4FGL J0420.2+4012/WISE J042013.43+401121.2 with z = 0.132, 4FGL J2220.5+2813/WISE J222028.72+281355.8 with z = 0.148 and 4FGL J2358.3+3830/WISE J235825.19+382856.5 with z = 0.200 . For the last source, we observed emission lines with EW > 5 , and thus classified it as a BZQ.

We observed 4 sources classified as BZBs in 4FGL and were able to measure the redshifts for 3 of them: 4FGL J0420.2+4012, 4FGL J2220.5+2813 and 4FGL J2358.3+3830, with z = 0.132, z = 0.148 and z = 0.200 respectively. In particular, 4FGL J2358.3+3830 presents emission lines with EW > 5 , thus being classified as BZQ.

gmw_00013414.name: J0736+4759
gmw_00013414.overview: J0736+4759 is an obscured active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:1609.04018]. It is classified as rotation-dominated with a central obscuration, displaying a highly symmetric double-peaked profile that may be caused by radiative transfer effects from a central dust lane [arXiv:1609.04018].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0736+4759 as a rotation-dominated obscured AGN host with unusually symmetric double-peaked profiles and significantly lower asymmetry than the other rotation-dominated galaxies.

quotes.quote: We classify one galaxy (J0736+4759) from the sample of 71 galaxies as Rotation Dominated + Obscuration due to its high degree of symmetry. Radiative transfer effects from a central dust lane could account for a single peaked Gaussian with a decrease in flux at zero velocity at both observed position angles, which would produce a double-peaked profile.

We find that the A value of J0736+4759 (0.12) is significantly less than the mean of the overall sample and that the mean of the A values for the group including J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 (0.58) is consistent with mean of the overall sample. J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 are classified as asymmetric rotation-dominated galaxies quantitatively according to sample statistics. We classify them as Rotation Dominated + Disturbance. We classify J0736+4759 as Rotation Dominated + Obscuration.

Our clearest example of this type of a rotation-dominated galaxy is the galaxy J0736+4759, which is rotation-dominated with a central obscuration due to its high degree of symmetry. The peaks are of equal flux for both position angles across the entire slit. This galaxy is consistent with the EPAGNs discussed in ( Smith2012 ).

gmw_00013415.name: J1018+5127
gmw_00013415.overview: J1018+5127 is a candidate dual active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1609.04018]. Its spatially resolved profiles match the predictions for a rotation-dominated system, leading to its classification as rotation-dominated with a disturbance [arXiv:1609.04018].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1018+5127 as a candidate dual AGN whose spatially resolved profiles match a rotation-dominated dual-AGN prediction and classify it as Rotation Dominated + Disturbance.

quotes.quote: We determine that one of the galaxies in the sample matches this observational prediction of a spectral profile of a rotation-dominated dual AGN. This galaxy, J1018+5127, is classified as `Rotation Dominated + Disturbance'. Figure J1018 both first shows the spatially-resolved profiles at both PAs for this candidate dual AGN.
The rotation-dominated galaxies have asymmetry values of 0.12 (J0736+4759), 0.57 (J1018+5127), -0.43 (J1250+0746), and -0.72 (J1516+0517).

We find that the A value of J0736+4759 (0.12) is significantly less than the mean of the overall sample and that the mean of the A values for the group including J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 (0.58) is consistent with mean of the overall sample. J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 are classified as asymmetric rotation-dominated galaxies quantitatively according to sample statistics. We classify them as Rotation Dominated + Disturbance.

gmw_00013416.name: J1250+0746
gmw_00013416.overview: J1250+0746 is a rotation-dominated galaxy that exhibits structural disturbances [arXiv:1609.04018]. Its measured asymmetry value is consistent with the mean of its sample, leading to its classification as a disturbed, rotation-dominated system [arXiv:1609.04018].

sources.summary: The paper measures J1250+0746 among the four rotation-dominated galaxies, finding its asymmetry consistent with the sample mean and classifying it as Rotation Dominated + Disturbance.

quotes.quote: The rotation-dominated galaxies have asymmetry values of 0.12 (J0736+4759), 0.57 (J1018+5127), -0.43 (J1250+0746), and -0.72 (J1516+0517).

Given the sample standard deviation (0.15) for this measurement, we can initially conclude that J0736+4759 is about two standard deviations below the mean and the remaining three rotation-dominated galaxies are consistent with the mean (J1250+0746), one standard deviation greater than the mean asymmetry (J1018+5127) and about two standard deviations above the mean (J1516+0517).

We find that the A value of J0736+4759 (0.12) is significantly less than the mean of the overall sample and that the mean of the A values for the group including J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 (0.58) is consistent with mean of the overall sample. J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 are classified as asymmetric rotation-dominated galaxies quantitatively according to sample statistics. We classify them as Rotation Dominated + Disturbance.

gmw_00013417.name: J1516+0517
gmw_00013417.overview: J1516+0517 is classified as a rotation-dominated galaxy with a disturbance [arXiv:1609.04018]. It exhibits an asymmetry value that is approximately two standard deviations above the mean for its sample [arXiv:1609.04018].

sources.summary: The paper measures J1516+0517 among the rotation-dominated galaxies, reporting asymmetry about two standard deviations above the mean and classifying it as Rotation Dominated + Disturbance.

quotes.quote: The rotation-dominated galaxies have asymmetry values of 0.12 (J0736+4759), 0.57 (J1018+5127), -0.43 (J1250+0746), and -0.72 (J1516+0517).

Given the sample standard deviation (0.15) for this measurement, we can initially conclude that J0736+4759 is about two standard deviations below the mean and the remaining three rotation-dominated galaxies are consistent with the mean (J1250+0746), one standard deviation greater than the mean asymmetry (J1018+5127) and about two standard deviations above the mean (J1516+0517).

We find that the A value of J0736+4759 (0.12) is significantly less than the mean of the overall sample and that the mean of the A values for the group including J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 (0.58) is consistent with mean of the overall sample. J1018+5127, J1250+0746, and J1516+0517 are classified as asymmetric rotation-dominated galaxies quantitatively according to sample statistics. We classify them as Rotation Dominated + Disturbance.

gmw_00013418.name: J0045.5 6726
gmw_00013418.overview: J0045.5 6726 is a compact Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy with a linear size of 164 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1308.0884]. It resides in a rich environment, where a denser galaxy distribution along its longer radio axis may help preserve its diffuse lobe emission by limiting expansion losses [arXiv:1308.0884].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0045.5 6726 as an anomalous compact asymmetric FRI source in a rich environment, with denser galaxy distribution along its longer radio side and possible lobe preservation by confinement.

quotes.quote: J0045.5 6726, which alone has a positive value for a_ 3 , has a significantly smaller linear size of 164 kpc and, therefore, the anomalous behavior for this source may be understood as arising from its relatively small size due to which the lobe extent may be more influenced by the local inter-stellar medium of the host galaxy rather than the intergalactic gas associated with the large scale galaxy distribution, which is what is probed by our approach. The high value of a_ 1 for the FRI source J0045.5 6726 indicates a rich environment for this source; however, the positive sign for a_ 3 implies that the longer side of the source is in denser regions. Additionally, the source has a high value for a_ 5 , showing that the density distribution is along the radio axis. This source has an FRI morphology and an alternate explanation for the positive value of a_3 for this source may be that the higher galaxy density on the longer side preserves the diffuse emission on that side by limiting expansion losses.

gmw_00013419.name: GBS-VLA J032920.67+311549.5
gmw_00013419.overview: GBS-VLA J032920.67+311549.5 is an extended extragalactic radio source associated with the cataloged source VLA 32 [arXiv:1511.07522]. It is proposed to be a radio galaxy characterized by non-thermal synchrotron emission, displaying radio lobes and jet-like structures [arXiv:1511.07522].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GBS-VLA J032920.67+311549.5 as an extended extragalactic radio source associated with VLA 32 and NVSS 032920+311549, grouping it with nonthermal lobe-and-jet sources proposed to be radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: In Figure fig:GBS032920 we show radio maps of source GBS-VLA J032920.67+311549.5 in NGC 1333 at 4.5 GHz and 7.5 GHz, corresponding to the concatenation of the three epochs. This source is 0 33 from the previously cataloged radio source VLA 32 which was classified as extragalactic ( 2011ApJ...736...25F ; 1999ApJS..125..427R ). Based on the angular separation from GBS-VLA J032920.67+311549.5, it is likely that VLA 32 is the counterpart of our detection. This source is also associated with NVSS 032920+311549 reported by 1998AJ....115.1693C (the angular separation is only 1 47), which has a flux at 1.4 GHz of 8.7 mJy.

This is consistent with all sources having negative spectral indices which suggests non-thermal emission, most probably synchrotron. Relativistic beaming of the synchroton emission can explain why the lobes and jet like emission are observed stronger and more defined in one direction than the other. Based on these characteristics we propose that these sources correspond to at least 4 radio galaxies.

gmw_00013420.name: GBS-VLA J034411.69+321039.4
gmw_00013420.overview: GBS-VLA J034411.69+321039.4 is a proposed radio galaxy with no previously reported counterparts in the SIMBAD or NVSS catalogs [arXiv:1511.07522]. The source exhibits negative-spectral-index synchrotron emission, which is characterized by asymmetric lobes and jet-like structures [arXiv:1511.07522].

sources.summary: The paper reports no SIMBAD or NVSS counterpart for GBS-VLA J034411.69+321039.4, then groups the extended source with negative-spectral-index synchrotron objects proposed to be radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: The source GBS-VLA J034411.69+321039.4 has no previously reported counterpart in the SIMBAD catalog, and no associated source was found in the NVSS catalog.

This is consistent with all sources having negative spectral indices which suggests non-thermal emission, most probably synchrotron. Relativistic beaming of the synchroton emission can explain why the lobes and jet like emission are observed stronger and more defined in one direction than the other. Based on these characteristics we propose that these sources correspond to at least 4 radio galaxies.

gmw_00013421.name: GBS-VLA J034421.76+320918.3
gmw_00013421.overview: GBS-VLA J034421.76+320918.3 is proposed to be an extragalactic radio galaxy [arXiv:1511.07522]. It exhibits non-thermal synchrotron emission with asymmetric lobes and jet-like structures, which may be explained by relativistic beaming [arXiv:1511.07522].

sources.summary: The paper treats GBS-VLA J034421.76+320918.3 as a possible radio galaxy, questions the nearby star identification, and groups it with nonthermal synchrotron sources proposed to be radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: The source GBS-VLA J034421.76+320918.3 is 1 58 from previously cataloged source Cl*IC 348 MM 42 detected by 2003ApJ...597..555M in infrared observations of IC 348. The source Cl*IC 348 MM 42 is classified as a star, yet no spectral type nor SED classification is given for this source ( 2003A&A...409..147P ; 2003ApJ...597..555M ). To our knowledge, there is no other identification for Cl*IC 348 MM 42. Based on their angular separation, it is likely that Cl*IC 348 MM 42 is the infrared counterpart of our detection, but if GBS-VLA J034421.76+320918.3 is actually a radio galaxy (as we will discuss later) these sources might be completely unrelated and just in the same direction as observed from the earth. Alternatively, Cl*IC 348 MM 42 may have been erroneously identified as a star, being instead an extragalactic source.

This is consistent with all sources having negative spectral indices which suggests non-thermal emission, most probably synchrotron. Relativistic beaming of the synchroton emission can explain why the lobes and jet like emission are observed stronger and more defined in one direction than the other. Based on these characteristics we propose that these sources correspond to at least 4 radio galaxies.

gmw_00013422.name: NVSS 034433+321255
gmw_00013422.overview: NVSS 034433+321255 is a radio galaxy candidate located outside the Perseus Cluster [arXiv:1511.07522]. Its emission encompasses the combined, unresolved emission of two nearby GBS-VLA sources, which exhibit non-thermal synchrotron emission with jet-like structures and lobes [arXiv:1511.07522].

sources.summary: The paper links NVSS 034433+321255 to combined emission from nearby GBS-VLA sources and includes the extended synchrotron structures among radio-galaxy candidates outside the Perseus Cluster.

quotes.quote: The source GBS-VLA 034433.91+321307.5 has no previously reported counterpart in the SIMBAD catalog. This source is 13 7 from radio source NVSS 034433+321255, with a reported flux at 1.4 GHz of 223.0 mJy 1998AJ....115.1693C . Based on the angular separation to GBS-VLA J034433.04+321241.3 and GBS-VLA 034433.91+321307.5, and the angular resolution of the NVSS observations ( = 45 ), we can assume that sources GBS-VLA J034433.04+321241.3 and GBS-VLA 034433.91+321307.5 might have been detected, yet not resolved, by 1998AJ....115.1693C and that the NVSS 034433+321255 emission would encompass the combined emission of both of our sources.

This is consistent with all sources having negative spectral indices which suggests non-thermal emission, most probably synchrotron. Relativistic beaming of the synchroton emission can explain why the lobes and jet like emission are observed stronger and more defined in one direction than the other. Based on these characteristics we propose that these sources correspond to at least 4 radio galaxies.

gmw_00013423.name: 4C+14.23
gmw_00013423.overview: 4C+14.23 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z=1.038 [arXiv:1510.00048]. The active galactic nucleus was observed flaring in GeV energy by the Fermi-LAT on October 13, 2009 [arXiv:1510.00048].

sources.summary: The paper names 4C+14.23 as the nearest flaring FSRQ to IceCube event 38, gives its redshift and angular distance, and notes Fermi-LAT observed it flaring in GeV energy.

quotes.quote: The nearest object is found to be 4C+14.23 which is a FSRQ at a redshift of z=1.038 and at an angular distance of 3.1^ . This FSRQ was also observed by Fermi-LAT in GeV flare on 13th of October 2009.
We found the nearest flaring FSRQ, 4C+14.23 to event 38 at a angular distance of 3.1^ which was also observed by Fermi-LAT when it was flaring in GeV energy .

gmw_00013424.name: host galaxy of the burst
gmw_00013424.overview: The host galaxy of the burst is an extended structure oriented in an east-west direction [arXiv:1203.4265]. It has a star formation rate typical of long gamma-ray burst hosts, and the burst itself occurred at an offset of approximately 5.8 kiloparsecs from the galactic center [arXiv:1203.4265].

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 091018 host as an extended east-west galaxy with fitted orientation, a 5.8 kpc burst offset, host magnitude 23.4 AB, and [O II]-based star-formation rates typical of long GRB hosts.

quotes.quote: In the late time images taken with Gemini (see Table table:photlog ) the host galaxy can clearly be seen as an extended structure, see Figure fig:host , visibly extended in East-West direction. An isophote fit using elliptical isophotes (using the ellipse package within IRAF) gave a best fit for an ellipse orientation of 89 4 degrees.

We found the GRB location to be offset by approximately 5.8 kiloparsec from the centre of the galaxy (as found from the elliptical isophote fits), see Fig fig:host . This offset, as well as the observed host magnitude, = 23.4 (AB; see Table table:photlog ), is well within the observed distribution for long GRBs at this redshift (e.g. Bloom,savaglio ).

Its flux of 6 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 can be used to derive an estimate of the starformation rate: using the equation from we find a SFR ( [ O II ] ) 4 Solar masses per year. If we instead use the empirical conversion from [ O II ] line luminosity to star formation rate derived by for GRB host galaxies, we find SFR ( [ O II ] ) 1.6 Solar masses per year (note that no extinction correction was applied to the [ O II ] line luminosity). This SFR ( [ O II ] ) is fairly typical for long GRB hosts ( savaglio ). In conclusion, the afterglow spectrum, host magnitude and morphology, and star formation rate are all in line with expectations for a normal long GRB host galaxy.

gmw_00013425.name: J1257+6349
gmw_00013425.overview: J1257+6349 is classified as a weak-line quasar [arXiv:2112.07799]. In its spectrum, the carbon IV emission line is barely detected, while the helium II emission line is detected [arXiv:2112.07799].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1257+6349 as a weak-line quasar with a measured line ratio of 3.57; it is marginally within the sample range and has negligible effect on the median.

quotes.quote: There are two quasars in our sample, J1429+5447 and J1257+6349, labeled as weak-line quasars (WLQ) by Shen19a . Their / \ ratios are 6.35 and 3.57, respectively. In their spectra, \ is barely detected. Their equivalent widths are less than 10 , while \ is detected.
Indeed, J1429+5447 has the highest / \ ratio in our sample, while J1257+6349 is marginally within the 16th 84th range. If we exclude these two WLQs, the median / \ ratio decreases from 2.54 to 2.52.

gmw_00013426.name: Source 27
gmw_00013426.overview: Source 27 is a short Chandra X-ray transient that exhibits flaring behavior [arXiv:2008.09200]. It has a faint potential counterpart in the UKIDSS Galactic plane survey classified as a galaxy, which may indicate the source is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2008.09200].

sources.summary: The paper describes Source 27 as a short Chandra transient with flaring behavior, a faint UKIDSS counterpart classified as a galaxy, and a possible AGN interpretation if that counterpart is real.

quotes.quote: Lastly, Source 27 is a short transient only detected in ObsID 21126. This source shows flaring behavior, with the longest duration flare lasting 2 ks. The total number of photons is small (32 6 in total) precluding any meaningful spectral analysis. There is one potential counterpart in the UKIDSS Galactic plane survey that lies just at the edge ( 0 65 ) of the source's PU and has magnitudes J=18.5 , H=17.6 , and K=17.1 , and is classified as a galaxy in the UKIDDS catalog. If this source is the true counterpart, then Source 27 could be an AGN.

gmw_00013427.name: CRATES J033149-105155
gmw_00013427.overview: CRATES J033149-105155 is a radio-bright blazar [arXiv:1810.04194]. Although located near a gamma-ray signal, likelihood-test analysis suggests the emission is unlikely to originate from this source [arXiv:1810.04194].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CRATES J033149-105155 as one of two nearby radio-bright blazars, but finds weak likelihood-test support and argues the gamma-ray signal is unlikely to originate from the CRATES blazars.

quotes.quote: As a result of our search we find two radio-bright blazars in the CRATES catalog located within 1.5 ^ of : CRATES J033149-105155 and CRATES J032952-100251. A re-analysis of the binned likelihood including point sources at the locations of both blazars with a fixed power law index of = 2.2 , near both the median and mean value for a blazar in the 3FGL catalog, yields TS < 2.5 for both blazars and an TS 21 . This, combined with the fact that the best fit power law 3.6 is well outside of the normal range of spectral indices for blazars, suggests that the signal is unlikely to originate from overlapping blazars in the CRATES catalog.

gmw_00013428.name: CRATES J032952-100251
gmw_00013428.overview: CRATES J032952-100251 is a radio-bright blazar [arXiv:1810.04194]. Although it is located near a gamma-ray signal, analysis suggests the emission is unlikely to originate from this source [arXiv:1810.04194].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CRATES J032952-100251 as one of two nearby radio-bright blazars, but finds weak likelihood-test support and argues the gamma-ray signal is unlikely to originate from the CRATES blazars.

quotes.quote: As a result of our search we find two radio-bright blazars in the CRATES catalog located within 1.5 ^ of : CRATES J033149-105155 and CRATES J032952-100251. A re-analysis of the binned likelihood including point sources at the locations of both blazars with a fixed power law index of = 2.2 , near both the median and mean value for a blazar in the 3FGL catalog, yields TS < 2.5 for both blazars and an TS 21 . This, combined with the fact that the best fit power law 3.6 is well outside of the normal range of spectral indices for blazars, suggests that the signal is unlikely to originate from overlapping blazars in the CRATES catalog.

gmw_00013429.name: ICRF J054138.0 054149
gmw_00013429.overview: ICRF J054138.0 054149 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1609.07255]. It is very faint in the optical band, with a Gaia magnitude of 20.7, and has no visible nearby host galaxy or bright star in the 2MASS or SDSS surveys [arXiv:1609.07255].

sources.summary: The paper flags ICRF J054138.0 054149 as a highly deviating non-VCS source with large Gaia uncertainties, many matched detections, no visible nearby host, and faint Gaia magnitude.

quotes.quote: The most deviating non-VCS source in this category is ICRF J054138.0 054149 ( =117.9  mas, X=1.3 ). The auxiliary quasar solution has large uncertainties for this source, although there is a good number of matched detections. No nearby bright star or host galaxy is visible in the 2MASS or SDSS survey, but the source is very faint for Gaia at G= 20.7 .

gmw_00013430.name: ICRF J155850.2-643229
gmw_00013430.overview: ICRF J155850.2-643229 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1609.07255]. The source is associated with the central region of the galaxy 2MASX J15585027-6432298, exhibiting a 10 milliarcsecond offset between its optical and radio centers [arXiv:1609.07255].

sources.summary: The paper describes ICRF J155850.2-643229 as the most discrepant defining source, possibly tied to the central part of 2MASX J15585027-6432298, with a 10 mas optical-center offset.

quotes.quote: The most discrepant defining source is ICRF J155850.2-643229 ( X=32 , =9.97  mas). The relatively bright source seen by Gaia ( G=16.3 ) could be the central part of the galaxy 2MASX J15585027-6432298 with a total magnitude around 14 in the Gaia band. The 10 mas offset corresponds to a shift of the optical centre by only 15 pc at the distance of the galaxy, which is not unreasonable if the optical emission associated with the radio source is not strong.

gmw_00013431.name: brightest cluster galaxy
gmw_00013431.overview: This galaxy is the brightest cluster galaxy of the Abell 523 cluster [arXiv:1104.3711]. It is located within a compact north-northeast galaxy clump offset from the cluster's X-ray gas peak, and it has no associated radio emission [arXiv:1104.3711].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Abell 523's BCG by coordinates and redshift, notes no associated radio emission, and places it in the compact NNE clump offset from the X-ray gas peak.

quotes.quote: To date, only the redshift for the brightest cluster galaxy [ BCG at R.A.= 04^ h 59^ m 12 97 , Dec.= +08 49 41.3 (J2000.0) ] has been acquired ( z=0.1036 ; Crawford et al. ). No radio emission is clearly associated with this galaxy.

To determine the CMR, we considered the 75 galaxies within a radius of 4 from the BCG and applied a two-sigma-clipping fitting procedure, obtaining B_j - R = 2.044-0.024 R for a subsample of 54 galaxies. The galaxy distribution reveals two significant peaks 5 ( 0.5 Mpc) far: the NNE peak, coincident with the BCG, and the SSW peak, with no dominant galaxy, at R.A.= 04^ h 59^ m 05 4 , Dec.= +08 45 06 (J2000.0).

However, a clear shift between the galaxies and gas distribution is present (see Fig. fig:5 ) and the BCG is not coincident with the X-ray peak. This cluster as shown by its irregular shape and lack of a dominant galaxy, was not in a relaxed stage, and is now strongly interacting (major merging) with a more compact cluster (the NNE structure) dominated by a bright BCG. As discussed in Sect. 2, the X-ray and optical distribution is similar (bimodal) but not in perfect agreement. The thermal gas distribution is shifted with respect to the galaxy distribution; this is more evident for the smaller and more compact NNE clump, where the position of the BCG is not at the center of the gas distribution.

gmw_00013432.name: G
gmw_00013432.overview: Galaxy G is a gravitational lensing galaxy that acts as a lens for a background quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0606317]. It has an estimated redshift of 0.58, which is deduced from absorption lines observed in the lensed quasar's spectra [arXiv:astro-ph/0606317].

sources.summary: The paper uses astrometry and flux ratios to model lensing galaxy G, assuming a redshift from absorption lines and comparing SIS and de Vaucouleurs mass profiles.

quotes.quote: The observational constraints available so far to model the potential well in \ consist of the relative astrometry of the lensed images A and B with respect to the lensing galaxy G (Morgan et al. ), and the image flux ratio measured in Section 4. Lacking a direct spectrum of the lensing galaxy, we assume a lens redshift \ = 0.58, as deduced from the MgII and FeII absorption lines observed in the spectra of the lensed quasar (Morgan et al. ). This value of the lens redshift drives all the following estimates of the Hubble constant, which scales as (1+ ). Using the lensmodel package (v 1.08; Keeton ), we fit the lens system with (i) a Singular Isothermal Sphere model plus external shear (SIS) and (ii) a de Vaucouleurs model (DV).

The SIS+ model has just enough parameters to be exactly constrained by the data (i.e. zero degree of freedom), but the DV model has one more parameter, the effective radius R_e of the lensing galaxy, that is unknown with the existing imaging data of . We therefore run our models with a fixed effective radius. We consider a wide range of plausible effective radii, 0.2  kpc < R_e < 20  kpc, and take the two boundaries for our calculations.

gmw_00013433.name: SDSS J140654.82 + 525310.7
gmw_00013433.overview: SDSS J140654.82 + 525310.7 is a faint object that has been classified as both a star and a galaxy [arXiv:1103.2132]. Its color space measurements are inconsistent with it hosting a quasi-stellar object, and it is considered an unlikely counterpart for Chandra/RASS X-ray emission [arXiv:1103.2132].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J140654.82 + 525310.7 as a possible background galaxy or star, finds its colors inconsistent with a QSO host, and rules it out as the Chandra/RASS counterpart.

quotes.quote: At these magnitudes, the automated SDSS star/galaxy separation becomes unreliable scranton02 ; indeed, the object is classified as both a star (in ri ) and as a galaxy ( ugz ).

If SDSS J140654.82 + 525310.7 is instead a background galaxy, its (u-g) = -0.85 0.55 is well below the typical (u-g) = 0.6 threshold commonly used to identify low-redshift SDSS QSOs. However, such a blue (u-g) color is beyond what is typically seen for even the bluest SDSS objects, and the galaxy's position elsewhere in color space is inconsistent with it hosting a QSO richards02 . A more likely explanation for this anomalously large UV-excess is the red leak of the SDSS u band filter, which is worst for the reddest objects.
Ordinary galaxies are weak X-ray emitters, and we conclude that SDSS J140654.82 + 525310.7, whether star or galaxy, is unlikely to be the Chandra /RASS counterpart.

gmw_00013434.name: AXJ1511.7+0758
gmw_00013434.overview: AXJ1511.7+0758 is a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus with a narrow-line optical spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0601245]. Its steep X-ray spectrum suggests that a large fraction of its X-ray emission may originate from star-forming processes [arXiv:astro-ph/0601245].

sources.summary: The paper identifies AXJ1511.7+0758 as the least luminous sample source with a narrow-line optical spectrum; its steep, low-luminosity X-ray emission may include star-forming processes, improving with a thermal component.

quotes.quote: The other source, AXJ1511.7+0758, is the less luminous in our sample. Its optical counterpart has a NL spectrum. The relatively low luminosity combined with the steep X-ray spectrum raises the possibility that a large fraction of the X-ray emission comes from star-forming processes. Thus we have added a Raymond-Smith component to the X-ray spectrum. The addition of this component significantly improves the fit ( ^2 36.9/20 for kT 0.2 keV).

gmw_00013435.name: GRB host galaxy
gmw_00013435.overview: The GRB host galaxy is located at a redshift of z = 0.903 [arXiv:1201.3885]. It exhibits modest host galaxy extinction and has a low gas column density [arXiv:1201.3885].

sources.summary: The paper reports the GRB host galaxy at redshift z=0.903, visible in optical images, offset from the afterglow, with modest host extinction and gas column density from SED fitting.

quotes.quote: The redshift of its underlying host galaxy was finally measured using VLT/FORS2 about 2.3 days after the trigger ( z =0.903; Rau2009GCN9353,McBreen2010 ).

The first night, the GRB host galaxy is clearly visible in the optical images, with the afterglow light dominating its western part (Fig. fig:090510fc ). While on the first night the afterglow was detected in g^ ^ i^ z^ but not in JHK_s , and the second night there was no sign of an afterglow in any band, except the host galaxy. Image subtraction clearly reveals the afterglow between the first- and the second-epoch of the combined g^ ^ i^ z^ images using HOTPANTS. http://www.astro.washington.edu/users/becker/hotpants.html Its coordinates measured against the USNO-B1 catalog are RA (J2000) = 22:14:12.53, Dec. = 26:34:59.0, with an error of 0 2 in each coordinate. The afterglow lies about 1 2 west of the center of its host galaxy (see also McBreen2010 ).

For SMC dust and a redshift of z=0.903 , it finds a host galaxy extinction of A_V^ host = 0.17_ 0.17 ^ +0.21 mag, a gas column density of N_ H ^ host = 0.05_ 0.05 ^ +0.15 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , and a spectral slope of _ opt =0.85 0.05 ( ^2 /d.o.f.=0.93).

gmw_00013436.name: BCG
gmw_00013436.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) is associated with a radio active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1102.0782]. However, the BCG itself appears slightly offset from this radio source, and hot intracluster plasma regions are located to its north and west [arXiv:1102.0782].

sources.summary: The paper describes the cluster BCG as associated with a radio AGN, slightly offset from it, and used as a reference point for nearby hot intracluster plasma.

quotes.quote: The brightest eastern component is associated with the BCG and can be classified as a radio AGN.
The BCG appears slightly offset from the radio AGN.
Hot regions are present to the north and west of the BCG.

gmw_00013437.name: second galaxy
gmw_00013437.overview: The second galaxy is located at a redshift of 0.162, approximately 90 kpc northeast of the main brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:1102.0782]. It is likely the brightest cluster galaxy of a smaller, infalling cluster that is currently interacting with the main system [arXiv:1102.0782].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the second galaxy northeast of the BCG as likely the BCG of a smaller infalling cluster, with a similar redshift and probable interaction.

quotes.quote: A second X-ray bright galaxy is visible 90 kpc to the northeast of the BCG, at a redshift of 0.162 . This galaxy is likely to be the BCG of a smaller, infalling galaxy cluster.
The BCG of the galaxy cluster is located at a redshift of 0.16, while the second galaxy is at a redshift of 0.162.

Both galaxies are visible in X-ray, at a projected distance of 90 kpc. Based on the ratio of their X-ray luminosities, the smaller galaxy appears to be the BCG of a second galaxy cluster with a mass of 10-40 percent of the mass of 1431, and given the similar redshifts of the two galaxies, they are likely interacting.

gmw_00013438.name: galaxy at 17h38m35.69s + 427 ^ 32'57.8"
gmw_00013438.overview: The galaxy at 17h38m35.69s + 427 ^ 32'57.8" is a blue galaxy that contaminated the 2014 i-band spectrum of the Y dwarf W1738 [arXiv:2403.12464]. It is visible in u, g, and i optical bands, but it is not detected in deep z-band or OSIRIS images [arXiv:2403.12464].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this coordinate-selected galaxy as a blue contaminant of W1738's 2014 i-band spectrum, visible in several HiPERCAM optical bands but not in deep z-band or earlier OSIRIS data.

quotes.quote: Thanks to our deep optical images, we found that the 2014 i band spectrum was contaminated by a galaxy bluer than the Y dwarf.

However, this bluer colour is spurious, owing to contamination by a galaxy at 17h38m35.69s + 427 ^ 32'57.8" in 2014. The galaxy is not visible in the deep z band images nor in the GTC/OSIRIS data obtained in 2012 by or the GTC/HiPERCAM data obtained by us in 2021, but it is clearly visible in our GTC/HiPERCAM u , g , , and i bands.

gmw_00013439.name: J000224
gmw_00013439.overview: The active galaxy J000224 displays clear visual evidence of blueshifted wings in its [OIII] emission lines [arXiv:1711.01269]. This profile features a broad component offset by 291 km s^-1 with a FWHM of 1227 km s^-1, which is indicative of ionized outflows or highly turbulent material [arXiv:1711.01269].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J000224 as one of two sources with clear blueshifted [OIII] wings, measuring a broad component offset by 291 km s^-1 with FWHM 1227 km s^-1.

quotes.quote: We also present two objects, J000224 and J155048, with the clearest visual evidence of blueshifted wings in the [ OIII ] 5007 \ and [ OIII ] 4959 \ emission lines.

The results of our fitting for J000224 and J155048 are shown in Figures  fig:J000224 and  fig:J155048 respectively with the narrow and broad components colour-coded. In J000224 the broad component is offset by a velocity of 291 km s ^ 1 with a FWHM of 1227 km s ^ 1 . In J155048 the broad component is offset by a velocity of 168 km s ^ 1 with a FWHM of 1506 km s ^ 1 . These values are roughly consistent with similar spectroscopic studies of [ OIII ] emission e.g. Vega01Kinem, Mullaney13Kinem, Collet16feedback, Harrison16KASHz, Sun17Kinem .

Our broad component FWHM therefore overestimate these measurements but likely still satisfy the w_ 80 >600 km s ^ 1 criterion outlined in Collet16feedback and Liu13feedback indicative of either ionised outflows or highly turbulent material. These objects are potentially of interest for further study with highly resolved imaging to understand their host properties and the location of the turbulent or outflowing material.

gmw_00013440.name: J155048
gmw_00013440.overview: J155048 is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1711.01269]. The galaxy exhibits clear visual evidence of blueshifted wings in its [OIII] emission lines, which feature a broad component offset by 168 km s^-1 with a FWHM of 1506 km s^-1 [arXiv:1711.01269].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J155048 as an AGN and highlights blueshifted [OIII] wings, measuring a broad component offset by 168 km s^-1 with FWHM 1506 km s^-1.

quotes.quote: This allows us to additionally identify J155048 as an AGN, which had its [ NeIII ] emission lie on a chip gap, whereas the remaining nine already lie in the AGN regime of the TBT diagram.
We also present two objects, J000224 and J155048, with the clearest visual evidence of blueshifted wings in the [ OIII ] 5007 \ and [ OIII ] 4959 \ emission lines.

The results of our fitting for J000224 and J155048 are shown in Figures  fig:J000224 and  fig:J155048 respectively with the narrow and broad components colour-coded. In J000224 the broad component is offset by a velocity of 291 km s ^ 1 with a FWHM of 1227 km s ^ 1 . In J155048 the broad component is offset by a velocity of 168 km s ^ 1 with a FWHM of 1506 km s ^ 1 . These values are roughly consistent with similar spectroscopic studies of [ OIII ] emission e.g. Vega01Kinem, Mullaney13Kinem, Collet16feedback, Harrison16KASHz, Sun17Kinem . Our broad component FWHM therefore overestimate these measurements but likely still satisfy the w_ 80 >600 km s ^ 1 criterion outlined in Collet16feedback and Liu13feedback indicative of either ionised outflows or highly turbulent material. These objects are potentially of interest for further study with highly resolved imaging to understand their host properties and the location of the turbulent or outflowing material.

gmw_00013441.name: galaxy ``A''
gmw_00013441.overview: Galaxy "A" is an emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.9578 [arXiv:astro-ph/0107331]. It is the likely host of GRB 970828 and may be part of an interacting system with a neighboring galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0107331].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy ``A'' as the likely z = 0.9578 host of GRB 970828, with [ O II ] and [ Ne III ] emission and a possible interacting, dusty environment.

quotes.quote: The subsequent optical observation by the Palomar 200-inch telescope revealed extended sources around the radio position . It shows that the radio source is located between two bright optical peaks; Djorgovski (2001) suggest that the GRB occured inside a dust lane (corresponding to a dark gap between two bright peaks ``A'' and ``B'' in their paper), or at an interface of two interacting galaxies. Following spectroscopic study by the Keck on the galaxy ``A'' clearly displayed two emission lines which are interpreted as [ O II ] and [ Ne III ] lines. This gives a redshift of z = 0.9578 for the galaxy which is likely a host of GRB 970828 .

gmw_00013442.name: B1827+0617
gmw_00013442.overview: B1827+0617 is a blazar and flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.75 [arXiv:0906.2792]. The source is characterized by flat-spectrum radio emission, X-ray and optical detections, and optical variability [arXiv:0906.2792].

sources.summary: The paper identifies B1827+0617 as the likely counterpart of 0FGL J1830.3+0617, with flat-spectrum radio emission, X-ray and optical detections, variability, and a redshift supporting an FSRQ/blazar classification.

quotes.quote: Within the \ 95 confidence error contour lies B1827+0617, a radio source with spectral index = 0.09 between 1.4 and 4.85 GHz. The flat spectral index and flux density of 443 mJy at 4.85 GHz are consistent with the bulk of \ sources associated with blazars. It is also detected in the 0.3-10 keV band by . Optical imaging in 2009 May identifies B1827+0617 at R 16.9 , and shows that it is at least 2 magnitudes brighter than on the Palomar Sky Survey plates.

Contemporaneous optical spectroscopy acquired during this high state finds a weak emission line that we attribute to Mg 2 2798 at redshift z = 0.75 , supporting a flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) classification. The variability characteristics and radio properties together indicate that 0FGL J1830.3+0617 at Galactic latitude b = +7. ^ 5 is a blazar.

Magnitudes were measured using a 1 5 radius aperture centered at the optical position of the object at coordinates (J2000.0) R.A.= 18^ h 30^ m 05. ^ s 95 , decl.= +06^ 19 16 2 in the USNO-B1.0 astrometric system.

gmw_00013443.name: the host galaxy
gmw_00013443.overview: The host galaxy of AT 2019pim is characterized by a moderate stellar mass of approximately 1.9 billion solar masses [arXiv:2401.16470]. It exhibits a moderate star-formation rate and low-to-moderate dust extinction, which are typical properties for star-forming galaxies at similar redshifts [arXiv:2401.16470].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes the AT 2019pim host galaxy through SED fitting, finding moderate star formation, moderate stellar mass, low-to-moderate dust extinction, and properties typical of star-forming long-GRB hosts.

quotes.quote: The late-time filter coverage is (marginally) sufficient to obtain basic constraints on the fundamental properties of the host galaxy using SED fitting. We use codes previously employed by and population-synthesis templates from to fit the ugrRz data against a model that assumes a single stellar population with a uniform star-formation history and dust attenuation. The data are well fit by a model with a moderate star-formation rate (SFR = 2.7^ +4.0 _ 1.1 M_ yr ^ 1 ), moderate stellar mass ( M_* = 1.9^ +0.5 _ 1.5 10^ 10 M_ ), and low to moderate dust extinction ( A_V = 0.18^ +0.36 _ 0.18 mag). These properties are typical of star-forming galaxies (and of long-GRB hosts) at similar redshifts.

gmw_00013444.name: J140820.65 + 505240.44
gmw_00013444.overview: The galaxy J140820.65 + 505240.44 has an H I radial velocity that is approximately 250 km s^-1 lower than its SDSS-measured radial velocity [arXiv:0705.3469]. This velocity discrepancy may indicate that H I gas is currently being deposited within the galaxy, which lacks any obvious optical companions [arXiv:0705.3469].

sources.summary: The paper reports that J140820.65 + 505240.44 has an H I radial velocity about 250 km s^-1 below the SDSS velocity, with no obvious optical companion, possibly indicating gas deposition.

quotes.quote: For all but one galaxy, J140820.65 + 505240.44, the radial velocity of the HI lines estimated in this manner agreed with the radial velocities measured from the SDSS spectra within W_ 50 /2. For J140820.65 + 505240.44, the HI radial velocity is about 250 km s ^ 1 smaller than the SDSS-measured radial velocity and there are no obvious optical companions within the area of the GBT beam. This velocity discrepancy may be the result of a significant amount of HI gas that is currently being deposited within this galaxy. Follow-up radio frequency spectral imaging is required to adequately address this issue.

gmw_00013445.name: J225304.56 + 010839.95
gmw_00013445.overview: J225304.56 + 010839.95 is a galaxy with a nearby companion, NGC 7401, located close in both sky position and radial velocity [arXiv:0705.3469]. The 21-cm emission lines of these two galaxies are partially blended, requiring deblending to estimate their individual profiles and neutral hydrogen masses [arXiv:0705.3469].

sources.summary: The paper describes J225304.56 + 010839.95 as a galaxy with a nearby velocity-matched companion, blended 21-cm emission, and a deblended H I profile used for mass measurement.

quotes.quote: A second galaxy, J225304.56 + 010839.95, has a companion galaxy that is nearby both in position on the sky and in radial velocity. This companion, NGC 7401, is at a radial velocity of about 370 km s ^ 1 larger than that of J225304.56 + 010839.95 according to NED and was also detected in 21-cm emission. As can be seen from the spectrum plotted in Fig. \ specs3 , the emission lines from these two galaxies are somewhat blended. Separate Voigt profiles were fit simultaneously to effectively de-blend the two line profiles so that the line centres and velocity widths could be estimated for J225304.56 + 010839.95 and NGC 7401 separately. For the measurement of the HI mass of J225304.56 + 010839.95, the emission line window was adjusted by eye to isolate its emission from that of NGC 7401.

gmw_00013446.name: NGC 7401
gmw_00013446.overview: NGC 7401 is a galaxy that has been detected in 21-cm neutral hydrogen emission [arXiv:0705.3469]. It acts as a companion to the galaxy J225304.56 + 010839.95, with which its emission lines are partially blended [arXiv:0705.3469].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 7401 as the companion to J225304.56 + 010839.95, detected in 21-cm emission with blended lines that were deblended from the target galaxy.

quotes.quote: This companion, NGC 7401, is at a radial velocity of about 370 km s ^ 1 larger than that of J225304.56 + 010839.95 according to NED and was also detected in 21-cm emission. As can be seen from the spectrum plotted in Fig. \ specs3 , the emission lines from these two galaxies are somewhat blended. Separate Voigt profiles were fit simultaneously to effectively de-blend the two line profiles so that the line centres and velocity widths could be estimated for J225304.56 + 010839.95 and NGC 7401 separately. For the measurement of the HI mass of J225304.56 + 010839.95, the emission line window was adjusted by eye to isolate its emission from that of NGC 7401.

gmw_00013447.name: The ERO detected in the radio
aliases_local: The ERO detected in the radio
gmw_00013447.overview: The ERO detected in the radio is an emission-line galaxy at z=1.610 with a spiral-like morphology [arXiv:astro-ph/0310742]. Its radio and X-ray properties suggest the presence of an obscured Type 2 active galactic nucleus rather than a starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0310742].

sources.original_name: The ERO detected in the radio
sources.summary: The paper describes the radio-detected ERO as a z=1.610 spiral-like emission-line galaxy whose radio and X-ray properties favor an obscured Type 2 AGN over a starburst.

quotes.quote: The ERO detected in the radio is an emission line object with [ OII ] 3727 at z=1.610 and a spiral-like morphology. The flux densities at 1.4 GHz and 4.8 GHz are 106.8 8.6 Jy and 155.1 9.6 Jy respectively, implying a flat/inverted spectrum with _ radio =+0.3 ( S( ) ^ ). The object is also present in the X-ray catalog of Alexander et al. (2003) (RA=03:32:10.80, Dec= -27:46:27.6, J2000) with fluxes F(0.5-2 keV)=8.67 10^ 17 cgs and F(0.5-8 keV)=3.08 10^ 16 cgs, corresponding to luminosities of 1.4 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 and 5 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 respectively. Its X-ray luminosity, _ radio and location in typical segregation diagrams (e.g. _ radio vs. radio flux, optical flux vs. 0.5-8 keV flux; Bauer et al. 2002) are more suggestive of a Type 2 AGN than a starburst galaxy.
The lack of broad MgII 2800 emission implies obscuration of the active nucleus. From the X-ray hardness ratio we derive log(N _ H ) < 22.7 cm ^ 2 (assuming a photon index =1.7 ).

gmw_00013448.name: the absorber towards B1318 263
gmw_00013448.overview: The absorber towards B1318 263 is a high-redshift candidate damped Lyman-alpha system [arXiv:0904.3330]. It is identified as an unusually depleted system, exhibiting a super-solar manganese-to-iron ratio and a constrained zinc-to-iron ratio [arXiv:0904.3330].

sources.summary: The paper flags the absorber towards B1318 263 as an unusually depleted DLA candidate, with super-solar Mn/Fe, constrained Zn/Fe, and dust-to-metals behavior still below Galactic disk sightlines.

quotes.quote: The one intriguing exception to this general statement is the absorber towards B1318 263. Although Zn is not detected for this system, the [ Mn/Fe ] is super-solar by +0.2 dex, which is unusual amongst DLAs (e.g. Pettini et al. 2000; Dessauges-Zavadsky et al. 2002). Super-solar [ Mn/Fe ] is seen in the Galactic ISM in cool disk clouds (e.g. Savage Sembach 1996) and is usually interpreted as a signature of depletion. Only one DLA in the compilation of Dessauges-Zavadsky et al. (2002) has [ Mn/Fe ] > +0.1. The DLA towards B1318 263 may therefore be amongst the most depleted. However, even in this case the [ Zn/Fe ] is constrained to be < +1.0, a value which is high amongst DLAs (see Figure znfe ), but still low compared to Galactic disk sightlines (e.g. Savage Sembach 1996).

gmw_00013449.name: PS S
gmw_00013449.overview: PS S is a southern point-like X-ray source embedded within bright remnant emission [arXiv:1606.08940]. The source is dominant in the hard X-ray band and exhibits characteristics typical of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1606.08940].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PS S as a southern point-like X-ray source embedded in bright remnant emission, gives its J2000 position and detection significance, and says its hard-band X-ray properties are typical of an AGN.

quotes.quote: One located near the geometrical center of the X-ray structure of the SNR at = 13 ^h 21 ^m 50 2, = 63 33 53 9, J2000.0 (indicated in Fig.  xmm-chandra as PS C), another embedded within weak diffuse emission near the northeast edge of the remnant (indicated as PS N), and the third located in the southern part of the SNR (indicated as PS S), which is embedded in bright X-ray emission.

Finally, the source PS S at _ J2000.0 = 13^ h 21^ m 47 8 , _ J2000.0 = 63 35 07 8 , presents a 11 significance above local background. From the analysis of the X-ray images we found that the source PS N is dominant in the soft X-ray band and is coincident with the IR source 2MASS J13215916 6332255 cutri2003 ; the source PS S is dominant in the hard X-ray band and displays X-ray characteristics typical of an active galactic nucleus; and the source PS C is dominant in the medium X-ray band and is marginally coincident with the IR source 2MASS J13214960-6333360 cutri2003 . Unfortunately, the low-resolution radio data do not allow observing any point-like counterpart. All of them are absent in the 843 MHz radio map.

gmw_00013450.name: A
gmw_00013450.overview: The high-redshift galaxy A is located in the Hubble Deep Field-North [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050]. It is likely undergoing a starburst phase characterized by a constant star formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050].

sources.summary: The paper describes A as one of three H-redshifted HDF-N targets; its SED resembles B and C, but it is grouped with B as a likely constant-SFR starburst.

quotes.quote: The SEDs of three objects (A,B,C) located in the redshift range for H are shown in figure 5a. The brightness and the shape of these SEDs are quite similar, while the H strength of object C is far different from that of A or B.
As can be seen, objects A and B are probably undergoing a starburst phase with a constant SFR in contrast to object C, which has already finished starburst activity.

gmw_00013451.name: B
gmw_00013451.overview: Located in the Hubble Deep Field North, B is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050]. It is likely undergoing a starburst phase characterized by a constant star formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050].

sources.summary: The paper describes B as one of three H-redshifted HDF-N targets; its SED resembles A and C, but it is grouped with A as a likely constant-SFR starburst.

quotes.quote: The SEDs of three objects (A,B,C) located in the redshift range for H are shown in figure 5a. The brightness and the shape of these SEDs are quite similar, while the H strength of object C is far different from that of A or B.
As can be seen, objects A and B are probably undergoing a starburst phase with a constant SFR in contrast to object C, which has already finished starburst activity.

gmw_00013452.name: C
gmw_00013452.overview: Located in the Hubble Deep Field-North, the high-redshift galaxy C has a spectral energy distribution shape similar to neighboring objects A and B [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050]. However, its much weaker hydrogen emission indicates that galaxy C has already finished its starburst activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050].

sources.summary: The paper describes C as an HDF-N target with a similar SED shape to A and B but much weaker H, interpreting it as having finished its starburst activity.

quotes.quote: The SEDs of three objects (A,B,C) located in the redshift range for H are shown in figure 5a. The brightness and the shape of these SEDs are quite similar, while the H strength of object C is far different from that of A or B.
As can be seen, objects A and B are probably undergoing a starburst phase with a constant SFR in contrast to object C, which has already finished starburst activity.

gmw_00013453.name: D
gmw_00013453.overview: Located at a spectroscopic redshift of approximately 3.2, the galaxy D is characterized by strong [O III] emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050]. This high-redshift star-forming galaxy resides in a region similar to Lyman-break galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0001050].

sources.summary: The paper describes D as a z~3.2 HDF-N target with strong [O iii] emission, lying above local starburst galaxies and in a region similar to Lyman-break galaxies.

quotes.quote: The SEDs of other two objects (D,E) for [ O iii ] are also shown in figure 5b.

In the case of the [ O iii ] emission line (figure 6b), objects D and E are located in a higher region compared with the distribution of the local starburst galaxies, and reside in a region similar to other Lyman-break galaxies.

We also detected strong [ O iii ] emission lines from both targets with the known spectroscopic redshift of z 3.2. We infer that the high- z star-forming galaxy tends to show strong [ O iii ] emission, just like the Lyman-break galaxies reported by Pettini et. al (1998).

gmw_00013454.name: MASCOT source 8987-3701
gmw_00013454.overview: MASCOT source 8987-3701 is a galaxy with an effective radius of 3.1 arcseconds [arXiv:2111.08719]. Its carbon monoxide spectrum and measured carbon monoxide luminosity have been observed to be very similar across different surveys [arXiv:2111.08719].

sources.summary: The paper compares MASCOT source 8987-3701 with xCOLDGASS, noting that both spectra are very similar and their CO luminosities agree within uncertainties.

quotes.quote: For MASCOT source 8987-3701 (xCOLDGASS ID: 32619) the IRAM beam extents beyond 3 R _ eff of the galaxy (R _ eff = 3.1''), while the effective radius of MASCOT source 9491-6101 (xCOLDGASS ID: 31775) is R _ eff = 5.6 '' and therefore only covered by the IRAM beam out to 2 R _ eff . This difference is reflected in the respective spectra obtained by MASCOT and xCOLDGASS (Figure xcoldgass_comparison ). While the spectra for 8987-3701 are very similar and their measured CO luminosities are in good agreement (within 15 and within uncertainties), the xCOLDGASS observations of 9491-6101 seem to miss a significant amount of flux (CO luminosities are within 40 and are not within uncertainties), even though Saintonge_2017 do apply an aperture correction to their CO flux measurements.

gmw_00013455.name: MASCOT source 9491-6101
gmw_00013455.overview: MASCOT source 9491-6101 is a galaxy with an effective radius of 5.6 arcseconds [arXiv:2111.08719]. It is representative in size of the xCOLDGASS survey sample, although standard aperture corrections underrepresent its total CO flux [arXiv:2111.08719].

sources.summary: The paper says MASCOT source 9491-6101 is representative in size and that xCOLDGASS likely misses significant CO flux for this galaxy despite aperture correction.

quotes.quote: For MASCOT source 8987-3701 (xCOLDGASS ID: 32619) the IRAM beam extents beyond 3 R _ eff of the galaxy (R _ eff = 3.1''), while the effective radius of MASCOT source 9491-6101 (xCOLDGASS ID: 31775) is R _ eff = 5.6 '' and therefore only covered by the IRAM beam out to 2 R _ eff . This difference is reflected in the respective spectra obtained by MASCOT and xCOLDGASS (Figure xcoldgass_comparison ). While the spectra for 8987-3701 are very similar and their measured CO luminosities are in good agreement (within 15 and within uncertainties), the xCOLDGASS observations of 9491-6101 seem to miss a significant amount of flux (CO luminosities are within 40 and are not within uncertainties), even though Saintonge_2017 do apply an aperture correction to their CO flux measurements.
That means that 9491-6101 (xCOLDGASS ID: 31775) is not an outlier with an unusually large size in the xCOLDGASS survey but representative of the entire xCOLDGASS sample.

In the case of an underestimation of the CO(1-0) luminosity by 60 (as is the case for source 9491-6101 in comparison with the xCOLDGASS survey, see Section 3.7), ( f_ mol ) would be underestimated by 0.3.

gmw_00013456.name: C4
gmw_00013456.overview: C4 is a distant quasar that has been utilized as a target for high-resolution continuum observations [arXiv:1612.02554]. It serves as a key reference source for testing advanced very long baseline interferometry astrometry and calibration techniques [arXiv:1612.02554].

sources.summary: The paper uses quasar C4 as a central VLBI astrometry target and in-beam reference, comparing MultiView and phase-referencing calibration through C4 images and measured astrometric offsets.

quotes.quote: The two sources in the centre of the distribution, the OH maser source and the quasar C4, were observed simultaneously because they lie within the primary beam of the VLBA antennas. They are the targets of the analyses presented in this paper, allowing the MultiView calibration to be tested for both a maser line and quasar continuum observations simultaneously. The sessions were long enough to ensure sufficient (u,v) coverage and sensitivity, spending 40 of the time on the OH--C4 pair with alternating 30--90 s scans on the C1, C2 and C3 calibrators.

We use the alternating quasar-only observations for the comparison between PR with a range of angular separations and MV techniques, using C4 as the target; using the OH maser source as target, we compare in-beam PR with C4 as reference, 0.4 ^o away, to MV using three calibrators 2^o , 4^o , and 6^o away.

Fig. fig:C4.maps shows the MVed image for the C4 quasar obtained using the three calibrators together and the three PRed images using a single calibrator 2^o, 4^o and 6^o away, for the two epochs. Astrometry is performed directly in the images of quasar C4 shown in Fig. fig:C4.maps by measuring the offset of the peak of brightness from the center of the map. This offset corresponds to the difference between our measurements and the catalog positions used for correlation. Fig. fig:astro a shows the astrometric measurements, or offsets, of the target quasar C4 using PR (analysis id. PR _ 2^o , PR _ 4^o , PR _ 6^o ) and MV (analysis id.: MV _ QSO ) at the two epochs of observations.

gmw_00013457.name: SDSS J120819.29+035559.4
gmw_00013457.overview: SDSS J120819.29+035559.4 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1910.09411]. The object hosts two broad-absorption line systems, which exhibit absorption-line variations driven by changes in the ionization continuum and show evidence of radiatively driven acceleration [arXiv:1910.09411].

sources.summary: The paper reports two BAL systems in SDSS J120819.29+035559.4, attributes absorption-line variation to ionization-continuum changes, finds line-locking, and interprets a kinematic shift as radiatively driven acceleration.

quotes.quote: We report the kinematic shift of the C IV broad absorption line (BAL) in quasar SDSS J120819.29+035559.4 (hereafter J1208+0355). This quasar shows two BAL systems, including a blue component of system A at -23500 km s^ 1 that shows a kinematic shift of --1166 65 km s^ 1 , and a red component of system B at -7000 km s^ 1 that can be decomposed into several narrow absorption lines (NALs).

First, we confirm that the most likely cause for the equivalent width variations of the absorption lines (at least for system B) in J1208+0355 is the ionization change scenario as a response to the changes in the ionization continuum according to the following observational factors: (1) coordinated multiple absorption lines strengthening; (2) the continuum flux shows an obvious weakening. Second, we find line-locking phenomena from the blended NALs within system B, indicating that these outflow clouds are driven by a radiative force caused by resonance lines. The above two research aspects convincingly reveal that the BAL outflows of J1208+0355 are affected by the background radiation energy. Therefore, we infer that the kinematic shift shown in system A may be produced by actual line-of-sight acceleration of the outflow clouds, which is driven by radiation pressure from the background light source.

gmw_00013458.name: absorption system at z = 1.1912 in LBQS 1435-0134
aliases_local: absorption system at z = 1.1912 in LBQS 1435-0134
gmw_00013458.overview: The absorption system at z = 1.1912 in LBQS 1435-0134 is a high-ionization state system detected in the sightline toward a quasar [arXiv:1608.07653]. It contains Ne VIII and O VI ions, which are collisionally ionized and trace a hot galactic halo [arXiv:1608.07653].

sources.original_name: absorption system at z = 1.1912 in LBQS 1435-0134
sources.summary: The paper identifies this absorber as a high-ionization system toward LBQS 1435-0134 and argues that collisional ionization and its column density favor a volume-filled hot galaxy halo.

quotes.quote: Using high S/N ratio HST /COS spectra, we discovered a high ionization state system at z=1.1912 in the sightline toward LBQS 1435-0134 , and two-components absorption lines are matched for , , , , , , , , , , and . , detected for the first time ( 5.8 ), is a particularly direct tracer of hot galactic halos, as its peak ion fraction occurs near 10^ 6.1   K , about the temperature of a virialized hot galaxy halo of mass 0.5 M^* . With and , a photoionization model cannot reproduce the observed column densities with path lengths of galaxy halos.

We have examined an absorption system at z = 1.1912 in the sightline toward LBQS 1435-0134 with high ionization species (i.e. and ) and several intermediate or low ionization species including and . We consider whether the observed absorption system is likely produced by the extended hot halo of a galaxy and if the gas, especially the and ions, is collisionally ionized or photoionized.

gmw_00013459.name: PMN J1844-0306
gmw_00013459.overview: PMN J1844-0306 is a candidate radio galaxy that exhibits a bright central S-shaped radio emission with a dual-jet morphology [arXiv:1905.03741]. Although it is surrounded by a faint radio halo, this diffuse emission is likely a foreground Galactic source rather than part of the galaxy itself [arXiv:1905.03741].

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J1844-0306 as a candidate radio galaxy with dual-jet/S-shaped radio emission, while arguing the surrounding halo is probably a foreground Galactic source rather than the galaxy halo.

quotes.quote: Radio continuum observations in the direction of G29.37+0.1 first revealed an elongated radio source with a dual jet morphology, which was classified as the candidate radio galaxy PMN J1844-0306 helfand89 . After the release of the Multi-Array Galactic Plane Imaging Survey (MAGPIS, helfand06 ), diffuse radio emission was detected around PMN J1844-0306, which was considered to come from a new SNR candidate. Then, the complex morphology of G29.37+0.1 in the radio band could be caused by the superposition of two different sources, a Galactic SNR and a radio galaxy.

They concluded that the radio properties of the central elongated emission (referred to as an S-shaped feature) are similar to the emission that is observed toward other radio galaxies, while the surrounding diffuse emission (the halo) could be either the halo of the radio galaxy or a Galactic source superimposed in the line of sight. C17 judged the latter scenario to be the most probable, supported by the detection of a complex of molecular clouds at 5-6 kpc that matches the diffuse radio emission.

The radio source G29.37+0.1 is formed by two sources: a bright central S-shaped feature that is surrounded by a faint halo. The former is likely a radio galaxy, while the latter could be either diffuse emission related to the radio galaxy or a Galactic source. Our distance analysis (Sect. dist ) shows that the halo is probably located at 6.5 kpc, that is, it is a Galactic source in the foreground with respect to the radio galaxy.

gmw_00013460.name: J0258+2030
gmw_00013460.overview: J0258+2030 is an AzTEC source located behind the MBM12 region [arXiv:1112.6241]. It has a radio counterpart observed at 1.4 GHz and is modeled to have a high star formation rate of 3,600 solar masses per year [arXiv:1112.6241].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0258+2030 as an AzTEC source behind MBM12 with a VLA radio counterpart, modeled through galaxy SEDs to infer a high star formation rate.

quotes.quote: From the present AzTEC observations of the MBM12 region, we find that other detected sources are likely to be located behind MBM12. Four AzTEC objects, defined as J0258+2030 (A 3_1 ), J0259+1925 (A 4_1 ), J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ), and J0257+1847 (A 9_2 ), were previously studied in the 1.4 GHz NRAO VLA survey for the sky of 40^ in J2000 con98 . J0259+1925 (A 4_1 ) is known as quasi-stellar object (QSO) at a redshift of z = 0.54. The flux density of this object at 1.4 GHz was 169.9 mJy. Three AzTEC sources, J0258+2030 (A 3_1 ), J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ), and J0257+1847 (A 9_2 ) have radio counterparts observed by con98 with the VLA at 1.4 GHz.

The GRASIL model silva98 computes the photometric evolution of galaxies based on the SEDs of the AzTEC sources, including the effects of a dusty interstellar medium. The model includes the escape time of young stars from molecular clouds, the fraction of residual gas in molecular clouds, the radius of molecular clouds, etc. The star formation rate (SFR) could be also derived by fitting the SEDs to the model kim10 . The results suggest that J0258+2030 (A 3_1 ), J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ), and J0257+1847 (A 9_2 ) can have SFRs of 3.6 10 ^3 M_ yr ^ 1 , 3.3 10 ^4 M_ yr ^ 1 , and 2.9 10 ^2 M_ yr ^ 1 , respectively.

gmw_00013461.name: J0259+1925
gmw_00013461.overview: J0259+1925 is a quasi-stellar object located at a redshift of z = 0.54 [arXiv:1112.6241]. The source has been detected at radio, millimeter, and X-ray wavelengths, exhibiting a flux density of 169.9 mJy at 1.4 GHz [arXiv:1112.6241].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0259+1925 as a z = 0.54 QSO detected by AzTEC, gives radio and X-ray measurements, and compares its radio and millimeter positions.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: the position of J0259+1925 was measured as =02^ h 59^ m 29^ s .5 , =+19^ 25^ 42^ at millimeter wavelengths

quotes.quote: J0259+1925 (A 4_1 ) is known as quasi-stellar object (QSO) at a redshift of z = 0.54. The flux density of this object at 1.4 GHz was 169.9 mJy. Three AzTEC sources, J0258+2030 (A 3_1 ), J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ), and J0257+1847 (A 9_2 ) have radio counterparts observed by con98 with the VLA at 1.4 GHz. The counterpart corresponding to J0259+1925 (A 4_1 ) was also classified as J025929+1925.7 (1REX) in the radio-emitting X-ray sources (REXs) survey by cac00 . Its X-ray flux was reported as 2.34 10^ 13 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 .

The NRAO VLA Sky Survey (NVSS) position of this source suggests that this object should be =02^ h 59^ m 29^ s .65 , =+19^ 25^ 44^ .9 (J2000) at radio wavelengths. From the present AzTEC observations, the position of J0259+1925 was measured as =02^ h 59^ m 29^ s .5 , =+19^ 25^ 42^ at millimeter wavelengths. So the difference between the radio and millimeter emission is =0^ s .15 and =2^ .9 . The flux at , measured with AzTEC was also measured as 234.3 mJy beam ^ 1 .

gmw_00013462.name: J0253+1805
gmw_00013462.overview: J0253+1805 is a radio source that corresponds to a millimeter-wavelength detection [arXiv:1112.6241]. Spectral energy distribution modeling indicates that it is a dusty galaxy with a high star formation rate of approximately 33,000 solar masses per year [arXiv:1112.6241].

sources.summary: The paper links J0253+1805 to a VLA radio source, reports consistent coordinates and millimeter flux, and models its SED as a dusty galaxy with high star formation.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The coordinate of J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ) was reported as =02^ h 53^ m 34^ s .88 and =+18^ 05^ 42^ .53 (J2000)

quotes.quote: The AzTEC source A 9_1 corresponds to J0253+1805 (J2000) in the NRAO VLA sky survey at 1.4 GHz con98 . The coordinate of J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ) was reported as =02^ h 53^ m 34^ s .88 and =+18^ 05^ 42^ .53 (J2000) using the VLA observations. This position agrees with the identification by the Automated Plate Measurement (APM) Facility at Cambridge of the radio sources from the Jodrell Bank VLA Astrometric Survey (JVAS) sne02 . We also compared the fluxes at 6 mm (Green Bank), NVSS 1.4 GHz (NRAO VLA Sky Survey), Green Bank 1.4 GHz, and VLA 1.4 GHz. Their results were respectively, 248, 304, 426, and 154 mJy. The millimeter-wavelength flux from this study was 298.5 mJy beam ^ 1 at 1.1 mm.

The GRASIL model silva98 computes the photometric evolution of galaxies based on the SEDs of the AzTEC sources, including the effects of a dusty interstellar medium. The model includes the escape time of young stars from molecular clouds, the fraction of residual gas in molecular clouds, the radius of molecular clouds, etc. The star formation rate (SFR) could be also derived by fitting the SEDs to the model kim10 . The results suggest that J0258+2030 (A 3_1 ), J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ), and J0257+1847 (A 9_2 ) can have SFRs of 3.6 10 ^3 M_ yr ^ 1 , 3.3 10 ^4 M_ yr ^ 1 , and 2.9 10 ^2 M_ yr ^ 1 , respectively.

gmw_00013463.name: J0257+1847
gmw_00013463.overview: J0257+1847 is an AzTEC source detected at 1.1 mm with a counterpart in the NRAO VLA sky survey [arXiv:1112.6241]. It is also identified as a Very Long Baseline Array calibrator source and has an estimated star formation rate of 290 solar masses per year [arXiv:1112.6241].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0257+1847 as an AzTEC source behind MBM12 with radio and VLBA calibrator counterparts, measured fluxes, coordinates, and a galaxy-SED-based star formation rate.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The coordinates of the AzTEC source agrees to within =02^ h 57^ m 45^ s .6 , =+18^ 47^ 06^ (J2000)

quotes.quote: The AzTEC source detected as J0257+1847 (A 9_2 ) has its counterpart in the radio source in the NRAO VLA sky survey by con98 .
