This source was also included in the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) Calibrator Survey (VCS1) at 2.3 and 8.4 GHz bea02 . sne02 also obtained the flux densities of this object at 6 mm (Green Bank), 1.4 GHz (NVSS), 1.4 GHz (Green Bank), and 1.4 GHz (VLA), which were 209, 103, 180, and 241 mJy, respectively. Synchrotron properties and their spectral indices of these objects will be discussed in detail in a future paper (S. Kim et al., in preparation).

The coordinates of the AzTEC source agrees to within =02^ h 57^ m 45^ s .6 , =+18^ 47^ 06^ (J2000). The flux at 1.1 mm is 53.6 mJy beam ^ 1 . The fluxes at other wavelengths were compared in the SEDs presented in Figure 6.
The results suggest that J0258+2030 (A 3_1 ), J0253+1805 (A 9_1 ), and J0257+1847 (A 9_2 ) can have SFRs of 3.6 10 ^3 M_ yr ^ 1 , 3.3 10 ^4 M_ yr ^ 1 , and 2.9 10 ^2 M_ yr ^ 1 , respectively.

gmw_00013464.name: PC 1035+4747
gmw_00013464.overview: PC 1035+4747 is a quasar classified as a radio-loud source [arXiv:astro-ph/0510257]. It is characterized by a distinct X-ray to optical spectral-index behavior [arXiv:astro-ph/0510257].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PC 1035+4747 as the only radio-detected QSO in the sample, classifies it as radio loud, and reports its distinct X-ray/optical spectral-index behavior.

quotes.quote: Only one QSO (PC 1035+4747) was detected in the radio band and for others, 3 upper limits of R_ L are shown. The only radio-detected QSO, PC 1035+4747, has R_ L =2.3 ; this object falls well into the RLQ regime.
Removing the one RLQ (PC 1035+4747) from the analysis did not change the fitted parameters and errors to the smallest digits displayed in Table tab:spec .

The RLQ PC 1035+4747 has 0.6 0.8 and using =0.6 for K-correction gives _ ox =-2.06 . Other than this one, the main source of errors in _ ox is the X-ray flux. Even for the source with the smallest source count (PC 1035+4747) the 1 error on _ ox is 0.05 .

gmw_00013465.name: 2MASX J04355560-1405542
gmw_00013465.overview: 2MASX J04355560-1405542 is a galaxy that is clearly visible in Herschel 100 to 350 micrometer maps [arXiv:1402.2483]. It is also identified with a nearby WISE catalog counterpart, WISE J043555.67-140554.1 [arXiv:1402.2483].

sources.summary: The paper notes that object E is visible in Herschel maps, identifies it as the galaxy 2MASX J04355560-1405542, and gives a nearby WISE counterpart.

quotes.quote: The object marked E in Fig.  fig:areas_I_maps (bottom-left frame) can be clearly seen in the Herschel 100--350 m maps. This is the object IRAS 04336-1412 (or L 1642-3) that was mentioned, but not studied in more detail, in  Sandell1987 and  Lehtinen2004 . NED http://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ identifies this object as a galaxy 2MASX J04355560-1405542. There is a suitable counterpart in the WISE catalogue, WISE J043555.67-140554.1, within 2 .

gmw_00013466.name: XID 485
gmw_00013466.overview: XID 485 is a likely high-redshift source with a probable redshift greater than 3 [arXiv:1209.4193]. It is significantly detected in the near-infrared by the Infrared Array Camera but lacks an optical counterpart in the Great Observatories Origins Deep Survey [arXiv:1209.4193].

sources.summary: The paper treats XID 485 as a likely high-redshift source with competing photometric redshifts, no GOODS optical counterpart, near-infrared detection, and the largest inferred obscuring column among the three sources.

quotes.quote: We note the presence of 3 sources at z>5 , for which we investigated in more detail the redshift information. Two of them (XID 139 and 197) have only one photometric redshift z=5.729 and 6.071, respectively, from which also provides a secondary solution of z=4.385 for XID 197 Luo10 , while XID 485 has two different photometric redshifts ( z=7.62 from Luo10 , with secondary solution z=3.309 , and z=4.42 from Santini09 ). These objects have no optical counterparts in the Great Observatories Origins Deep Survey (GOODS; XID 139 and 485) or Galaxy Evolution from Morphologies and SEDs (GEMS; XID 197, which is not in the GOODS field) observations, while they are significantly detected by the Infrared Array Camera (IRAC) in the near-IR. Thus, a high-redshift ( z>3 ) solution appears probable.

If they were placed at z=3.7 (the median redshift of the sample), the best-fitting N_H would be 50^ +23 _ 18 10^ 22 , 42^ +20 _ 24 10^ 22 and 118^ +41 _ 28 10^ 22 cm^ 2 for XID 139, 197 and 485, respectively (i.e., they would still be in the highly obscured class).

gmw_00013467.name: GRB host at z_ GRB = 3.96855
gmw_00013467.overview: The GRB host galaxy at z = 3.96855 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0508270] with an exceptionally high neutral hydrogen column density [arXiv:astro-ph/0508270]. Its interstellar medium exhibits very low metallicity, little to no dust depletion, and unusually dense absorbing gas [arXiv:astro-ph/0508270].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 050730 host at z=3.96855, finding an exceptionally high neutral hydrogen column, narrow metal-line components, low dust and metallicity, and unusually dense absorbing gas.

quotes.quote: We identify the GRB host at z_ GRB = 3.96855 based on the hydrogen Lyman absorption series, narrow absorption lines due to heavy ions such as O I, C II, Si II, S II, Ni II, Fe II, C IV, Si IV, and N V, and fine structure transitions such as O I ^* , O I ^ ** , Si II ^* , C II ^* , and Fe II ^* . Together these transitions allow us to study the the properties of the interstellar medium (ISM) in the GRB host. The principal results are as follows. (1) We estimate a neutral hydrogen column density of N( )=22.15 0.05 in the host. (2) The associated metal lines exhibit multiple components over a velocity range of 80 , with >90 of the neutral gas confined in 20 .

(3) Comparisons between different ionic transitions show that the host has little/no dust depletion and has 1/100 solar metallicity. (4) The absorbing gas has much higher density than that of intervening damped \ absorption (DLA) systems.

gmw_00013468.name: SDSS J120312.63-001118.8
gmw_00013468.overview: SDSS J120312.63-001118.8 is a high-redshift quasar [arXiv:1206.3611]. Spectroscopic observations of its Lyman-alpha and silicon IV emission lines indicate that the object has a redshift of approximately 4.601 [arXiv:1206.3611].

sources.summary: The paper reports MMT Red Channel follow-up spectroscopy of the high-redshift quasar SDSS J120312.63-001118.8, with two exposures and a processed spectrum yielding a redshift consistent with YFOSC.

quotes.quote: Thanks to the Chinese Telescope Access Program http://info.bao.ac.cn/tap/ , SDSS J120312.63-001118.8 was also observed with the Red Channel Spectrograph on the MMT 6.5m telescope Observation reported here was obtained at the MMT Observatory, a joint facility of the Univeristy of Arizona and the Smithsonian Institution. at Mt. Hopkins, Arizona, USA on Feb. 29, 2012, with a wavelength coverage of 5100-8600 and a spectral resolution of 1.6 . It was observed twice, with the exposures of 10 minutes and 15 minutes respectively. The spectrum was processed using the IRAF Echellette package and is shown in Fig. 2. The average redshift estimated from Ly and SiIV (1400 ) emission lines is 4.601 0.008, consistent with the result (z=4.592 0.048) obtained from the YFOSC observation.

gmw_00013469.name: FBQS J152350.4+391404
gmw_00013469.overview: FBQS J152350.4+391404 is a broad-absorption line quasar that exhibits visible iron absorption troughs across multiple epochs [arXiv:1501.00091]. The object displays strong ultraviolet iron variability [arXiv:1501.00091], Balmer absorption features [arXiv:1501.00091], and an outflow located approximately 34 parsecs from the central source [arXiv:1501.00091].

sources.summary: The paper finds FBQS J152350.4+391404 has visible Fe troughs across epochs, strong ultraviolet Fe variability, Balmer absorption evidence, and a CLOUDY outflow model at about 34 pc.

quotes.quote: The top panels of Figure 2 show that the three spectra of FBQS J152350.4+391404 are similar to the intermediate epochs of FBQS J140806.2+305448, especially the KECK/ESI spectrum (MJD 53377; Hall et al. 2011). Troughs of Fe 2 multiplets are clearly visible in all three epochs. The difference spectra reveal strong variability in the ultraviolet Fe 2 multiplets ( Fe 2 UV1 and UV2+3) and very slight variations in the optical Fe 2 multiplets ( Fe 2 Opt8 and Opt6+7). Meanwhile, the red-band spectrographs of the SDSS and BOSS captured a highly blueshifted velocity and an extremely broad absorption trough of H at approximately 4700 in the SDSS DR7 and DR10 spectra. Furthermore, a near-infrared spectrum that is quasi-simultaneous with the SDSS DR10 spectrum, which was acquired by the TripleSpec spectrograph of the Hale 200-inch telescope at Palomar Observatory, suggests the existence of H BALs.

Similarly, a model with U = 10^ 2 , n _e = 10^7  cm^ 3 , N _H = 10^ 23  cm^ 2 and FWHM = 3000 is the optimal photoionization model for FBQS J152350.4+391404, and there are also obvious Cr 2 absorption lines present at approximately 2850 in the difference spectra. Using the ionization parameter definitions and the inferred values of the ionization photon emission rate, ionization parameter and density, one can determine the distance of the outflow winds from the central ionization source, which is found to be R _ BAL 0.16 pc for FBQS J140806.2+305448 and 34 pc for FBQS J152350.4+391404.

gmw_00013470.name: FBQS J140800.4+345125
gmw_00013470.overview: FBQS J140800.4+345125 is an iron low-ionization broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1501.00091]. The object exhibits an invariant broad absorption line system alongside separate, variable iron absorption troughs that are completely overlapping [arXiv:1501.00091].

sources.summary: The paper describes FBQS J140800.4+345125 as an OFeLoBAL quasar with overlapped Fe absorption, invariant Mg/Al/Fe troughs, and separate broad-band troughs interpreted as variable Fe absorption.

quotes.quote: As shown in Figure 3, the BALs of Fe 2 multiplets are ambiguous or severely overlapped in FBQS J105528.8+312411 and J140800.4+345125. The spectral variability in these two cases is deepening overall below Mg 2 and blueward. It is difficult to resolve the absorption multiplets based on their single observations, but it is possible to identify strong variations of the iron multiplets in their difference spectra.

For FBQS J140800.4+345125, we identify the absorption troughs of Mg 2 , Al 3 and Fe 2 UV1 (perhaps higher-velocity Mg 2 ) in the FBQS spectrum and mark them by blue diagonal line regions. However, the residual fluxes of the three troughs are constant in all epochs. The normalized fluxes of these troughs in the difference spectra are 1. This broad absorption line system is invariant. In addition, there are other broad-band absorption troughs in the difference spectra blueward of the invariant absorption troughs noted above, which are surmised to represent the variable absorption of Fe 2 UV62, UV1 and UV2+3 with the same blueshifted velocity as the invariant absorption system; the iron absorption should be completely overlapping.

gmw_00013471.name: eHAQ0104+1506
gmw_00013471.overview: eHAQ0104+1506 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=1.42 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits associated absorption and is affected by dust extinction, with optical data favoring a higher extinction value than near-infrared data [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0104+1506 as a quasar and discusses its associated absorption, photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0104+1506 ( z=1.42 ) Insecure dust fitting due to flux calibration issues of the INT spectrum. The \ is partly absorbed by associated absorption. The \ emission around the \ emission line is possibly very strong causing the \ line to appear weaker. The optical data favor a solution with high dust extinction ( A(V) 0.6 ) whereas the near-infrared data indicate somewhat lower extinction ( A(V) 0.3 ). The optical and near-infrared data might be reconciled by a steeper extinction curve or a steeper intrinsic power-law slope, or a combination of both.

gmw_00013472.name: eHAQ0109+0435
gmw_00013472.overview: eHAQ0109+0435 is a reddened broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of approximately 3.47 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits very weak emission lines, a complex absorption line structure, and a possible excess of iron emission [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0109+0435 as a BAL quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0109+0435 ( z=3.47 ) This is a reddened BAL quasar with very weak emission lines and a very complex absorption line structure. The redshift is determined from absorption lines and from the small emission line around _ obs 4800 , which is interpreted as Ly . The absolute flux calibration is insecure due to the scaling to the band, which is highly affected by the BAL features. The reddening is therefore only estimated form the photometry. There is possibly an excess of Fe 2 emission causing the observed excess in the z and Y bands compared to the best-fit template.

gmw_00013473.name: eHAQ0142+0257
gmw_00013473.overview: eHAQ0142+0257 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=2.30 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits narrow associated absorption lines and has been detected in radio surveys at 1.4 GHz [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0142+0257 as a reddened quasar and discusses its associated absorption, radio information.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0142+0257 ( z=2.30 ) This is a reddened quasar with narrow associated absorption especially in C 4 , Si 4 , and N 5 . The strong absorption around \ and ] is possibly caused by absorption of the iron `pseudo-continuum'. The object is detected in both FIRST and the NRAO VLA Sky Survey NVSS Condon1998 at 1.4 GHz.

gmw_00013474.name: eHAQ0147+0411
gmw_00013474.overview: eHAQ0147+0411 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of approximately z=1.77 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object has been detected in both the near-ultraviolet and far-ultraviolet bands by NASA's Galaxy Evolution Explorer [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0147+0411 as a reddened quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0147+0411 ( z=1.77 ) This is a reddened quasar. Due to possible issues with the flux calibration of the INT spectrum, we have only estimated reddening from the photometry. The object has a detection by NASA's Galaxy Evolution Explorer (GALEX) in the two bands (near-UV and far-UV): NUV = 20.59 0.10 AB mag and FUV = 20.73 0.18 AB mag.

gmw_00013475.name: eHAQ0147+1549
gmw_00013475.overview: eHAQ0147+1549 is a radio-loud BL Lacertae object that lacks identifiable spectral features [arXiv:1608.08404]. The source exhibits photometric variability between different observation epochs and is detected in the radio band at 1.4 GHz [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper classifies eHAQ0147+1549 as a likely BL Lac object, citing absent spectral features, radio loudness, variability, and appearance in a candidate gamma-ray blazar catalog.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0147+1549 (BL Lacertae Object) There are no spectral features that can hint at a redshift. The object is detected in radio by NVSS at 1.4 GHz, F_ 1.4 GHz = 17 mJy. Given the optical flux, i=18.3 mag, this object is classified as radio loud following Ivezic2002 . The object exhibits variability between the different epochs of observation; The photometry from SDSS and UKIDSS seems to be offset with respect to one another, and the spectrum does not seem to match the SDSS photometry in the u and g bands. These pieces of evidence hint at this object being a BL Lac object.

gmw_00013476.name: eHAQ0157+1321
gmw_00013476.overview: The object eHAQ0157+1321 is classified as a reddened quasar [arXiv:1608.08404]. Photometric template fitting indicates a redshift of approximately 1.75, which is consistent with its observed spectrum [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0157+1321 as a reddened quasar and discusses its photometric constraints.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0157+1321 ( z=1.73 ) This is a reddened quasar. The spectral shape might be influenced by flux calibration issues for the INT spectra. This object also appears in the KX-catalog of Maddox2012 ; however, these authors assign a redshift of z=0.8524 . This redshift is inconsistent with the data presented here, since we see no evidence of the H and O 3 emission lines, which should be observable at the red end of our spectrum at this redshift. Moreover, our template fitting using only the photometry (excluding the W_3 and W_4 bands) yields z_ phot =1.75 0.10 .

gmw_00013477.name: eHAQ0300+0440
gmw_00013477.overview: eHAQ0300+0440 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of approximately z=1.88 [arXiv:1608.08404]. Its spectrum exhibits narrow associated absorption and requires a steep extinction curve to model its dust properties [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0300+0440 as a reddened quasar and discusses its associated absorption, photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0300+0440 ( z=1.88 ) This is a reddened quasar with narrow associated absorption. The spectrum and photometry require a steeper extinction curve than the SMC-like curve from Gordon2003 . We therefore use the extinction curve derived for similar quasars from Zafar2015 to obtain a better fit.

gmw_00013478.name: eHAQ0828+0313
gmw_00013478.overview: eHAQ0828+0313 is a weak-line quasar located at a redshift of approximately z=1.99 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits weak broad absorption line features and shows no signs of dust reddening in its near-infrared photometry [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0828+0313 as a weak-line quasar and discusses its photometric-redshift comparison, photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0828+0313 ( z=1.99 ) This is a weak line quasar with weak BAL features blueward of . We are not able to match the spectrum and photometry simultaneously. However, we note that the template is not suitable for weak line quasars, since it was derived for `regular' quasars. The NIR photometry is well matched by quasar template with no dust reddening applied. The quasar appears in the photometric catalogs of Richards2004, Richards2009 who estimate photometric redshifts of z_ phot =0.275 and z_ phot =0.225 , respectively.

gmw_00013479.name: eHAQ0839+0556
gmw_00013479.overview: eHAQ0839+0556 is a weak-line quasar located at a redshift of z=1.49 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object lacks strong emission lines, and its spectral energy distribution is well-fitted by a continuum template with an intrinsically steep slope [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0839+0556 as a weak-line quasar and discusses its photometric-redshift comparison.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0839+0556 ( z=1.49 ) There are no strong emission lines for this object, however, we observe small absorption features that match up with , ] , and . This redshift is furthermore consistent with the photometric redshift of z_ phot =1.5 0.1 . The overall SED is well-fitted by a continuum template with an intrinsically steep slope with no emission lines and reddened by a steep extinction curve.

gmw_00013480.name: eHAQ0856+0643
gmw_00013480.overview: eHAQ0856+0643 is a peculiar quasar located at a redshift of z=2.49 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits narrow associated absorption, with an optical spectrum that is well described by dust reddening [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0856+0643 as a quasar and discusses its associated absorption, photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0856+0643 ( z=2.49 ) This is a `peculiar' quasar with narrow associated absorption. The optical spectrum is well described by dust reddening A(V) =0.44 , however, the infrared photometry requires no reddening. The steeper extinction curve of Zafar2015 does not provide a satisfactory solution either.

gmw_00013481.name: eHAQ0913+0910
gmw_00013481.overview: eHAQ0913+0910 is a FeLoBAL quasar located at a redshift of z=1.95 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits no apparent reddening due to dust, though a large number of blended broad absorption line features are present in its observed optical spectrum [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0913+0910 as a FeLoBAL quasar and discusses its photometric-redshift comparison, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0913+0910 ( z=1.95 ) This is a FeLoBAL quasar with no apparent reddening due to dust. The large number of blended BAL features in the observed optical spectrum makes it impossible to identify the quasar continuum. This leads to a strong evident mismatch between the optical data and the template. The object appears in the catalog of photometric quasar candidates from Richards2009 who report a photometric redshift of z_ phot =0.225 .

gmw_00013482.name: eHAQ0927 0233
gmw_00013482.overview: eHAQ0927 0233 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=1.24 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It possibly exhibits broad absorption line features and shows a small deficit in its Y and J band emission [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0927 0233 as a reddened quasar and discusses its photometric-redshift comparison.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0927 0233 ( z=1.24 ) This is a reddened quasar possibly with BAL features blueward of ] . There is a small deficit in the Y and J bands relative to the best-fit template. This is possibly due to variations in the emission around H . The object appears in the catalog of photometric quasar candidates from Richards2009 who report a photometric redshift of z_ phot =3.875 .

gmw_00013483.name: eHAQ0943+1300
gmw_00013483.overview: The object eHAQ0943+1300 is a highly absorbed broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of approximately 2.68 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits mild reddening, very weak emission lines, and absorption with various velocity profiles [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ0943+1300 as a BAL quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ0943+1300 ( z=2.68 ) This is a highly absorbed BAL quasar with mild reddening and very weak emission lines. The absorption exhibits various velocity profiles which makes an exact redshift determination difficult. The estimate of reddening is insecure and only based on the near-infrared photometry.

gmw_00013484.name: eHAQ1025+1324
gmw_00013484.overview: eHAQ1025+1324 is a peculiar broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=2.57 [arXiv:1608.08404]. Its optical spectrum is characterized by a high amount of reddening, whereas its near-infrared photometry indicates a lower level of reddening [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1025+1324 as a BAL quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1025+1324 ( z=2.57 ) This is a `peculiar' BAL quasar. The spectrum is well described by a high amount of reddening ( A(V) = 0.78 ), but the near-infrared photometry requires less reddening ( A(V) = 0.15 ). We note that the template may not provide a good description for the optical spectrum, due to the large amount of absorption.

gmw_00013485.name: eHAQ1030+1040
gmw_00013485.overview: eHAQ1030+1040 is a iron low-ionization broad-absorption line (FeLoBAL) quasar [arXiv:1608.08404]. While photometric modeling initially suggested a redshift of approximately 4.2, spectral analysis reveals the quasar is actually located at a lower redshift of 1.58 [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1030+1040 as a FeLoBAL quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1030+1040 ( z=1.58 ) From photometric modelling (using bands from g to W_2 ), we infer a redshift of z_ phot =4.2 0.1 , consistent with the estimate from Richards2009 : z_ phot =4.215 . However, the spectrum reveals that this object is indeed a FeLoBAL quasar at lower redshift. The near-infrared photometry is consistent with no reddening whereas the optical spectrum seems to require some reddening, however, the estimated A(V) is highly uncertain due to the strong absorption of the continuum.

gmw_00013486.name: eHAQ1136+0027
gmw_00013486.overview: eHAQ1136+0027 is a highly reddened quasar located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=1.12 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits associated absorption and displays near-infrared photometry that is offset relative to its optical data, which may be caused by intrinsic variability [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1136+0027 as a highly reddened quasar and discusses its associated absorption, photometric-redshift comparison, photometric constraints.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1136+0027 ( z=1.12 ) This is a highly reddened quasar with associated absorption. The near-infrared photometry is offset relative to the optical data. This is possibly caused by intrinsic variability in the quasar. The object appears in the photometric catalogs of Richards2004 and Richards2009 who assign photometric redshifts of z_ phot =0.33 and z_ phot =3.89 , respectively. The spectroscopic redshift if measured accurately from [ O 2 ] 3727 .

gmw_00013487.name: eHAQ1210+1429
gmw_00013487.overview: eHAQ1210+1429 is a highly reddened weak-line quasar at a redshift of z=1.48 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits very strong associated absorption at its redshift, with optical and near-infrared photometry indicating a high but variable amount of dust reddening [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1210+1429 as a weak-line quasar and discusses its associated absorption, photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1210+1429 ( z=1.48 ) This is a highly reddened quasar with weak emission lines and very strong associated absorption at the quasar redshift z_ abs =1.486 . The optical spectrum is well fitted by a high amount of dust ( A(V) =1.1 ), however, the near-infrared photometry requires less reddening ( A(V) =0.36 ; shown as the blue template in the figure). This indicates that the intrinsic power-law slope of the quasar is steeper than average or that the extinction curve is steeper than SMC.

gmw_00013488.name: eHAQ1237+1233
gmw_00013488.overview: eHAQ1237+1233 is a reddened broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=2.31 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The quasar exhibits weak emission lines and displays strong absorption in its optical spectrum [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1237+1233 as a BAL quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1237+1233 ( z=2.31 ) This is a reddened BAL quasar with weak emission lines. The optical spectrum is better fitted by a rather high A(V) whereas a fit to the near-infrared photometry results in a significantly lower amount of reddening: A(V) =0.12 . We caution the reader that the template does not provide a reasonable description of the optical data, due to the strong absorption. We therefore only provide the A(V) derived from the near-infrared photometry.

gmw_00013489.name: eHAQ1244+0841
gmw_00013489.overview: eHAQ1244+0841 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=1.87 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits weak broad absorption line features in its spectrum [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1244+0841 as a reddened quasar and discusses its photometric-redshift comparison.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1244+0841 ( z=1.87 ) This is a reddened quasar with weak BAL features. We note that the optical spectrum seems to indicate a high A(V) of 0.45 mag, but this is highly uncertain due to the possible absorption around \ and ] . The object appears in the photometric catalog of Richards2009 who infer a photometric redshift of z_ phot =2.72 .

gmw_00013490.name: eHAQ1312+1431
gmw_00013490.overview: eHAQ1312+1431 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=1.64 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits weak emission lines alongside narrow absorption features [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1312+1431 as a reddened quasar and discusses its photometric-redshift comparison.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1312+1431 ( z=1.64 ) This is a reddened quasar with weak \ and ] lines. There appears to be absorption from \ lines. There is narrow absorption on top of . The object appears in the photometric catalog of Richards2009 who infer a photometric redshift of z_ phot =3.54 .

gmw_00013491.name: eHAQ1340+0151
gmw_00013491.overview: The object eHAQ1340+0151 is classified as a quasar [arXiv:1608.08404]. It has a tentative redshift identification of z=1.94, though it lacks strong spectral features to securely confirm this value [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1340+0151 as a quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1340+0151 ( z=1.94? ) There are no strong spectral features to securely identify this target. There is a sharp break in the SED after the z band. We tentatively identify the emission feature in the blue edge of the spectrum as Ly at redshift z=1.94 , which matches with the weak absorption feature in the g band. The reddening required for this redshift solution is quite high ( A(V) 1 mag), but does not match the near-infrared photometry. At the same redshift, the near-infrared photometry requires no reddening.

gmw_00013492.name: eHAQ1340+1458
gmw_00013492.overview: eHAQ1340+1458 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=1.73 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits weak broad absorption lines in carbon IV and silicon IV, along with possible variations in its iron pseudo-continuum [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1340+1458 as a reddened quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1340+1458 ( z=1.73 ) This is a reddened quasar with weak BAL in \ and Si 4 . There is possibly variations in the iron pseudo continuum around ] . The near-infrared data require less reddening ( A(V) 0.3 ) than the optical data. This indicates that the extinction curve is steeper than SMC or that the intrinsic slope of the quasar power-law is steeper than average.

gmw_00013493.name: eHAQ1346+0114
gmw_00013493.overview: eHAQ1346+0114 is a highly reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=1.73 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits weak emission, blueshifted absorption, and possible variations in its iron pseudo-continuum [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1346+0114 as a highly reddened quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1346+0114 ( z=1.73 ) This is a highly reddened quasar with weak \ emission and blueshifted absorption in both \ and ] . There is possibly variations in the iron pseudo continuum around ] . The near-infrared data require less reddening ( A(V) 0.3 ) than the optical data. This indicates that the extinction curve is steeper than SMC or that the intrinsic slope of the quasar power-law is steeper than average.

gmw_00013494.name: eHAQ1455+0705
gmw_00013494.overview: eHAQ1455+0705 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of approximately z=1.90 [arXiv:1608.08404]. The object exhibits associated absorption features, as well as tentative evidence of an intervening absorption system at a redshift of z=1.650 [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ1455+0705 as a reddened quasar and discusses its associated absorption, photometric-redshift comparison.

quotes.quote: eHAQ1455+0705 ( z=1.90 ) This is a reddened quasar with associated absorption in \ and ] . We see tentative evidence of an intervening \ absorption system at z= abs =1.650 . Richards2009 infer a photometric redshift of z_ phot =4.26 . The object is observed by Maddox2012 who also classify the object as a quasar at redshift z=1.9094 .

gmw_00013495.name: eHAQ2255+1213
gmw_00013495.overview: eHAQ2255+1213 is a reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=2.05 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits broad absorption features likely caused by a combination of weak high-ionization broad absorption lines and iron low-ionization broad absorption lines [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ2255+1213 as a FeLoBAL quasar and discusses its radio information.

quotes.quote: eHAQ2255+1213 ( z=2.05 ) This is a reddened quasar with broad absorption around \ and ] possibly caused by a combination of weak high-ionization BAL and FeLoBAL features. The iron absorption would also explain the deficit observed in the Y and J bands. The object is detected in the FIRST survey at 1.4 GHz, F_ 1.4 GHz =4.19 0.16 mJy.

gmw_00013496.name: eHAQ2256+0531
gmw_00013496.overview: eHAQ2256+0531 is a highly reddened, weak-line quasar at a redshift of z=1.35 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It is a known radio source that exhibits narrow associated absorption and emission from both its host galaxy and hot dust [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ2256+0531 as a weak-line quasar and discusses its associated absorption, radio information, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ2256+0531 ( z=1.35 ) This is a highly reddened quasar with weak emission lines an narrow associated absorption. We observe a large excess in the H and K bands, most likely due to the H line in the H band and emission from the host-galaxy and hot dust that start to become important in the K band (rest-frame 1 m). The object is a known radio source detected at several frequencies. It is detected in FIRST and NVSS at 1.4 GHz with a flux of F_ FIRST =11.99 0.13 and F_ NVSS =14.8 0.6 mJy, respectively. Jackson2007 report a flux at 8.4 GHz of F_ 8.4 GHz =10.7 0.2 mJy and Coble2007 report a flux at 28.5 GHz of F_ 28.5 GHz =8.15 0.15 mJy.

gmw_00013497.name: eHAQ2303+0747
gmw_00013497.overview: eHAQ2303+0747 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=1.31 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It is classified as a quasar based on its broad emission line, and its reddening has been estimated using photometry [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ2303+0747 as a quasar and discusses its photometric constraints, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ2303+0747 ( z=1.31 ) The spectrum from INT does not match the photometry due to issues in the flux calibration. We therefore only use the spectrum to securely classify this object as a quasar on the basis of the broad emission line ( \ at z=1.31 ). We estimate the reddening using only the photometry. The object is detected in the two bands of GALEX (near-UV and far-UV): NUV = 22.7 0.2 AB mag and FUV > 23.2 AB mag.

gmw_00013498.name: eHAQ2321+1121
gmw_00013498.overview: The object eHAQ2321+1121 is classified as a reddened quasar at a redshift of z=2.18 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits a deficit in the J and H bands, which is plausibly explained by variations in the iron pseudo continuum [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper identifies eHAQ2321+1121 as a reddened quasar and summarizes the spectral and photometric evidence used for that classification.

quotes.quote: eHAQ2321+1121 ( z=2.18 ) This is a reddened quasar. We observe a deficit in the J and H bands compared to the best-fit template. This can plausibly be explained by variations in the iron pseudo continuum. This would also explain the small excess in the spectrum around the z band.

gmw_00013499.name: eHAQ2358+1030
gmw_00013499.overview: The object eHAQ2358+1030 is a highly reddened quasar located at a redshift of z=2.05 [arXiv:1608.08404]. It exhibits very strong and narrow emission lines, including a clearly detected helium II emission line [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper identifies eHAQ2358+1030 as a highly reddened quasar and summarizes the spectral and photometric evidence used for that classification.

quotes.quote: eHAQ2358+1030 ( z=2.05 ) This is a highly reddened quasar with very strong and narrow emission lines. We clearly detect the He 2 emission line redwards of . We furthermore observe an emission line at _ obs = 6988.8 , which is caused by second order contamination from Ly . This quasar is described in detail in Heintz2016a .

gmw_00013500.name: eHAQ2359+1354
gmw_00013500.overview: The object eHAQ2359+1354 is a quasar located at a redshift of 2.79 [arXiv:1608.08404]. Observations of the quasar show evidence of metal absorption from iron and silicon, as well as a damped Lyman-alpha system at a redshift of 2.248 [arXiv:1608.08404].

sources.summary: The paper describes eHAQ2359+1354 as a quasar and discusses its DLA absorption evidence, reddening/extinction behavior.

quotes.quote: eHAQ2359+1354 ( z=2.79 ) We observed this target with grism 6 to look for a DLA around z_ abs = 2.248 . We see evidence for metal absorption from Fe 2 and Si 2 and a sharp drop around 4000 , which corresponds to the location of the Ly line from the DLA. We only use the spectrum to fix the redshift and subsequently determine the reddening by fitting the BOSS spectrum.

gmw_00013501.name: MS1520-A
gmw_00013501.overview: MS1520-A was initially identified as a candidate Lyman-alpha emitter at a redshift of approximately 7 [arXiv:1204.3233]. However, spectroscopic observations detected neither Lyman-alpha emission nor a ultraviolet continuum, making both Lyman-alpha emitter and Lyman-break galaxy classifications unlikely [arXiv:1204.3233].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MS1520-A as the only remaining z~7 LAE candidate, but spectroscopy finds no Ly emission or continuum and makes both LAE and LBG interpretations unlikely.

quotes.quote: As a result, one object in MS1520 remained (hereafter MS1520-A). Table 1 and Figure 4 show its photometry and images. In our previous NB980 survey in 2003, we had selected more candidates with more inclusive criteria z' - NB980 > 1.3 and 2 limiting magnitudes. Then, we had conducted spectroscopy on 2003 June 22--23 with multi-object slits and FOCAS. We confirmed that MS1520-A selected in Section SelectionCriteria had been also observed in 2003.
We inspected the sky-subtracted stacked spectrum of MS1520-A and could identify neither Ly emission, a UV continuum nor any other spectral features.

This is 2--5 times fainter than F^ NB _ Ly , deep enough to detect Ly . Thus, MS1520-A is unlikely an LAE. Possibility of the Candidate Being an LBG Another possible origin of MS1520-A is a z 7 LBG. However, we could neither see any faint continuum by eye on the 2 dimensional spectrum nor in the 1 dimensional one after the 4 pixel binning in the spatial direction.

gmw_00013502.name: M-7443-06103
gmw_00013502.overview: M-7443-06103 is a disk galaxy with a identified kinematic center and major kinematic axis [arXiv:1712.03418]. Its structure is characterized by a surface-brightness profile that fits a broken exponential disk model [arXiv:1712.03418].

sources.summary: The paper uses M-7443-06103 to demonstrate surface-brightness profiling, marking its kinematic centre and major axis and fitting its profile with a broken exponential disc model.

quotes.quote: The upper panel of Fig. figure:SBprofile-example shows the surface-brightness distribution across the image of the galaxy M-7443-06103 in sky coordinates (pixels). The position of the kinematic centre of the galaxy is marked by the circle and the major kinematic axis of the galaxy is indicated by the line in the upper panel of Fig. figure:SBprofile-example . The lower panel shows the observed surface-brightness profile (points) for the galaxy M-7443-06103. We use the surface-brightness profile in solar units for the bulge-disc decomposition.

The broken exponential fit to the surface-brightness profile of the galaxy M-7443-06103 is shown by the line in the lower panel of Fig. figure:SBprofile-example . The isophotal radius of the galaxy, the central surface brightness of the disc, and the galaxy luminosity estimated using Eq. ( equation:decomp2 ) are reported in Table table:sample .

gmw_00013503.name: M-8341-09102
gmw_00013503.overview: M-8341-09102 is a disk galaxy observed as part of the MaNGA survey [arXiv:1712.03418]. It features a stellar disk with measurable radial oxygen-abundance distributions across its individual spaxels [arXiv:1712.03418].

sources.summary: The paper presents M-8341-09102 as an example MaNGA galaxy with radial oxygen-abundance distributions and an N/O diagnostic diagram from individual spaxels.

quotes.quote: Panel a of Fig. figure:ohx-rg-m-8341-09102 shows the radial distribution of the (O/H) _ R abundances across the disc of the MaNGA galaxy M-8341-09102. The grey points are abundances in the individual spaxels. The dashed line is the best fit to those data. Panels b, , and d of Fig. figure:ohx-rg-m-8341-09102 show the radial distributions of the (O/H) _ S , (O/H) _ N , and (O/H) _ P abundances in the disc of M-8341-09102, respectively. The grey points are abundances in the individual spaxels, and the solid line depicts the best fit to those data.

The dashed line in Panels b, , and d is the R_g - (O/H) _ R relation from the Panel a . Panel e shows the (O/H) _ R - N/O diagram. The grey points denote abundances in the H ii regions in the nearby galaxies; the red points are abundances in the individual spaxels of the MaNGA galaxy M-8341-09102.

gmw_00013504.name: M-8439-12701
gmw_00013504.overview: M-8439-12701 is a galaxy characterized by its emission lines and star-forming regions [arXiv:1712.03418]. Its spaxel spectra show nitrogen-line diagnostic behavior that is broadly compatible with H II regions in nearby galaxies, except in low-metallicity, low-excitation areas [arXiv:1712.03418].

sources.summary: The paper compares spaxels in M-8439-12701 with H II regions, finding its nitrogen-line diagnostic behavior broadly compatible except in low-metallicity, low-excitation regions.

quotes.quote: The panels in the left column of Fig. figure:p-ln2ha-oh show the [ N ii ] /H ratio as a function of the excitation parameter P for the spaxels in the MaNGA galaxy M-8439-12701 and for the H ii regions in nearby galaxies with abundances 12+log(O/H) 0.05 (the value of 12+log(O/H) is indicated in each panel). The blue points are spaxels in M-8439-12701 and the red squares are the H ii regions in nearby galaxies. The squares represent the mean values of the [ N ii ] /H ratio in bins of 0.05 dex in P . The bars denote the mean values of the scatter of [ N ii ] /H in the bins. The solid line represents the same H ii regions in nearby galaxies but both the R _ 2 and N _ 2 lines are increased by a factor of 1.3.

Inspection of Fig. figure:p-ln2ha-oh reveals that the [ N ii ] /H - P diagrams for the MaNGA spaxel spectra are compatible with the one for the spectra of H ii regions in nearby galaxies. Differences are seen only in low-metallicity, low-excitation regions. Thus, there is little room for a significant increase of the strength of the low-ionization lines, [ O ii ] and [ N ii ] , relative to the Balmer lines in the MaNGA spaxel spectra due to the contribution of the DIG radiation.

aliases_external: Del Moro et al. 2016 source 28 / CDF-S XID 28
gmw_00013505.overview: The galaxy 28 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a quasar [arXiv:1712.07250]. It exhibits a flat effective photon index and an extremely low rest-frame X-ray luminosity relative to its mid-infrared luminosity [arXiv:1712.07250].

sources.summary: The paper singles out source 28/XID 28 among X-ray-undetected quasars: it is detected in the 7 Ms CDF-S catalog, has flat X-ray spectrum and low X-ray luminosity, and a differing photometric redshift.

quotes.quote: We note that amongst the X-ray undetected quasars in the sample, there is one source, 28 (see Table 1 from ), that is now detected in the 7 Ms CDF-S catalog (XID 28; ). This source has a very flat effective photon index of <0.93 (compared to the typical 1.8 for unabsorbed AGN) and an extremely low rest-frame X-ray luminosity ( L_ 0.5-7 keV 1.5 10^ 41  erg s ^ 1 , uncorrected for absorption) compared to its intrinsic luminosity measured in the MIR band ( log   L_ 6  m =45.97   erg s ^ 1 ), consistent with the upper limit reported in our analysis ( ) We note however, that the photometric redshift assumed in our analysis of this source ( z=2.55 ) differs from that reported in the Luo et al. 2017 ( ) catalog ( z=1.81 ).

gmw_00013506.name: J0312 + 1836
gmw_00013506.overview: J0312 + 1836 is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits a soft X-ray slope [arXiv:1711.07538]. The object is hypervariable, showing a slow, smooth optical outburst of 1 to 3 magnitudes from what is likely a normal low state [arXiv:1711.07538].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0312 + 1836 as a hypervariable AGN with a slow optical outburst, likely normal low state, weak intrinsic absorption, and a soft X-ray slope.

quotes.quote: Two of the objects (J0312 and J1422) show slow smooth outbursts by 1-3 mags, with superimposed variability. J1519, and possibly J1422, show evidence for two (or more) peaks. This is expected in a binary, or sheared ``Chang-Refsdal'', microlensing scenario, though it is not our intention to test this hypothesis at this stage. The key point is that for all of J0312, J1422, and J1519 it looks like the low-state may be the normal one.

A specific example of this is evident in the second observation of J0312 + 1836, where the observation with just 200 counts shows a broad 90 per cent confidence limit on N_ H, int . We observe a range in power-law slopes ( ). Flat power law slopes are characterised by = 2 , as seen in J1519 + 0011, soft slopes by > 2 , as seen in J0312 + 1836 ( 3 significance) and J2232 0806 ( 9 significance), and hard slopes by < 2 , which we observe in J1422 + 0140 to a smaller ( 2 ) significance.

gmw_00013507.name: J1422 + 0140
gmw_00013507.overview: J1422 + 0140 is a hypervariable active galactic nucleus that exhibits a comparatively hard X-ray slope [arXiv:1711.07538]. It displays a slow, smooth outburst with possible multiple peaks and contains absorption features consistent with an intervening system [arXiv:1711.07538].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1422 + 0140 as a hypervariable AGN with a slow outburst, possible multiple peaks, intervening absorption features, and a comparatively hard X-ray slope.

quotes.quote: Two of the objects (J0312 and J1422) show slow smooth outbursts by 1-3 mags, with superimposed variability. J1519, and possibly J1422, show evidence for two (or more) peaks. This is expected in a binary, or sheared ``Chang-Refsdal'', microlensing scenario, though it is not our intention to test this hypothesis at this stage. The key point is that for all of J0312, J1422, and J1519 it looks like the low-state may be the normal one.

The absorption features in J1422 + 0140 are consistent with an intervening system. Whilst the doublet on the blue wing of the profile (at 2750 ) is likely absorption in an outflowing component intrinsic to the AGN, the second doublet seen at 2500 \ in Fig. \ fig:masses is either absorption in an extreme outflow (at 0.1 ) or more probably in an intervening system at z 0.855 .

We observe a range in power-law slopes ( ). Flat power law slopes are characterised by = 2 , as seen in J1519 + 0011, soft slopes by > 2 , as seen in J0312 + 1836 ( 3 significance) and J2232 0806 ( 9 significance), and hard slopes by < 2 , which we observe in J1422 + 0140 to a smaller ( 2 ) significance.

gmw_00013508.name: J1519 + 0011
gmw_00013508.overview: J1519 + 0011 is a hypervariable active galactic nucleus that exhibits multiple optical peaks and likely has a normal low state [arXiv:1711.07538]. The object also shows X-ray fading over a short timescale of 14 days [arXiv:1711.07538].

sources.summary: The paper presents J1519 + 0011 as a hypervariable AGN with multiple optical peaks, a likely normal low state, flat X-ray slope, and significant short-timescale X-ray fading.

quotes.quote: J1519, and possibly J1422, show evidence for two (or more) peaks. This is expected in a binary, or sheared ``Chang-Refsdal'', microlensing scenario, though it is not our intention to test this hypothesis at this stage. The key point is that for all of J0312, J1422, and J1519 it looks like the low-state may be the normal one.

There is evidence for statistically significant ( 4.5 ) variability between the observations of J1519 + 0011, despite these observations being just 14 days apart. We see that whilst is consistent between the two observations, the normalisation has decreased, indicating that the object faded between observations. The optical light curve over the same period (Fig. \ fig:lightcurves_xmm ) does not show a significant change, possibly due to larger scatter and insufficient temporal sampling. We could therefore be seeing a differentially fading X-ray component in J1519 + 0011, or this could be due to fast X-ray variability commonly seen in AGN ( gierlinski08 , parker15 ).

gmw_00013509.name: J2232 0806
gmw_00013509.overview: J2232 0806 is a hypervariable active galactic nucleus that exhibits a flat-topped light curve, suggesting its high state may be its normal state [arXiv:1711.07538]. The source displays a soft X-ray spectrum and shows marginal evidence for a soft X-ray excess component [arXiv:1711.07538].

sources.summary: The paper describes J2232 0806 as a hypervariable AGN with a flat-topped light curve, possibly normal high state, soft X-ray spectrum, and a marginally improved soft-excess SED fit.

quotes.quote: The fourth object (J2232) looks rather different, showing a flat top with dips. In this case, it may be that the high state is the normal one.

We observe a range in power-law slopes ( ). Flat power law slopes are characterised by = 2 , as seen in J1519 + 0011, soft slopes by > 2 , as seen in J0312 + 1836 ( 3 significance) and J2232 0806 ( 9 significance), and hard slopes by < 2 , which we observe in J1422 + 0140 to a smaller ( 2 ) significance.
J2232 0806 did show evidence for an improved fit with the SX component ( \ of 1.07 compared to 1.11 without the SX component), however, the difference in end result was small.

gmw_00013510.name: SDSS J142333.95+453626.2
gmw_00013510.overview: SDSS J142333.95+453626.2 is a foreground galaxy with an estimated photometric redshift of approximately 0.6 [arXiv:1411.7368]. Located 0.5 arcseconds from GRB 130907A, this galaxy may explain the observed late-time flattening of the burst's optical afterglow [arXiv:1411.7368].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J142333.95+453626.2 as a foreground galaxy near GRB 130907A, with photometric redshift about 0.6, possible optical-afterglow flattening, and a limited galaxy-flux contribution.

quotes.quote: A foreground galaxy (SDSS J142333.95+453626.2 with photometric redshift z=0.6 0.3 ) at a distance of 0.5'' from GRB 130907A has been identified by 2013GCN..15192 with i and magnitudes comparable to that of the afterglow at 3 days after the trigger. This galaxy could tentatively explain the flattening of the optical afterglow at these late times. On the other hand, 2013GCN..15223 have limited the contribution of galaxy flux in and i bands to > 22.6 mag.

gmw_00013511.name: GALEX source J005813.0-033946
gmw_00013511.overview: GALEX source J005813.0-033946 is a compact starburst galaxy or region projected over the disc of a spiral galaxy at the same redshift [arXiv:1612.03275]. This luminous UV source represents a young, rapidly star-forming, and low-metallicity environment with a star-formation rate of 1 solar mass per year [arXiv:1612.03275].

sources.summary: The paper describes GALEX source J005813.0-033946 as the UV-bright starburst nucleus/component of iPTF15dld's host, sharing redshift with a spiral galaxy and showing young, rapidly star-forming properties.

quotes.quote: The SN is hosted by a compact starburst galaxy/region that, in turn, appears projected over the disc of a spiral galaxy. The narrow emission lines we detected in our spectra (see Sect. res:spectra ) indicate that the two objects, starburst and spiral galaxy, are located at the same redshift, although we cannot assess whether they form a unique structure or a galaxy pair. The starburst nucleus is a luminous UV source which was detected by GALEX on 8 October 2008 (GALEX source J005813.0-033946) with AB magnitudes FUV = 18.89, and NUV = 18.38, (Kron aperture; note that the NUV band, 2300 , is similar to the uvm2 band of Swift /UVOT).

This and the lack of UV flux variability suggest that the source detected by UVOT is dominated by the emission of the starburst region, so that the UV emission of the SN is undetectable. At a distance of 200 Mpc, the starburst component has an absolute magnitude in g band of -18.5 mag, which places it at the bright end of the blue compact dwarf luminosity function Tolstoy2009 .

This satisfactory match indicates the presence of a young massive star population, consistent with the explosion of a massive stellar core that has evolved from a main sequence mass of 20 M _ (see Section sect:discussion ). We note that a Milky Way extinction curve only provides a match with the starburst colors if the intrinsic extinction is as high as E_ B-V = 0.8, which is inconsistent with the observed colours of the starburst and indicates that this region presents the characteristics of a more rapidly star-forming, lower metallicity, less evolved environment than our Galaxy. In fact, the star-formation rate of 1 M _ yr ^ 1 derived by Palli2016 from radio excess detection within a region a few kpc across, spatially compatible with the UVOT source, points to an explosion site of high star formation rate per unit mass.

gmw_00013512.name: source 52
gmw_00013512.overview: Source 52 is a galaxy candidate with an elongated optical counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0402102]. Its optical spectrum shows calcium H and K lines and the G-band, indicating a redshift of z=0.255 [arXiv:astro-ph/0402102].

sources.summary: The paper treats source 52 as a galaxy candidate: its elongated optical counterpart has absorption features and z=0.255, while X-ray spectral and timing tests leave the galaxy hypothesis unresolved.

quotes.quote: The source identified as the optical counterpart of our source 52 in the i-band photometry is elongated and the spectrum shows calcium H and K lines and the G-band. From the spectral line shifts, a redshift of z=0.255 has been determined by the AXIS group and they propose that this source could be a galaxy. We extracted the MOS spectra of source 52 in the same way as described in Section sec:0751spectra . We have fitted the spectrum with the extra-galactic origin in mind and the results can be found in Table tab:src52specfits .

We find a reasonably hard X-ray spectrum, which can not be fitted with a black body. We have also investigated a temporal variation, binning the data into 1ks and 5ks bins. However, we find no significant variation during our observation. From the X-ray spectral fitting and short time scale temporal analysis, we can not support nor disregard the galaxy hypothesis.

gmw_00013513.name: BCG
gmw_00013513.overview: The BCG is the brightest cluster galaxy in the galaxy cluster Abell 2254 [arXiv:1110.0846]. It is located near the peak of the cluster's X-ray emission and has a projected bright companion galaxy [arXiv:1110.0846].

sources.summary: The paper identifies BCG as ID. 53, notes it is only slightly brighter than other bright cluster galaxies, places it near the X-ray peak, and uses its coordinates as A2254's center.

quotes.quote: No evident dominant galaxy is present in the cluster since the brightest galaxy in our catalog (ID. 53, ^ =16.22 , hereafter BCG) is less than 0.5 mag brighter then the following brightest galaxies (IDs. 117, 102, 112 with ^ =16.64 , 16.66, and 16.67 respectively).
The BCG is close to the peak of the X-ray emission and has a (projected) bright companion ( ^ =17.01 , ID. 50 hereafter BCGc).
As for the center of A2254, we adopt the position of the BCG [ R.A.= 17^ h 17^ m 45 86 , Dec.= +19 40 48.4 (J2000.0) ] .

gmw_00013514.name: BCGc
gmw_00013514.overview: BCGc is a spiral galaxy characterized by a large bulge and an arm directed toward the brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:1110.0846]. It exhibits optical emission lines, ultraviolet emission, strong infrared emission, and faint radio emission [arXiv:1110.0846].

sources.summary: The paper describes BCGc as a projected bright companion to BCG with optical emission lines, infrared, UV, and faint radio emission; Subaru images show it as a spiral with a large bulge.

quotes.quote: The BCG is close to the peak of the X-ray emission and has a (projected) bright companion ( ^ =17.01 , ID. 50 hereafter BCGc). BCGc is characterized by [ OII ] , H , and [ OIII ] emission spectral lines, strong 24 MIPS-Spitzer emission see at http://sha.ipac.caltech.edu/applications/Spitzer/SHA/ , UV emission http://galex.stsci.edu/GalexView/ , and faint radio emission (ID. 2 in Table 3 of Rizza et al. ). The Subaru images show that it is a spiral galaxy with a large bulge and an arm directed toward the BCG (see Fig. merging ). We find a quite large relative velocity difference with respect to the BCG ( 1400 ).

gmw_00013515.name: ID. 93
gmw_00013515.overview: ID. 93 is a cluster galaxy with an optical spectrum typical of elliptical galaxies [arXiv:1110.0846]. It is a likely optical counterpart to a bright radio source, though a nearby faint, extended object introduces some uncertainty regarding this identification [arXiv:1110.0846].

sources.summary: The paper treats ID. 93 as the likely optical counterpart of a bright radio source, describing an elliptical spectrum, radio-galaxy-level power, a nearby faint extended object, and uncertain radio identification.

quotes.quote: The likely optical counterpart might be the cluster galaxy ID. 93 (hereafter ``RG'') which has an optical spectrum typical of ellipticals. ID. 93, with an absolute magnitude M_ R 21.9 and a radio power log P_ 1.4GHz ( W Hz ^ 1 ) 24.5 , can be accommodated among the radio galaxies in clusters (e.g., Ledlow Owen ; Bardelli et al. ). However, a very faint, extended object close to RG is revealed by the Subaru image (Fig. agn ). Due to the present resolution in radio data, there is some doubt on the identification of RG as the (unique) responsible of the whole radio emission of the bright discrete radio source.

gmw_00013516.overview: The flat-spectrum radio quasar 0202 172 is characterized by a high measured polarization of 5.15 percent [arXiv:astro-ph/0101502]. It also exhibits a very blue power law continuum, which may indicate a shift in the location of its synchrotron peak [arXiv:astro-ph/0101502].

sources.summary: The paper treats 0202 172 as an exception to the polarization-synchrotron trend, noting high measured polarization, an unusually blue continuum, and possible movement of the synchrotron peak.

quotes.quote: Secondly, 0202 172 has a measured polarisation of 5.15, but also has a very blue power law continuum ( = -1.85 ). This continuum slope is possibly too steep to attribute to synchrotron (it implies a value of p = 1.3 - in turn implying a rather flat energy distribution ). However, the location of the synchrotron peak may have shifted in the period since the polarisation measurements were made (the polarisation measurements were taken 8 years prior to our photometry observations).

gmw_00013517.name: WHDF 8222
gmw_00013517.overview: WHDF 8222 is a quasar at a redshift of z=2.68 that has been classified as a galaxy [arXiv:2211.07324]. It is a faint X-ray source, and deep H-band imaging reveals a double object that is probably gravitationally lensed [arXiv:2211.07324].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WHDF 8222 as a z=2.68 DESI QSO classified as a galaxy, lists it as a faint X-ray source, and notes H-band morphology indicating a double, probably lensed object.

quotes.quote: Note that the z=2.68 DESI QSO (ID 8222 in Table tab:WHDF_desi_fullinfo ) is found to be a double object in deep WHDF H-band imaging with 0.''9 seeing.
The two DESI QSOs classified as galaxies with g<22.5 are WHDF 8222 at z=2.68 and WHDF 3081 at z=1.31 .
WHDF 8222 is similarly listed as a fainter X-ray source by see Table tab:WHDF_desi_fullinfo . We have already noted that the z=2.68 QSO is a double object (in H band) and probably lensed.

gmw_00013518.name: WHDF 3081
gmw_00013518.overview: WHDF 3081 is a quasar at a redshift of z=1.31 that is classified as a galaxy [arXiv:2211.07324]. It is a relatively bright X-ray source and appears to be interacting with a pair of very red, compact neighboring sources [arXiv:2211.07324].

sources.summary: The paper treats WHDF 3081 as a z=1.31 DESI QSO classified as a galaxy, associates it with the X-ray source WHDFCH052, and notes apparent interaction with two red compact neighbors.

quotes.quote: The two DESI QSOs classified as galaxies with g<22.5 are WHDF 8222 at z=2.68 and WHDF 3081 at z=1.31 .
WHDF 3081 is also found to be a relatively bright X-ray source, WHDFCH052, listed by but not by .
The z=1.31 QSO appears to be interacting with a pair of very red compact sources at 3'' distance.

gmw_00013519.name: GALEX J091332.6-101108.2
gmw_00013519.overview: GALEX J091332.6-101108.2 is a Seyfert 1.9 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=0.055 [arXiv:1103.5900]. The continuum of this galaxy is dominated by stellar absorption features originating from its host galaxy [arXiv:1103.5900].

sources.summary: GALEX J091332.6-101108.2 is the only nonstellar source in the observed GUVV2 sample, classified as a z=0.055 Seyfert 1.9 galaxy with host-galaxy dominated continuum.

quotes.quote: All the observed sources are classified as stars except one AGN (i.e., GALEX J091332.6-101108.2).

The spectrum of GALEX J091332.6-101108.2 shows a strong broad H emission line, but a very weak and even undetectable broad H component, which allows us to classify the object as a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy rather than a typical type I AGN. The redshift is inferred to be z=0.055 according to the H emission line. The continuum of the object is dominated by the stellar absorption features emitted from the host galaxy, which makes the object is not included in the quasar candidates selected from the GUVV2 catalog through their colors by Wheatley et al. (2008).

gmw_00013520.overview: The active galactic nucleus 0141+268 is a radio galaxy associated with a BL Lacertae type galaxy [arXiv:1804.05640]. At a wavelength of 4.2 cm, the source features a compact nucleus and a faint radio jet [arXiv:1804.05640].

sources.summary: The paper uses 0141+268 as the occulted background source, identifying it as a radio galaxy and AGN associated with a BL Lac type galaxy, with a compact radio nucleus and faint jet.

quotes.quote: The occultation of the radio galaxy 0141+268 by the asteroid (372) Palma on 2017 May 15 was observed using six antennas of the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA).

The background source in the present observations is the active galactic nucleus (AGN) 0141+268 (J0144+270), associated with a BL Lac type galaxy 2016AJ....152...12L . At the wavelength used ( =4.2 cm), the source consists of a compact nucleus, < 3 milliarcseconds (mas), and a faint jet. An image of the source at 8.7 GHz from the VLBA experiment VCS-II-D/BG219D (2014 June 9; 2016AJ....151..154G ), is shown in Figure  fig:0141+268 .

gmw_00013521.name: J1040.5+0617
gmw_00013521.overview: J1040.5+0617 is a blazar that exhibits minor activity during its quiescent state [arXiv:1910.13725]. During this quiescent state, the source displays flux variations as large as a factor of three, while its flare emission is characterized by softer spectral properties [arXiv:1910.13725].

sources.summary: The paper reports that J1040.5+0617 had a loner-flare significance of 7.1, minor quiescent-state activity, softer flare emission, and quiescent flux variations as large as a factor of three.

quotes.quote: The blazars are listed in Table tab:src . Most of the flares of the blazars are 10 -19 above the quiescent light curves, while in the cases of J0055.1 1219, J0303.4 2407, J1040.5+0617, and J2134.2 0154, the significances compared to the quiescent light curves are 6.0 , 7.7 , 7.1 , and 9.3 , respectively.

We cautiously note that for several cases, there appear to be minor activities, such as in J0133.1 5201, J0303.4 2407, and J1040.5+0617. However for these activities, only a few data points are slightly above the 1 ranges of the light curves. We thus still considered them as in the quiescent states.

For the other 8 sources, five did not have significant spectral changes but three of them (J0221.1+3556, J1040.5+0617, and J1748.6+7005) had softer emission when in flares. Examining them, the 3-day binned light curve of J0221.1+3556 shows a complex flaring pattern with two major peaks, while the latter two had relatively ``active" quiescent states: J1040.5+0617 had as large as a factor of 3 flux variations and J1748.6+7005 showed minor activities following the flare, and returned to the lowest-flux level at the end of its long-term light curve.

gmw_00013522.name: J1748.6+7005
gmw_00013522.overview: J1748.6+7005 is a blazar that exhibits spectral variations during different activity states [arXiv:1910.13725]. While its quiescent state is best described by a log-parabola model, its flaring state emission is consistent with a power law and characterized by softer emission [arXiv:1910.13725].

sources.summary: The paper finds J1748.6+7005 may be power-law in the flaring state, has softer flare emission, and shows minor post-flare activity before returning to its lowest long-term flux.

quotes.quote: However from the \ spectra we obtained (see below Section sec:spe ), we noted that five sources (J0221.1+3556, J0236.8 6136, J0629.3 1959, J1617.9 7718, and J1748.6+7005), provided with spectral models of a log-parabola in 4FGL, might have power-law emission instead, particularly in the quiescent states. We re-ran the likelihood analysis assuming a power-law model for them in the source models, and found that a log-parabola is not significantly preferred over a power law in the quiescent states of the first four sources, while for J1748.6+7005, its emission in the flaring state can be described with a power law.

Our detailed analysis shows that in the quiescent states, four of the log-parabola sources are actually well described with a power law, and J1748.6+7005 in the flaring state had emission consistent with being a power law. We note that the spectrum of the last source in the quiescent state also appears flat, although the likelihood analysis indicates that the spectrum is better described with a log-parabola. In any case, we have found four clear cases showing spectral form changes, from a power law in the quiescent state to a log-parabola in the flaring state, which are supported by the their spectra (Figure fig:spec ) we obtained. This result is slightly different from previous studies such as in hcd14 and kn15 , since they have found that nearly all the sources kept the same form of emission during flares. As indicated by both Figure fig:spec and Table tab:src , most sources (16 of them) had harder emission when in flares, which is a pattern often seen in bright \ emission of blazars (e.g., nal13,hay+15,bri+16 ). For the other 8 sources, five did not have significant spectral changes but three of them (J0221.1+3556, J1040.5+0617, and J1748.6+7005) had softer emission when in flares. Examining them, the 3-day binned light curve of J0221.1+3556 shows a complex flaring pattern with two major peaks, while the latter two had relatively ``active" quiescent states: J1040.5+0617 had as large as a factor of 3 flux variations and J1748.6+7005 showed minor activities following the flare, and returned to the lowest-flux level at the end of its long-term light curve.

gmw_00013523.name: GOODS LRb 001 1 q1 51 1
gmw_00013523.overview: GOODS LRb 001 1 q1 51 1 is a high-redshift emission-line galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. Its redshift was revised to z=1.6045 after the identification of CIII] emission in its spectrum [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper revises GOODS LRb 001 1 q1 51 1 to z=1.6045 after comparing VIMOS and FORS2 spectra and identifying CIII] emission.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 001 1 q1 51 1 at z=2.2077 (flag A) in the previous release, was observed also with FORS2 (GDS J033226.67-274013.4). The low-S/N UV absorption lines were misclassified in the VIMOS spectrum. Instead, thanks to the comparison with the FORS2 spectrum, CIII ] in emission could be identified. The new redshift obtained, z=1.6045 , with quality flag B, is in agreement with the FORS2 measurement ( z=1.612 , flag A).

gmw_00013524.name: GOODS LRb 003 new q1 61 1
gmw_00013524.overview: GOODS LRb 003 new q1 61 1 is a high-redshift emission line galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. It has a revised redshift of z=1.0890, which is supported by the detection of CIII] and FeII emission lines [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper revises GOODS LRb 003 new q1 61 1 from a low redshift to z=1.0890 using CIII] and FeII lines, agreeing with Silverman et al.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 003 new q1 61 1 at z=0.067 (flag A) in the previous release. A possibly broad CIII ] emission line was previously identified as [ OII ] . This object was also observed by Silverman et al. (in preparation), where the measured redshift is z=1.089 . Our new estimate of the redshift based on CIII ] and FeII lines is in agreement with the more recent measurement. We find z=1.0890 with quality flag B.

gmw_00013525.name: GOODS LRb 003 new q3 11 1
gmw_00013525.overview: GOODS LRb 003 new q3 11 1 is a high-redshift emission-line galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. It is classified as a Lyman-alpha galaxy with a revised redshift of z=2.5882 [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper revises GOODS LRb 003 new q3 11 1 to z=2.5882 after comparing two VIMOS observations and correcting a Ly-alpha emission-line alignment.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 003 new q3 11 1 at z=2.6114 (flag B) in the previous release. This object was also observed in another mask (GOODS LRb 003 new 2 q3 30 1, z=2.5831 , flag B). Both estimates are based on the detection of a Ly- in emission. However, we noticed that the former measurement required a slight adjustment (i.e. the Ly- was correctly identified, but slightly misaligned). The new resulting redshift, z=2.5882 , with quality flag B, is in better agreement with the second VIMOS measurement.

gmw_00013526.name: GOODS LRb 003 new 1 q3 68 1
gmw_00013526.overview: GOODS LRb 003 new 1 q3 68 1 is an emission-line galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. It has a confirmed redshift of approximately 0.73, which was verified through multiple mask observations [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper confirms GOODS LRb 003 new 1 q3 68 1 at z=0.7297 through a second MR-mask observation and upgrades the redshift quality flag.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 003 new 1 q3 68 1 at z=0.7297 , flag C based on the identification of [ OII ] . The redshift is correct and it is confermed by a second observation in a MR mask (GOODS MR new 1 d q2 32 1 at z=0.7308 , flag A). We simply upgraded the quality flag from C to B in this case.

gmw_00013527.name: GOODS LRb 003 new 2 q1 42 1
gmw_00013527.overview: GOODS LRb 003 new 2 q1 42 1 is an emission line galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. It has a revised redshift of z=0.7357, determined by the identification of a broad MgII emission line and [OII] [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper revises GOODS LRb 003 new 2 q1 42 1 to z=0.7357 after identifying broad MgII and [OII] rather than Ly-alpha.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 003 new 2 q1 42 1 at z=2.1084 (flag B) in the previous release. The broad MgII emission line was misclassified as Ly- . This object was also observed in one of the new reduced masks (GOODS LRb dec06 3 q1 46 2, z=0.7358 , flag A). Two spectral features are clearly identified: a broad emission line from MgII and [ OII ] . Therefore, the new redshift assigned is z=0.7357 with quality flag A.

gmw_00013528.name: GOODS MR new 1 d q3 2 1
gmw_00013528.overview: GOODS MR new 1 d q3 2 1 is a high-redshift emission line galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. It has a revised redshift estimate of z=1.0349, which was corrected from an earlier estimate of z=0.5107 after its spectral features were reidentified as [OII] and MgII [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper revises GOODS MR new 1 d q3 2 1 to z=1.0349 after comparison with another observation identifies [OII] and MgII.

quotes.quote: GOODS MR new 1 d q3 2 1 at z=0.5107 (flag B) in the previous release. Two spectral features were misclassified: [ OIII ] and H . Thanks to the comparison with a second observation of this object (GOODS MR 001 q4 3 1 z=1.0338 , flag A), we identified [ OII ] and MgII instead. The new redshift estimate is z=1.0349 with quality flag B.

gmw_00013529.name: GOODS LRb 001 1 q2 66 1
gmw_00013529.overview: GOODS LRb 001 1 q2 66 1 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1001.1115]. Revised spectral analysis of the galaxy reveals typical active galactic nuclei features, including a broad MgII emission line, a broad Fe bump, and Ca H and K lines at a redshift of approximately 0.663 to 0.665 [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper identifies AGN-like spectral features in GOODS LRb 001 1 q2 66 1 and revises its redshift to about z=0.663-0.665.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 001 1 q2 66 1 at z=0.240 (flag B) in the previous release. The broad MgII emission line was misclassified as [ OII ] . After revising the spectrum we could identify some typical AGN features (e.g. a broad MgII emission line and broad Fe bump at 2960 ) plus [ OII ] and Ca H and K. The new redshift obtained is z=0.6632 (flag A).
GOODS LRb 003 new 1 q2 46 2 is the same object as GOODS LRb 001 1 q2 66 1 (see above). The new redshift measurement is z=0.6649 (flag A).

gmw_00013530.name: GOODS LRb 001 1 q3 5 3
gmw_00013530.overview: GOODS LRb 001 1 q3 5 3 is a high-redshift emission line galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. Although previously identified at a redshift of z=0.076, its single visible emission line is more likely Lyman-alpha, suggesting a tentative redshift of z=2.2980 [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper revises GOODS LRb 001 1 q3 5 3 from z=0.076 to a tentative z=2.2980 by reidentifying its single visible emission line as Ly-alpha.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 001 1 q3 5 3 at z=0.076 (flag B) in the previous release. The spectrum shows only one visible emission line at 4000 , which was previously interpreted as [ OII ] . For this object we find more likely the identification as Ly- in emission, which gives a new tentative redshift of z=2.2980 (flag C).

gmw_00013531.name: GOODS LRb 001 q4 44 1
gmw_00013531.overview: GOODS LRb 001 q4 44 1 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1001.1115]. Spectroscopic analysis of the source reveals broad carbon emission lines as well as iron and aluminum absorption lines [arXiv:1001.1115].

sources.summary: The paper revises two spectra of the same GOODS source to z=1.609 and z=1.608 using broad emission and absorption-line identifications.

quotes.quote: GOODS LRb 001 q4 44 1 and GOODS LRb 003 new q4 28 1 at z=1.6285 (flag B) in the previous release. We reviewed the redshifts of the two spectra for this object, which were based on the identification of two very broad emission lines (i.e. CIV and CIII ] ). In addition, we identified several absorption lines (e.g. FeII, AlIII) that helped further calibrating the redshifts. The new measurements are z=1.609 (flag A) and z=1.608 (flag A) for GOODS LRb 001 q4 44 1 and GOODS LRb 003 new q4 28 1, respectively.

gmw_00013532.name: CXOCY J125304.0-090737
gmw_00013532.overview: CXOCY J125304.0-090737 is a high-redshift quasar located at z=4.179 [arXiv:astro-ph/0211643]. It exhibits a typical quasar spectrum, faint hard X-ray emission, and strong X-ray-to-optical emission compared to optically selected quasars [arXiv:astro-ph/0211643].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CXOCY J125304.0-090737 as a z=4.179 high-redshift CYDER quasar with a typical QSO spectrum, faint hard X-ray emission, and X-ray-strong optical-to-X-ray properties.

quotes.quote: We find a high redshift quasar, CXOCY J125304.0-090737 at z=4.179 , which suggests that the abundance of low luminosity high redshift quasars may be larger than what would be expected from reasonable extrapolations from the quasar optical luminosity function.

CXOCY J125304.0-090737 is an optically faint quasar ( M_B=-23.69+5 log (h_ 65 ) ) with a typical QSO spectrum (figure  fig5 ). In X-rays, CXOCY J125304.0-090737 is also X-ray faint ( f_X = 1.7 0.4 10^ 15 ergs s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in the [ 0.5-2.0 ] keV band) with a somewhat harder spectrum ( 1.7 or HR 0.35 ) than typical low redshift or high redshift optically selected quasars. We speculate that a reflection component can slightly harden the spectrum but by no means is this the only mechanism. CXOCY J125304.0-090737 X-ray-to-optical emission is X-ray strong ( _ ox 1.35 ) compared to high redshift optically selected quasars (see Castander et al 2002 for further details). CXOCY J125304.0-090737 is the only quasar above z=4 found so far in the CYDER survey.

gmw_00013533.name: CXOCY J125241.0-091622
gmw_00013533.overview: CXOCY J125241.0-091622 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=2.282 [arXiv:astro-ph/0211643]. It exhibits a hard X-ray spectrum and a red optical color, both of which indicate that the object is obscured [arXiv:astro-ph/0211643].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CXOCY J125241.0-091622 as a z=2.282 quasar with a hard X-ray spectrum, red optical color, and X-ray/optical evidence for obscuration.

quotes.quote: CXOCY J125241.0-091622 is a quasar at redshift z=2.282 (Figure  fig4 ) with a harder than usual X-ray spectrum 1.3 . In the optical its V-K color is 4.0 which is one magnitude redder than the expected colour of a prototype quasar at this redshift (Figure  fig3 ). Both X-ray and optical data thus indicate that this is an obscured object.

gmw_00013534.name: XMMSL1 J065525.2+370815
gmw_00013534.overview: XMMSL1 J065525.2+370815 is a galaxy that exhibits an apparently extended, blue optical morphology [arXiv:1702.06807]. Its infrared colors place it in the overlap region between star-forming galaxies and luminous red galaxies, and it hosted a fading X-ray transient consistent with a tidal disruption event [arXiv:1702.06807].

sources.summary: The paper treats XMMSL1 J065525.2+370815 as a transient-associated source: imaging and WISE colours support a galaxy/host interpretation, while its X-ray fading is broadly consistent with tidal disruption.

quotes.quote: The SDSS image, taken coincident with the observed X-ray peak in 2006, shows an apparently extended source with a blue colour. A weak trace is visible in the 2011 WHT ACAM imaging, although this is not sufficient to extract a spectrum. We therefore either find this source in its low flux state at this time, or we are detecting the faint host galaxy of a now faded transient.

The colours of this source are not stellar and neither do they lie in the region occupied by typical AGN. It appears to lie in the overlap region between star forming galaxies and luminous red galaxies. This supports the idea that we are observing a galaxy, superimposed upon which is a transient event.
