The X-ray light curve shows a single peak with subsequent fading, characterised by two detections in 2003 and 2006 and two upper limits in 2008 and 2016, which are broadly consistent with the t ^ 5/3 decay expected from tidal disruption events.

gmw_00013535.name: XMMSL1 J162533.2+632411
gmw_00013535.overview: XMMSL1 J162533.2+632411 is a weak-emission galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.104 [arXiv:1702.06807]. It is tentatively classified as a highly absorbed Type II or intermediate active galactic nucleus that exhibits unusually hard X-ray behavior [arXiv:1702.06807].

sources.summary: The paper identifies XMMSL1 J162533.2+632411 as a weak-emission galaxy at z=0.104 and a likely highly absorbed Type II or intermediate AGN with unusually hard X-ray behavior.

quotes.quote: With our WHT observations we also detected XMMSL1 J162533.2+632411, a galaxy with weak H emission, and weak Na I and Mg Ib absorption complexes. We were unable to perform profile fitting on the H line, but were able to obtain a redshift. We tentatively classify this source as a Type II, highly absorbed AGN on account of the narrow appearance of H and lack of a soft X-ray band detection in the Slew Survey, and include it in Table tab:lines .

XMMSL1 J162533.2+632411 was designated a quasar candidate in automated classification analysis of SDSS DR10 objects Brescia . We detected the source with the WHT but we found only very weak lines with which to estimate a redshift of z=0.104 (Fig. fig:otherspectra ). The source is remarkably hard in the X-rays, having both a hard band and a full band detection in the Slew Survey - the only one of our candidate AGN not present in the Slew soft band. While counts above 2 keV are seen in the XRT spectrum, the source remained very hard, with a hardness ratio of 2--10 keV/0.3--2 keV = 3.5.

gmw_00013536.name: NGC 4493
gmw_00013536.overview: NGC 4493 is a lenticular galaxy that features a distorted stellar disc [arXiv:2009.02135]. The galaxy shows clear evidence of an interaction with a neighboring companion and hosts a young stellar component estimated to be a few hundred million years old [arXiv:2009.02135].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4493 as a counterexample host: an S0 galaxy with SN 2004br and SN 1994M, a distorted interacting disc, and a young stellar component a few hundred Myr old.

quotes.quote: The discovery of SN 2004br 2004IAUC.8340....1G , a 91T-like event 2012MNRAS.425.1789S , in NGC 4493 of S0 morphology (see Table tabSNhostmorph and Fig. deltm15vsmorph ), which consists of only old stellar population from a naive point of view. In NGC 4493, another SN Ia was also discovered SN 1994M; 1994IAUC.5982....2W with a normal spectroscopic classification 2012AJ....143..126B . Interestingly, this host galaxy has a distorted stellar disc and shows obvious evidence of an interaction with neighbor/companion galaxy 2012A&A...544A..81H . The age of the younger stellar component in this galaxy is estimated to be down to about a few hundred Myr 2009MNRAS.397..717S .

gmw_00013537.name: SDSSJ0738+2710
gmw_00013537.overview: SDSSJ0738+2710 is a quasar spectrum characterized by the narrowest line and the strongest narrow emission line component [arXiv:1606.02726]. Because the black hole properties derived from its emission line width are strongly biased by this narrow-line region contribution, the object is excluded from analysis [arXiv:1606.02726].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSSJ0738+2710 as having the narrowest line and strongest narrow component; its line-width-derived black-hole properties are biased enough that the object is excluded from the analysis.

quotes.quote: The sole exception is the line in the spectrum of SDSSJ0738+2710. In addition to having the narrowest line, this spectra also has the strongest narrow component (EW = 63 ), which suggests that a contribution from the narrow-line region might be important. Introducing a single Gaussian for the narrow emission, while retaining a double Gaussian for the broad emission, the FWHM of the broad component increases to 3400 (compared to 1580 without the narrow component). For consistency, the parameters quoted in Table tab:emissionproperties are from the model with no narrow component. However, because the properties derived from the emission line width (the BH mass and the mass-normalised accretion rate) are strongly biased by the probable contribution from the narrow-line region, SDSSJ0738+2710 is excluded from the analysis in Section sec:results .

gmw_00013538.name: Source 2864
gmw_00013538.overview: Source 2864 is an extragalactic background source located near the southern edge of the NGC 2024 star-forming region [arXiv:2208.07815]. It is identified as a blazar that exhibits multi-epoch submillimeter variability and a core-jet structure [arXiv:2208.07815].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Source 2864/GBS-VLA J054121.69 021108.3 as a multi-epoch submillimeter variable, arguing VLBA compactness, core-jet structure, and no parallax make it an almost certain background blazar.

quotes.quote: Along with JW 566 in OMC2/3, our search for faint transient events uncovers a multi-epoch variable near the southern edge of the NGC 2024 star-forming region field, at R.A. \ 05:41:21.7, Dec. \ 02:11:08.3 , associated with Source 2864 in megeath2012 and also GBS-VLA J054121.69 021108.3. This source is likely a blazar, as indicated by a parallax consistent with a background object kounkel17 , but it has at times been previously classified as a Class II YSO megeath2012 due to its spectral energy distribution and its location within an active star-forming region and rejected as a quasar because of the YSO classification. In contrast with JW 566, Source 2864 is detected in the every epoch of submm monitoring of the field.

The source remained extremely bright in follow-up VLBA observations kounkel17 , requiring a very small angular size for the emission region. Additionally, the VLBI image of Source 2864 reveals a core-jet structure petrov2021 that is typical of extragalactic sources. More importantly, the source revealed no measurable parallax, implying a location more distant than Orion. The source is therefore almost certainly a background, extragalactic, source, which, following the mid-IR color selection by paggi20 , we identify as a blazar.

gmw_00013539.name: QSO 4
gmw_00013539.overview: QSO 4 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0009229]. The quasar is located on the northern edge of a rich cluster of galaxies at a redshift of approximately 1.1 [arXiv:astro-ph/0009229].

sources.summary: The paper identifies QSO 4/B2 1335+28 as the field's only radio-loud quasar, near a rich z~1.1 red-galaxy clump and on the northern edge of cluster galaxies.

quotes.quote: The most distinctive excess in the bottom panel is at around QSO 4. In fact this clump corresponds to the cluster at z 1.1 identified previously by Yamada97 and Tanakaetal00 , and its richness is at least between those of the Virgo and the Coma clusters.

Since cl 2, near QSO 4, shows fairly lumpy and extended structure, we also estimate N_ 0.5 in the area centered on the QSO (the third row). The area does not overlap with cl 2 region, but the count is still large. These results agree with those in Tanakaetal00 based on deeper images of this small region.

QSO 4 (B2 1335+28) is the only radio-loud quasar (RLQ) in our field. Although RLQs often reside in the central regions of clusters (e.g. Ellingson Yee 1994), SG99 found that several RLQs at z>1 are also located in the outskirts of cluster regions. In our case, Tanaka et al.(2000) showed that QSO 4 also resides on the northern edge of the distribution of the cluster galaxies.

gmw_00013540.name: o2
gmw_00013540.overview: The object o2 is an extended source with a compact, bright nucleus surrounded by a faint elongated nebula [arXiv:astro-ph/0009064]. It has the morphology of a faint edge-on spiral galaxy and is most likely a background galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0009064].

sources.summary: The paper describes o2 as an extended source near PSR B0656+14, with a compact nucleus, faint elongated nebula, edge-on spiral-like morphology, and a likely background-galaxy interpretation.

quotes.quote: The object o2 shows a compact bright nucleus at _ 2000 = 6^h 59^m 48 16, _ 2000 = 14 ^ 14 23 2 (1 85 north of the pulsar). The nucleus is surrounded by a faint elongated nebula which occupies an area of 3 0 7 . The minimum distance between the nebula's southern edge and the pulsar, 1 1 in the F160W band, is smaller than the BTA seeing. This explains naturally the apparent association of the pulsar and o2 in the R and I bands.
The morphology of o2 looks like that of a faint spiral galaxy seen edge-on.
Thus, we conclude that o2 is most likely a faint background galaxy, as are the other extended objects o3 and o4.

gmw_00013541.name: o3
gmw_00013541.overview: Located north of a pulsar, o3 is an extended object with an irregular structure and a bright knot at its eastern edge [arXiv:astro-ph/0009064]. It appears to be associated with the object o2 and is classified as a faint background galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0009064].

sources.summary: The paper identifies o3 as an extended object north of the pulsar, describes its irregular knotty structure and association with o2, and includes it among likely faint background galaxies.

quotes.quote: The extended object o3 ( _ 2000 = 6^h 59^m 48 23, _ 2000 = 14 ^ 14 25 1) is 4 \ north of the pulsar. It has an irregular structure with a bright knot at its eastern edge. Being at only 1 \ from the northern edge of o2, the object o3 appears to be associated with o2 in the F160W image.
Thus, we conclude that o2 is most likely a faint background galaxy, as are the other extended objects o3 and o4.

gmw_00013542.name: o4
gmw_00013542.overview: The object o4 is an extended source located west of a pulsar [arXiv:astro-ph/0009064]. It exhibits a clumpy morphology that varies strongly across different bands, and it is most likely a faint background galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0009064].

sources.summary: The paper describes o4 as an extended source west of the pulsar with band-dependent, clumpy morphology, uncertain fluxes, and a likely interpretation as a faint background galaxy.

quotes.quote: The structure of the object o4 ( _ 2000 = 6^h 59^m 47 84, _ 2000 = 14 ^ 14 22 0; 5 \ west of the pulsar) varies strongly from band to band.

The spectrum of o4 appears to be more complicated. However, the estimated fluxes of this object are less definite because of its clumpy morphology and rather low S/N ( 1.5--2), particularly in the B, V and F555W bands.
Thus, we conclude that o2 is most likely a faint background galaxy, as are the other extended objects o3 and o4.

gmw_00013543.name: host galaxy of GRB 080928
gmw_00013543.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 080928 is a distant galaxy that remains undetected in deep optical and near-infrared imaging [arXiv:1007.0383]. These observations establish an absolute magnitude limit of greater than -19.94 in the r' band, which is consistent with the known luminosities of other gamma-ray burst hosts [arXiv:1007.0383].

sources.summary: The paper does not detect a galaxy at the GRB 080928 afterglow position, sets deep optical/NIR flux and absolute-magnitude limits, and finds no plausible host among nearby projected galaxies.

quotes.quote: The deep fifth-epoch GROND images taken 6.5 months after the burst at a seeing of 1'' do not show any galaxy underlying the position of the optical transient down to the following 3 upper limits (AB magnitudes): g^ =25.4, ^ =25.6, i^ =24.6, z^ =24.3, J=22.0, H=21.6, K_S=20.9 . Assuming for simplicity a power law spectrum for this galaxy of the form F_ ^ _ gal , for the ^ band this translates into an absolute magnitude of M_ ^ = m_ ' k , where =45.54 mag is the distance modulus and k the cosmological k correction, k= -2.5 (1- _ gal ) (1+z ). For a representative value of _ gal =1, this gives a lower limit of M_ ^ > -19.94 , which agrees with the luminosities found so far for the GRB host galaxy population.

Since no galaxy underlying the position of the afterglow could be detected, only deep flux limits for its host galaxy could be obtained. No other host galaxy candidate could be identified. However, given the redshift of the burst, this is not remarkable and matches the ensemble properties of the luminosities of GRB host galaxies found so far Savaglio2009 .

gmw_00013544.name: G1
gmw_00013544.overview: G1 is a relatively bright galaxy with an apparent magnitude of 23.41 [arXiv:1007.0383]. Photometric analysis indicates a best-fit redshift of 1.46, making it unlikely to be the host galaxy or the foreground absorber for the nearby GRB 080928 [arXiv:1007.0383].

sources.summary: G1 is a relatively bright galaxy near the GRB afterglow; its offset and photometric redshift make it unlikely to be the GRB 080928 host or the foreground absorber.

quotes.quote: Close to the position of the afterglow there is a relatively bright galaxy (labeled G1 in Fig.  fig:epozoom ) with ^ =23.41 0.05 . Using the stacked GROND g^ ^ i^ z^ band images from the fifth epoch, its central coordinates (Table  tab:g1-3 ) are offset by 2.6 0.3 arcse from the position of the optical afterglow. If this galaxy is at the redshift of the burst, then the projected offset of the optical transient from its center is 22.2 2.6 kpc. This is almost 20 times more than the median projected angular offset of 1.31 kpc found by Bloom2002a for a sample of 20 host galaxies of long bursts, making it unlikely that this is the host galaxy of GRB 080928 .
They indicate that with high probability none of the galaxies is the host galaxy and that G1 is not the foreground absorber seen in the afterglow spectrum.

When we treat the redshift as a free parameter, not fixing it to the value of the afterglow or the absorbing system, we find that the best HyperZ solution for G1 is z=1.46^ +0.15 _ 0.10 (Fig.  fig:galsed ), in both cases (whether we consider G2 to be a separate galaxy or not), confirming that G1 is not related to any other object.

gmw_00013545.name: G2
gmw_00013545.overview: G2 is a diffuse galaxy located near the galaxy G1 and a gamma-ray burst afterglow [arXiv:1007.0383]. Although its redshift is not conclusively determined, a potential redshift solution of 0.736 suggests it may be responsible for an absorption line system observed in the afterglow spectrum [arXiv:1007.0383].

sources.summary: G2 is a diffuse object near G1 and the GRB afterglow. Its free-redshift fit is inconclusive, but a z=0.736 solution could link it to the foreground absorption system.

quotes.quote: Some arcseconds south of G1 lies a diffuse object that could either be physically associated to G1 or represent another foreground/background galaxy. This object (G2 in Fig.  fig:epozoom ) is 1.5 0.3 arcsec away from the afterglow position. If it is at the redshift of the burst, its projected distance from the afterglow is 13 2.6 kpc, again hardly in agreement with the observed GRB offset distribution. However, both objects/galaxies are potentially close enough in projection to imprint a signal on the GRB afterglow spectrum.

Unfortunately, in the case of G2 a HyperZ fit with the redshift as a free parameter leads to no conclusive results, the resulting error bars are very large, and the photometry can be affected by the nearby galaxy G1. On the other hand, a HyperZ fit with the redshift fixed at z=0.736 gives a reasonable photometric solution ( ^2 /d.o.f = 1.07; Table  tab:hypresult ). This makes it possible that G2 is responsible for the absorption line system seen in the afterglow spectrum, given the proximity of G2 to the spectral slit passing over G3 and the afterglow.

gmw_00013546.name: G3
gmw_00013546.overview: G3 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.736 [arXiv:1007.0383]. Photometric analysis of the galaxy in four optical bands matches this redshift, though its dust extinction remains unconstrained [arXiv:1007.0383].

sources.summary: G3 is a galaxy identified in the slit 30 arcsec from the afterglow at z=0.736. Its HyperZ solution matches that redshift, though four-band data leave dust extinction unconstrained.

quotes.quote: In the 1 slit passing over the afterglow, identify a galaxy 30 away from the afterglow at a redshift of z=0.736 . This redshift is identical to the value found for the absorption line system Vreeswijk2008a . We labeled this galaxy as G3.

For object G3, we find a HyperZ solution in very good agreement with the value of z=0.736 reported by ( ^2 /d.o.f = 1.01). However, the detection in only the four optical bands does not allow us to constrain the dust extinction in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J045316-394905 as one of three remaining HFP candidates, close to the 20 GHz flux-density threshold, with spectra shown and observer-frame peak frequencies for the trio of 15--22 GHz.

quotes.quote: Thus only 3 HFP candidates remain (J045316-394905, J081240-475153, J183923-345348), i.e. 4.5 of sources in the sample. They are all close to the 20 GHz flux density threshold. gmw_00013550.name: J042127-481805
gmw_00013550.overview: J042127-481805 is classified as a blazar [arXiv:1210.2414]. It is identified as a likely high-synchrotron-peak source with a flat spectral energy distribution extending up to at least the J-band [arXiv:1210.2414].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J042127-481805 as one of six likely high-synchrotron-peak sources: PACO plus WISE fits require only a lower SED peak limit, and 2MASS photometry remains flat to J band.

quotes.quote: For the remaining 6 sources (J042127-481805, J093841-655611, J182927-570534, J194131-760548, J203958-720655, J230303-184126) the fit yields values of _ p ^ SED larger than 10^5 GHz, i.e. above the range covered by the data. Thus, only a lower limit ( _ p ^ SED 10^5 GHz) could be obtained. The Two Micron All Sky Survey (2MASS; Skrutskie et al. 2006) photometry, available for all these sources, confirms that their SEDs keep flat up to at least the J-band ( 2.4 10^5 GHz).
Thus, these are likely ``High Synchrotron Peak'' (HSP) sources ( _ p ^ SED > 10^6 GHz).

gmw_00013556.overview: The galaxy 91529 is classified as a rejuvenated galaxy [arXiv:1905.07541]. Following an initial star-forming period, it experienced a quiescent phase, underwent a period of rejuvenation, and subsequently returned to a quiescent state [arXiv:1905.07541].

sources.summary: The paper uses 91529 as a worked LEGA-C example whose history becomes quiescent, rejuvenates from bins 5 to 8, returns to quiescence, and has p_REJ = 1.

quotes.quote: For example, 91529 (see Figure fig:fit3 ) is star-forming in bin 2 then has a quiescent period in bin 3 and 4, which is followed by a period of rejuvenation from bin 5 to 8 before the galaxy is quiescent again from bin 9 to 12. In this case, p_ REJ = p_ Q_3 p_ SF_5 = 1 , and the galaxy is classified as a rejuvenated galaxy.

We also compute how long the galaxy was quiescent for before the first rejuvenation episode, i.e. the time range where p_ Q >0.5 after the initial SF episode and before rejuvenation (bin 3 to bin 4 for 91529), as well as measure how long the rejuvenation episode lasts, i.e. the time range where p_ SF >0.5 during the rejuvenation episode (bin 5 to 8 for 91529).

gmw_00013557.name: ATLAS J025.6821-33.4627
gmw_00013557.overview: ATLAS J025.6821-33.4627 is a highly luminous quasar at a redshift of z = 6.31 [arXiv:1502.07748]. Its spectrum exhibits a broad Lyman-alpha emission line, a faint Lyman-beta signal, and a transmission excess that may indicate a nearby ionized region [arXiv:1502.07748].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ATLAS J025.6821-33.4627 as a z=6.31 quasar with M_1450=-27.8, broad Ly emission, possible Ly signal, and transmission excess that may indicate nearby ionized gas.

quotes.quote: ATLAS J025.6821-33.4627 This quasar exhibits a broad Ly emission line from which we have estimated a redshift, z = 6.31 0.03. By scaling the template spectrum employed in Section sec2.3 to the observed redshift and z band magnitude of the object we estimate it has M_ 1450 = -27.8 0.2. This is the same as estimated for SDSS J1148+5251 by , making this the joint second most luminous known z \ 6 quasar, after SDSS J0100+2802 b38 . The spectrum exhibits a faint signal consistent with Ly , and an excess in transmission between 8700 A and 8800 A . This could indicate an ionised region close to the quasar, however near-infra-red spectroscopy is needed to better constrain the exact quasar redshift and hence the nature of any ionised regions.

gmw_00013558.name: ATLAS J029.9915-36.5658
gmw_00013558.overview: ATLAS J029.9915-36.5658 is a high-redshift quasar with an estimated redshift of z = 6.02 [arXiv:1502.07748]. It has an absolute magnitude of M_1450 = -27.0 and exhibits a probable absorption feature superimposed on its Lyman-alpha emission line [arXiv:1502.07748].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ATLAS J029.9915-36.5658 as a z=6.02 quasar with M_1450=-27.0 and a probable absorption feature superimposed on its Ly emission line.

quotes.quote: ATLAS J029.9915-36.5658 For this quasar, we estimate a redshift of z = 6.02 0.03 based upon the position of the broad Ly line. Following the same procedure as above, we estimate an absolute magnitude of M_ 1450 = -27.0 0.1. There is a probable absorption feature superimposed on the Ly emission line, as can also be observed in the spectra of several of the quasars first reported by .

gmw_00013559.name: RXJ 0921+4529
aliases_external: RXJ0921+4529 system / A quasar reference position
gmw_00013559.overview: RXJ 0921+4529 is a binary quasar system consisting of two distinct components, A and B [arXiv:1008.4156]. These components exhibit different spectra and redshifts, with a projected linear separation of 59.6 kpc [arXiv:1008.4156].

sources.summary: The paper argues RXJ 0921+4529 is a binary quasar system because components A and B have different spectra and redshifts, with a projected separation of 59.6 kpc.

quotes.quote: The new spectral observations were compared with the previously observed spectra of components A and B of RXJ 0921+4529, i.e. the same components observed in different epochs. We found a significant difference in the spectrum between the components that cannot be explained with microlensing and/or spectral variation. We conclude that RXJ 0921+4529 is a binary quasar system, where redshifts of quasars A and B are 1.6535 0.0005 and 1.6625 0.0015, respectively.

Taking the average redshift of A and B quasars ( z=1.658 ) and assuming flat cosmological model with _ m ­ = 0.27, _ = 0.73 and H_0 = 71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , the corresponding angular diameter distance of RX J0921+4529 is 1765 Mpc and the transverse separation (i.e. projected linear distance) between A and B components is 59.6 kpc.

gmw_00013560.name: iPTF15dmn host
gmw_00013560.overview: The iPTF15dmn host galaxy is not located within the SDSS footprint [arXiv:1608.06518]. It contains an active galactic nucleus suggested by nuclear optical variability, broad H-alpha emission, and narrow-line ratios, though it does not appear in the AGN locus based on radio emission or WISE colors [arXiv:1608.06518].

sources.summary: The paper links the iPTF15dmn host to AGN evidence from nuclear optical variability and broad/narrow line emission, while noting it is outside SDSS and outside radio or WISE AGN loci.

quotes.quote: We note that the host galaxies of iPTF15dmn and iPTF15fgl are not in the SDSS footprint so we cannot calculate their optical SFR with this method.

The presence of an AGN in iPTF15dmn is suggested by the optical variability and nuclear origin observed during our iPTF follow--up. A pre-outburst optical spectrum of iPTF15dmn host by the 6dF galaxy redshift survey bpt81,jsc+04 reveals broad H emission, and a large [ N 2 ] to H emission line ratio in the narrow lines galaxy emission component, that also point toward the presence of an AGN in this galaxy bpt81 .
It is interesting to note that neither iPTF15dni nor iPTF15dmn appear in the locus of AGN based on their radio emission or WISE colors (Figs. power_vs_z and wise_col_col ).

gmw_00013561.name: host galaxy of iPTF15dni
gmw_00013561.overview: The host galaxy of iPTF15dni is a likely active galactic nucleus host [arXiv:1608.06518]. Its optical spectrum shows broad hydrogen emission and strong narrow emission lines, though its radio emission and WISE colors do not place it in the standard active galactic nucleus selection loci [arXiv:1608.06518].

sources.summary: The paper treats the host galaxy of iPTF15dni as a likely AGN: optical spectra show broad H and strong narrow lines, but radio and WISE colors do not place it in AGN selection loci.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of iPTF15dni shows AGN signatures in its optical spectrum so we do not estimate its optical SFR (see Section optical_propt for discussion).

Optical spectroscopy of iPTF15dni obtained after the GW trigger reveals a similar pattern of broad H emission and strong narrow emission lines of [ N 2 ] and O 3 k16 . The spectrum is contaminated by significantly stronger stellar continuum; however, we consider it quite likely that an AGN is present in this case as well.

It is interesting to note that neither iPTF15dni nor iPTF15dmn appear in the locus of AGN based on their radio emission or WISE colors (Figs. power_vs_z and wise_col_col ). The fact that iPTF15dni and iPTF15dmu are not classified as AGN for their radio emission is not surprising.

gmw_00013562.name: 2QZ J223831.1 301152
gmw_00013562.overview: 2QZ J223831.1 301152 is a quasar located at a redshift of z = 1.6 [arXiv:1711.08110]. It has a DSS optical counterpart, a GALEX counterpart, and exhibits radio variability at 5.5 GHz [arXiv:1711.08110].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Source 2238 3011 as the quasar 2QZ J223831.1 301152 at z=1.6, notes GALEX/DSS counterparts, and reports significant 5.5 GHz variability in the FRB 160102 field.

quotes.quote: Source 2238 3011 was found to vary significantly at 5.5 GHz but not at 7.5 GHz. We identify it to the quasar 2QZ J223831.1 301152 from the ``Half a Million Quasar Survey'' HMQ at z = 1.6. This source is also present in the GALEX survey GALEX (GALEX J223831.1 301152) and has a DSS DSS optical counterpart.

gmw_00013563.name: host galaxy
gmw_00013563.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 201015A is located at the coordinates RA 23h 37m 16.4757s and Dec +53d 24m 54.626s [arXiv:2205.12750]. It has an observed magnitude of 22.9 [arXiv:2205.12750].

sources.summary: The paper reports the GRB 201015A host galaxy's magnitude and MMT position, places it about 10 kpc from the 1.5 GHz source, and argues the radio emission is afterglow-dominated.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: position of the host: =23^ h 37^ m 16.4757^ s , = +53^ 24^ 54.626^ (J2000; uncertainty = 0.235 )

quotes.quote: The host galaxy was firstly pinpointed by Belkin2020a and subsequently confirmed by Rastinejad2020 and Rossi2020 , who found a magnitude = 22.9 0.2 . With the MMT observations, we derive the position of the host: =23^ h 37^ m 16.4757^ s , = +53^ 24^ 54.626^ (J2000; uncertainty = 0.235 ); this is found to be 1.86 ^ far from the source observed at 1.5 GHz, which corresponds to roughly 10 kpc at z = 0.426 . The uncertainty in the radio position at 1.5 GHz is 0.03 ^ , which is 170 pc, and therefore we can state that the emission observed at 1.5 GHz is consistent with being generated by the afterglow. Moreover, as the beam size at 1.5 GHz is roughly 0.18 ^ 0.12 ^ , the emitting region should be of the order of 1 kpc 0.7 kpc; if the detected emission were caused by a high star forming region, we would have observed a stable emission in the optical at the same position instead of a transient event.

gmw_00013564.name: J032615.68-061358.2
gmw_00013564.overview: J032615.68-061358.2 is an observed candidate object with a power-law continuum that resembles a quasar [arXiv:2105.09171]. Because it lacks a Lyman-alpha break in its spectral range, it is likely a quasar at a redshift below 5.4 [arXiv:2105.09171].

sources.summary: The paper reports J032615.68-061358.2 as an observed candidate whose continuum appears quasar-like, likely at z<5.4, but whose Lyman break is absent and classification remains unconfirmed.

quotes.quote: We note that we observed a sixth object: J032615.68-061358.2. The continuum looks like a power law, however, we do not see a Lyman- break in our spectral range. This indicates that it likely is a quasar but at z<5.4 where the Lyman- line moves out of our spectral range. The predicted redshift of z=5.62 was too high. We do not consider it for our efficiency test here because we can not confirm the classification.

gmw_00013565.name: J152330.66+293539.1
gmw_00013565.overview: J152330.66+293539.1 is a newly discovered high-redshift quasar at a redshift of 5.73 [arXiv:2105.09171]. It exhibits a relatively blue color that would cause it to be missed by traditional color cut selections [arXiv:2105.09171].

sources.summary: The paper presents J152330.66+293539.1 as a newly discovered z=5.73 quasar with matching random-forest redshift, quasar-like J-band colors, an LBT/MODS spectrum, and colors that would fail common cuts.

quotes.quote: J152330.66+293539.1 is a newly discovered quasar at redshift 5.73 based on Lyman- emission. The predicted redshift was 5.72 and the high- z probability was 0.83. The object was part of our NOT photometric follow-up observations, where we measured the J-band magnitude. The obtained colors where J W 2 = 0.80 and z J = -0.32 in AB which are consistent with quasars at this redshift as seen in Fig. fig:lapalma . We observed this object with the MODS spectrograph on the LBT and present the spectrum in Fig. fig:quasar_spectra together with the other discovered quasars.

This object has a relatively blue color of i z =1.84 , so it would not be part of a typical color cut selection like Banados2016 where candidates were cut at i z >2 . This indicates that our method can find quasars that are missed by traditional color cuts even if our random forest is trained with objects largely from these selections.

gmw_00013566.name: J163752.18+024158.1
gmw_00013566.overview: J163752.18+024158.1 is a newly discovered high-redshift quasar [arXiv:2105.09171]. It has an observed redshift of 5.76, which was determined based on its Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:2105.09171].

sources.summary: The paper presents J163752.18+024158.1 as a newly discovered z=5.76 quasar whose random-forest redshift estimate closely matches the observed redshift, followed up with GoodmanHTS on SOAR.

quotes.quote: J163752.18+024158.1 is a newly discovered redshift 5.76 quasar based on the Lyman- emission. The redshift prediction in our high- z candidate catalog z=5.80 ( high redshift regression ) is very close to the observed redshift and the high- z probability was 0.57. We observed this object with the GoodmanHTS on the SOAR telescope.

gmw_00013567.name: J110942.97-285521.0
gmw_00013567.overview: J110942.97-285521.0 is a high-redshift quasar [arXiv:2105.09171]. Spectroscopic observations with the FORS2 instrument identified the object at a redshift of z=5.01, close to its predicted redshift of 5.09 [arXiv:2105.09171].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J110942.97-285521.0 as a z=5.01 quasar in the lower-redshift part of the final high-z candidate set, observed with FORS2 and predicted at z=5.09.

quotes.quote: Furthermore we were able to obtain follow-up observations for one additional object in the lower redshift range of our final high-z candidate set using FORS2. We identify the object, J110942.97-285521.0, as a quasar at z=5.01. The predicted redshift was 5.09.

gmw_00013568.name: J001150.03-244400.1
gmw_00013568.overview: J001150.03-244400.1 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of z=5.41 [arXiv:2105.09171]. Spectroscopic observations of the quasar reveal a strong absorption trough in its Lyman-alpha forest at observed wavelengths between 6300 and 6500 Angstroms [arXiv:2105.09171].

sources.summary: The paper reports J001150.03-244400.1 as a newly discovered z=5.41 quasar in the final high-z candidate set, observed with GoodmanHTS, with a lower random-forest redshift prediction and Lyman-forest absorption.

quotes.quote: We discovered J001150.03-244400.1 at redshift z=5.41 . While we selected and observed this object based on the preliminary selection described above, this quasar is also part of our final high- z candidate set . This quasar was observed with the GoodmanHTS spectrograph. We show the discovery spectrum in Fig. fig:quasar_spectra and list the object information in Table table:observedobjects .

The random forest regression predicted a redshift of z=5.16 , significantly lower than its real redshift. The high- z probability was 0.95. Interestingly, the spectrum shows a strong absorption through in the Lyman- forest at observed wavelengths of 6300-6500 .

gmw_00013569.name: J1447+1025
gmw_00013569.overview: J1447+1025 is an active galactic nucleus characterized as a faint and narrow-lined source [arXiv:1012.1871]. Within its observed sample, it stands out as an outlier with a comparatively high normalized accretion rate [arXiv:1012.1871].

sources.summary: The paper singles out J1447+1025 as an outlier in one measured distribution and describes it as a faint, narrow-lined source with comparatively high normalized accretion-rate behavior within the sample.

quotes.quote: Indeed, one of our sources (J1447+1025) has very similar properties, in terms of , \ and . However, this source looks as an outlier in the \ distribution.

For instance, the faint and narrow-lined source J1447+1025 is one of the higher \ sources in our sample. On the other hand, the hightest \ source (J0800+3051) has a similarly narrow \ line but is among the most luminous sources in the sample (a factor of 5.6 more luminous than J1447+1025), thus approaching 4 .

gmw_00013570.name: J 160624.72+363256.8
gmw_00013570.overview: J 160624.72+363256.8 is a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1611.07572]. According to Sloan Digital Sky Survey data, this active galaxy has a redshift of 0.281 [arXiv:1611.07572].

sources.summary: The paper used Seyfert galaxy J 160624.72+363256.8 as a test target, reporting its SDSS magnitude and redshift, target flux, reduced spectrum, and 30-minute signal-to-noise.

quotes.quote: During the last night of the set, June 21/22, we acquired the spectrum of the Seyfert galaxy J 160624.72+363256.8 (with the SDSS g band magnitude of 19 7 ), which has a redshift of 0.281 according SDSS data.

The flux from the target that got through the slit corresponded to an R band magnitude of about 20^ m . Figure  fig_spectra shows the direct image and reduced spectrum of the target. As is evident from the figure, an S/N ratio of about 6--7 can be achieved with a 30 min exposure, demonstrating the high efficiency of the spectrograph.

gmw_00013571.name: SDSS J161226.53+141917.8
gmw_00013571.overview: SDSS J161226.53+141917.8 is a faint host galaxy with an estimated redshift of approximately 0.091 [arXiv:1312.0012]. Based on its color and absolute magnitude, it is classified as a blue late-type galaxy that exhibits weak hydrogen-alpha emission [arXiv:1312.0012].

sources.summary: SDSS J161226.53+141917.8 is the faint host of iPTF13beo; the paper classifies it as a blue late-type galaxy and estimates z 0.091 from weak host H emission.

quotes.quote: The source was detected at =16^h12^m26.63^s , =+14^ 19'18.0'' J2000, 1.4 arcsec apart from the center of the faint ( u 20.9 mag, 19.5 mag) galaxy SDSS J161226.53+141917.8. This faint host galaxy was never classified morphologically before, however it can be classified as a blue late-type galaxy using its colour and absolute magnitude, following the definition.
The redshift z 0.091 was estimated from a weak narrow unresolved H emission, which seems to originate from the SN host galaxy.

gmw_00013572.name: host galaxy of the GRB
gmw_00013572.overview: The host galaxy of the GRB is a faint, extended object detected in deep R-band imaging [arXiv:astro-ph/0607601]. Late-time low-resolution spectroscopy of the galaxy yielded a very low signal-to-noise ratio, leaving it without a measured redshift [arXiv:astro-ph/0607601].

sources.summary: The paper reports a faint extended late-time source at the GRB 060108 afterglow position, likely host-galaxy light, with attempted spectroscopy too weak for a redshift and possible contamination of late optical flux.

quotes.quote: Deep R band imaging obtained 12 days post burst with the VLT reveals a faint, extended object ( R 23.5 mag) at the location of the afterglow. Although the brightness is compatible with the extrapolation of the slow decay with index _2 , significant flux is likely due to a host galaxy.

Late-time low-resolution spectra were acquired with the VLT in an attempt to measure the host galaxy redshift. However, the object was quite faint and spectra have a very low signal-to-noise ratio, with no clear emission or absorption lines.

Alternatively, if the R band observation ( R =23.5 mag taken at 12.67 days after the trigger) is attributed to emission from the host galaxy of the GRB, a break in the optical GRB light curve at t_ break,2 would not have been observed. As mentioned before, a significant host galaxy contamination is expected, since the source appears to be extended in the late VLT image.

gmw_00013573.name: NGC 6334B
gmw_00013573.overview: NGC 6334B is a radio source projected on the NGC 6334 region [arXiv:astro-ph/0512627]. It is classified as a background extragalactic active galactic nucleus candidate that exhibits hard X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0512627].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 6334B as a hard X-ray/radio source projected on NGC 6334, likely a background extragalactic AGN-like source contributing to the INTEGRAL excess.

quotes.quote: A probable scenario is emission from a heavily absorbed, compact and hard \ source that is associated with the AGN candidate radio source NGC 6334B.

We find that the observed \ flux may come from a bright point-like hard \ source located at (FK5: 17:20:21.8; -35:52:48) that is clearly seen in Fig. fig:image b. The source coincides with the known radio source NGC 6334B (FK5: 17:20:21.8; -35:52:48.2) studied by Moran and collaborators (see Moran (1990) and references therein). They suggested that NGC 6334B is a background extragalactic source.

The \ spectrum of the point-like source NGC 6334B extends up to 9 keV (see Fig. fig:chanSpec left panel) and is also well matched to the observed \ flux. Thus, more observations (e.g. with XMM-Newton ) are required to get a real constraint on the relative contribution of the HEE region and the AGN-like NGC 6334B source to the \ excess.

gmw_00013574.name: J013802+012424
gmw_00013574.overview: J013802+012424 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:1704.07445]. Its absorption features include a doublet indicating saturated absorption with an optical depth exceeding 1000, alongside a strong absorption wing that extends blueward [arXiv:1704.07445].

sources.summary: The paper uses J013802+012424 as an individual BAL-quasar example: its absorption is shallower than related lines, has a blueward wing, and forms a saturated doublet implying optical depth above 1000.

quotes.quote: Despite our bias towards finding only strong \ BALs (see Section sec:sample ), we observe that, in general, the \ absorption is not quite as deep as the corresponding \ and \ absorption (see, for example, J013802+012424 in Fig. fig:defspec and J151211+012807 in Fig. fig:probspec ).
There are also cases such as J013802+012424 where the \ BAL has a strong wing extending blueward, but this wing is less pronounced in \ and not seen in .

Finally, the \ absorption in J013802+012424 (Fig. fig:defspec ) is a doublet that appears to have the same depth in both components, indicating saturated absorption and optical depth > 3. This puts the optical depth of \ at > 1000 and gives very high values of the total column density in the outflow (see Section sec:discuss for further discussion).

gmw_00013575.name: host galaxy of GRB 160821B
gmw_00013575.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 160821B is a face-on, disturbed spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.162 [arXiv:1905.02159]. It exhibits active star formation and features a warped central bulge that suggests an ongoing merger [arXiv:1905.02159].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 160821B host at z=0.162, locating it near the afterglow and describing it as a face-on disturbed spiral with merger-like structure and active star formation.

quotes.quote: The position of the proposed host galaxy measured from our \ images is RA(J2000) = 18:39:53.968, Dec(J2000) = +62:23:34.35.
The resulting 2D and 1D extracted spectra are shown in Figure fig:whtspec , with emission lines of H , H , [ S 2 ] and [ O 3 ] providing a redshift of z=0.1616 0.0002 .

At a redshift z=0.162 the separation between afterglow and host corresponds to 16.4 kpc in projection, which is consistent with the offset distribution found for other sGRBs fong13a,tunnicliffe14 . Morphologically, the host appears to be a face-on, disturbed spiral galaxy (Figure fig:field ). The extended, warped appearance of the central bulge suggests an ongoing merger, and the nebular emission lines are consistent with active star formation.

gmw_00013576.name: GALEXASC J170959.67+820003.4
gmw_00013576.overview: GALEXASC J170959.67+820003.4 is a galaxy-shaped object located at an estimated distance of approximately 43 Mpc [arXiv:1809.04479]. It is the apparent host galaxy of the candidate supernova AT 2018we, also known as Gaia18alj [arXiv:1809.04479].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GALEXASC J170959.67+820003.4 as the galaxy-shaped apparent host near AT 2018we, notes that no host redshift was available, and estimates the host distance at about 43 Mpc from the supernova light curve.

quotes.quote: Based on this classification, we have also estimated the distance to its assumed host galaxy, which was found to be around 43 Mpc. AT 2018we, also known as Gaia18alj, is a candidate supernova discovered by the Gaia Photometric Alert system near the galaxy shaped object GALEXASC J170959.67+820003.4 on Feb 12, 2018, at coordinates =17h9m57s, =82 ^ 0'3'' (J2000) delgado2018 ; for the photometric alert page, see http://gsaweb.ast.cam.ac.uk/alerts/alert/Gaia18alj/.
We did not find any redshift published for its assumed host galaxy. Thus we have used the peak magnitude of the SN to estimate its distance.
We have also estimated the distance to its assumed host galaxy as 43 Mpc, assuming AT 2018we to be of type Ia. The host is thus apparently a relatively closeby galaxy.

gmw_00013577.name: ALMA source ID 45
gmw_00013577.overview: ALMA source ID 45 is a high-redshift galaxy with an estimated photometric redshift of 2.42 [arXiv:2309.10988]. It is identified as the longer-wavelength member of a blended galaxy pair detected in JWST imaging [arXiv:2309.10988].

sources.summary: The paper reports that ALMA source ID 45 is probably the longer-wavelength member of a blended JWST pair and assigns it a photometric redshift of 2.42 from long-wavelength photometry.

quotes.quote: At the position of our ALMA source ID 45 we found that there were two blended galaxies in the longer-wavelength JWST images, yet at shorter JWST wavelengths one of these galacxies was undetected. In the JADES catalogue there was only one ID for these two sources (ID 207277) that had a spectroscopic redshift of 0.332. This spectroscopic redshift likely corresponds to the galaxy brighter at short wavelengths, while our ALMA source is probably the second galaxy that is only detected at longer wavelengths. We therefore use the longer wavelength photometry (where this galaxy is detected) to fit an SED, which results in a photometric redshift of 2.42.

gmw_00013578.name: ALMA-HUDF-69
gmw_00013578.overview: ALMA-HUDF-69 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of 1.69 [arXiv:2309.10988]. Space telescope observations reveal that this source is a large, resolved spiral galaxy [arXiv:2309.10988].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ALMA-HUDF-69 with ASAGAO counterpart ID 44, describes the counterpart as a large resolved spiral at redshift 1.69, and measures its ALMA flux with a 2 arcsec aperture.

quotes.quote: Lastly, our source ALMA-HUDF-69 has a counterpart ID 44 in the ASAGAO catalogue, where it is reported that the peak pixel flux density is nearly 6 times fainter than the integrated flux density. The JWST / HST counterpart to this galaxy is a large resolved spiral at redshift 1.69, so it is likely that this galaxy is also highly resolved in our ALMA image. We find that a 2D Gaussian is a poor fit to the ALMA data, so we simply use an aperture with a radius of 2 arcsec to measure its flux density.

gmw_00013579.name: AGN J050550.35 675017.5
gmw_00013579.overview: AGN J050550.35 675017.5 is a candidate active galactic nucleus that has not been spectroscopically confirmed [arXiv:1305.6927]. The source is associated with a mildly edge-on galaxy that may host a bright nucleus [arXiv:1305.6927].

sources.summary: The paper says AGN J050550.35 675017.5 was not spectroscopically confirmed, though it was selected as an X-ray and mid-infrared candidate and appears associated with a mildly edge-on galaxy, making it likely an AGN.

quotes.quote: We were unable to confirm the AGN J050550.35 675017.5 from . They selected this as an X-ray source from higher resolution (than ROSAT) XMM-Newton data, while we selected it as a QSO-Aa mid-IR candidate. In the OGLE-III images it is associated with a 13 3 arcsec, mildly edge-on galaxy that may have a bright nucleus. As such, the source is almost certainly an AGN. However, assign a redshift of a z=0.07 quasar based on a single noisy line interpreted as H , and we are unable to confirm this redshift or identify an alternative.

gmw_00013580.name: source 6
gmw_00013580.overview: Source 6 is a galaxy that was previously assigned a photometric redshift of z=2.68 [arXiv:1905.11417]. However, observations of the source detected neither CO(1-0) emission nor any rest-frame optical emission lines, indicating that this photometric redshift is unreliable [arXiv:1905.11417].

sources.summary: The paper reports no CO(1-0) or rest-frame optical emission for source 6, concludes its z=2.68 photometric redshift is unreliable, and excludes it from further analysis.

quotes.quote: For source 6 we observed neither CO(1-0) emission, nor any rest-frame optical emission lines. Thus, we conclude that the photometric redshift of z=2.68 , on which we based both the VLA and MOSFIRE observations, is not reliable.
Thus, we exclude sources 2, 6, 9 and 14 (starred in Table tab:source_info ) from further analysis.

gmw_00013581.name: source 9
gmw_00013581.overview: Source 9 is an astronomical object that was targeted for Very Large Array observations to detect its CO(1-0) emission line [arXiv:1905.11417]. However, because of an incorrect cataloged redshift, the expected line fell at the edge of the observed frequency band where spectral noise was highest, leading to its exclusion from further analysis [arXiv:1905.11417].

sources.summary: The paper says source 9 had an incorrect catalogue redshift; the expected CO(1-0) line fell at a noisy band edge, so it was excluded from further analysis.

quotes.quote: Because we relied upon the catalogued redshifts when taking our VLA data, the observed frequency intervals for sources 2 and 9 are not optimised for the correct redshift (see note below Table tab:source_info ). Thus, the frequency range of the observations for source 2 does not encompass the CO(1-0) line, whereas for source 9, the expected position of CO(1-0) falls at the edge of the observed frequencies, where the spectral noise is greatest.
Thus, we exclude sources 2, 6, 9 and 14 (starred in Table tab:source_info ) from further analysis.

gmw_00013582.name: source 12
gmw_00013582.overview: Source 12 is a galaxy with a reliable spectroscopic redshift that lacks a CO(1-0) detection [arXiv:1905.11417]. It is an outlier in predicted CO luminosity, which may indicate that its dust-to-gas ratio is greater than expected [arXiv:1905.11417].

sources.summary: The paper gives source 12 a CO(1-0) upper limit despite a reliable spectroscopic redshift, notes CO should have been detectable, and flags it as the main luminosity-conversion outlier.

quotes.quote: For the two sources without CO(1-0) detections but with reliable spectroscopic redshifts (sources 12 and 15) we estimate upper limits on the CO(1-0) emission based on the moment zero maps, centred on the expected frequency of the CO(1-0) line.

However, the same is not true for source 12, for which we would expect to detect CO based on the predicted CO luminosity and noise limit. We can only speculate that one of the assumptions applied in the RJ method does not apply to this source, e.g. that the dust-to-gas ratio is greater than expected (see Section sub:single_band_method for the assumptions).

The values of derived from both the single-band fluxes and SEDs are mostly consistent with the predictions from the to- \ conversion factors derived by (solid and dotted lines in Figure fig:lum_comp ), with all but source 12, consistent to within a factor of two.

gmw_00013583.name: source 15
gmw_00013583.overview: Source 15 is a galaxy with a reliable spectroscopic redshift [arXiv:1905.11417]. It was not detected in CO(1-0) emission, likely because the data was too noisy to detect the predicted signal [arXiv:1905.11417].

sources.summary: The paper treats source 15 as a reliable-redshift non-detection, estimates a CO(1-0) upper limit, attributes the non-detection to noise, and notes a large MAGPHYS stellar-mass difference.

quotes.quote: For the two sources without CO(1-0) detections but with reliable spectroscopic redshifts (sources 12 and 15) we estimate upper limits on the CO(1-0) emission based on the moment zero maps, centred on the expected frequency of the CO(1-0) line.
It is likely that source 15 is not detected because it is just below the detection limit, i.e. the data here were too noisy to detect the predicted signal.

The stellar masses derived via MAGPHYS and LePhare are consistent within a factor of two (with no systematic offset) for all but source 15, for which the MAGPHYS derived \ is a factor of five greater (we trust our SED fit in Figure fig:SEDs ).

gmw_00013584.name: source 16
gmw_00013584.overview: The astronomical object source 16 exhibits resolved structure in its dust continuum emission [arXiv:1905.11417]. This dust continuum emission is modeled using two elliptical Gaussian components [arXiv:1905.11417].

sources.summary: The paper says source 16 required native-resolution spectra, shows resolved dust-continuum structure fit with two Gaussians, has the sample's lowest continuum S/N, and used externally supplied Herschel data.

quotes.quote: For sources 8 and 16 it was necessary to use the native resolution ( 16 ) to extract the spectra (see Figure fig:spectra ).

Thus, unlike for the CO(1-0) data, some of our sources (particularly source 16) show resolved structure in the dust continuum emission. We therefore use CASA's imfit to fit elliptical Gaussians to the source emission, focussing on the region of interest within 5'' of the source. We fit two Gaussians to the emission of source 16 but fit all other sources with a single Gaussian element. We use the flux error returned by imfit as the uncertainty on our derived fluxes. The S/N of the continuum fluxes varies from 4 (Source 16) to 29 (Source 1).
For source 16, no Herschel data is available in the catalogue. Instead, we use the Herschel data described in provided via private correspondence .

gmw_00013585.name: J0530+0042
gmw_00013585.overview: J0530+0042 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=0.7 [arXiv:2209.09342]. It exhibits a high Gaia excess statistic, and imaging reveals a faint neighboring source to the northeast with no convincing evidence of a foreground lens [arXiv:2209.09342].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0530+0042 as a z=0.7 quasar with the strongest high-redshift Gaia excess statistic; DECam imaging reveals a nearby faint source but no convincing foreground lens.

quotes.quote: Above z=0.5 , only 7 (5 of 73 quasars) have C^ * / >2 , and 1 (1 quasar, J0530+0042 at z=0.7 ) has C^ * / >3 . The latter has DECam imaging (NOIRLab Prop. ID 2014B-0193; PI: F. Walter), which shows a faint ( 20.5 ABmag) source, 3.5 arcsec to the Northeast, which is not catalogued by Gaia DR3, DELVE DR2, or NSC DR2. As this neighbour is beyond the 3.5-arcsec-wide B_ P /R_ P aperture, the relation to the high C^ * value is unclear, but we conclude that there is no convincing evidence for a foreground lens.

gmw_00013586.name: J003856.98-292224.3
gmw_00013586.overview: J003856.98-292224.3 is a likely iron low-ionization broad absorption line quasar located at a redshift of approximately 2.27 [arXiv:1905.04069]. The object exhibits strong nitrogen emission alongside broad absorption line troughs in several ions, including iron [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J003856.98-292224.3 as a likely FeLoBAL quasar at about z 2.27, with strong nitrogen emission and BAL troughs in several ions including Fe 2.

quotes.quote: J003856.98-292224.3 This quasar is likely a FeLoBAL at z 2.27 0.005 with strong nitrogen emission. The peak at 4150 is N 5 in the observed frame and the narrower line at 5750 is N 3 ] at 1750 rest-frame. BAL troughs are seen in N 5 , C 2 , Si 4 , C 4 , Al 2 , Al 3 and Fe 2 .

gmw_00013587.name: J021119.80-195943.0
gmw_00013587.overview: J021119.80-195943.0 is a iron low-ionization broad absorption line quasar located at a redshift of approximately 2.45 [arXiv:1905.04069]. The object exhibits unusually strong N III] emission and has absorption trough red edges near a redshift of 2.365 [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J021119.80-195943.0 as a FeLoBAL at z=2.45, noting uncertain emission redshift, unusually strong N 3] emission, and trough red edges near z=2.365.

quotes.quote: J021119.80-195943.0 is a FeLoBAL at z=2.45 0.02 . The emission redshift is uncertain, because even the 1900 complex is affected by absorption. It also displays unusually strong N 3 ] 1750 emission. The red edges of the troughs are around z=2.365 .

gmw_00013588.name: J044756.84-230748.3
gmw_00013588.overview: J044756.84-230748.3 is a quasar with a redshift of at least 2.945 [arXiv:1905.04069]. It displays weak broad carbon IV and 1900 emission, alongside low-ionization and mini broad-absorption line systems [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper places J044756.84-230748.3 at z at least 2.945, describing weak C 4 and 1900 emission plus LoBAL and mini-BAL absorption systems.

quotes.quote: J044756.84-230748.3 Based on multiple absorption lines arising from a near damped Ly absorber this quasar has a redshift of at least z=2.945 0.005 . It displays weak broad C 4 emission from 5700-6300 and weak broad 1900 emission around 7100-7600 . It has a LoBAL with Si 4 , C 4 , and Al 3 at z=2.82 as well as a mini-BAL in N 5 , Si 4 , C 4 at z=2.745 .

gmw_00013589.name: J113252.86-063243.3
gmw_00013589.overview: J113252.86-063243.3 is a quasar with an estimated redshift of z=2.41, identified primarily from its 1900 emission complex [arXiv:1905.04069]. It displays low-ionization broad absorption line features at z=2.27, alongside narrower absorption troughs at higher redshifts [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper estimates J113252.86-063243.3 at z=2.41 from the 1900 emission complex, with LoBAL absorption at z=2.27 and narrower troughs at several higher redshifts.

quotes.quote: J113252.86-063243.3 is likely at z=2.41 0.01 mainly identified from the 1900 emission complex. It displays a LoBAL ( Si 4 , C 4 , Al 2 , Al 3 ) at z=2.27 . It further has narrower absorption troughs ( Si 4 , C 4 , Al 2 , Al 3 ) at z=2.319 , z=2.333 , and z=2.363 .

gmw_00013590.name: J191946.08+743747.1
gmw_00013590.overview: J191946.08+743747.1 is a broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=1.604 [arXiv:1905.04069]. It is classified as a iron low-ionization broad-absorption line quasar that also exhibits narrow absorption features at z=1.5915 [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J191946.08+743747.1 as a BAL quasar at z=1.604, showing FeLoBAL absorption and additional narrow absorption near z=1.5915.

quotes.quote: J191946.08+743747.1 This BAL quasar is at z=1.604 0.001 identified by the narrow Mg 2 emission. It shows a FeLoBAL with Al 2 , Al 3 , Fe 3 , Fe 2 and Mg 2 absorption. The red end of the troughs is around z=1.55 . It further displays narrow absorption in Al 2 , Al 3 , Fe 2 and Mg 2 at z=1.5915 0.0005 .

gmw_00013591.name: J220912.01+061920.1
gmw_00013591.overview: J220912.01+061920.1 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=1.91 [arXiv:1905.04069]. It is classified as a iron low-ionization broad absorption line quasar, displaying broad absorption troughs down to z=1.76 alongside narrower absorption features near z=1.893 [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J220912.01+061920.1 as a z=1.91 quasar, describing FeLoBAL troughs down to z around 1.76 and narrower absorption near z=1.893.

quotes.quote: J220912.01+061920.1 This quasar is at z=1.91 0.01 identified by the narrow C 4 and Al 3 lines. It displays a FeLoBAL ( Si 4 , C 4 , Al 2 , Al 3 , Fe 3 , Mg 2 ), where the blue end of the troughs is around z=1.76 . It further has narrower Si 4 , C 4 , Al 2 , Al 3 , Fe 2 absorption around z=1.893 .

gmw_00013592.name: J124615.10+713923.6
gmw_00013592.overview: J124615.10+713923.6 is a newly discovered quasar located at a redshift of z=3.995 [arXiv:1905.04069]. It has a possible, though uncertain, X-ray counterpart in the ROSAT 2RXS catalog [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper reports J124615.10+713923.6 as a newly discovered z=3.995 quasar with a possible ROSAT 2RXS counterpart, but cautions that the X-ray association is uncertain.

quotes.quote: Of the newly discovered quasars one, J124615.10+713923.6 at z=3.995 has an X-ray counterpart in ROSAT 2RXS with f_ 0.1-2.4 keV = 2.26 10^ 13 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 at a distance of 62 7 . As the match distance implies this AllWISE source is not the most probable match to the X-ray source ( TRXS match flag =2 ) with a p_i 0.24 relative probability to be the correct counterpart to the X-ray source among all possible AllWISE candidates. While we wanted to report this here for completeness, we would also like to caution to blindly associate the X-ray flux with the quasar.

gmw_00013593.name: J095947.52-103437.7
gmw_00013593.overview: J095947.52-103437.7 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=3.165 [arXiv:1905.04069]. It has a reliable X-ray counterpart detected in both the soft and total bands [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper reports J095947.52-103437.7 as a new z=3.165 PS-ELQS quasar with a reliable XMMSL2 counterpart and soft- and total-band X-ray fluxes.

quotes.quote: Another new PS-ELQS quasar, J095947.52-103437.7 ( z=3.165 ), has a counterpart in XMMSL2.

In both cases the AllWISE counterparts constitute the best match to the X-ray source and seem reliable. The counterpart to J095947.52-103437.7 has a 0.24 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 detection in the soft band and a flux of 0.67 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 in the total band.

gmw_00013594.name: J171721.32+422428.3
gmw_00013594.overview: J171721.32+422428.3 is a quasar at a redshift of z=3.495 [arXiv:1905.04069]. It is associated with a reliable X-ray counterpart detected in both the soft and total X-ray bands [arXiv:1905.04069].

sources.summary: The paper notes J171721.32+422428.3 as a z=3.495 quasar from ELQS pilot observations with a reliable XMMSL2 counterpart detected in soft and total X-ray bands.

quotes.quote: We further want to report one a XMMSL2 counterpart for the quasar J171721.32+422428.3 ( z=3.495 ). The latter quasar was discovered during pilot observations of the ELQS Schindler2018 , but not selected to be in the ELQS quasar catalog.

In both cases the AllWISE counterparts constitute the best match to the X-ray source and seem reliable. The counterpart to J095947.52-103437.7 has a 0.24 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 detection in the soft band and a flux of 0.67 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 in the total band. The X-ray counterpart to J171721.32+422428.3 was detected in the soft band with 0.94 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and in the total band with 2.42 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

gmw_00013595.name: Galaxy 119
aliases_external: Galaxy 119 / S09 object 119 / SDSS DR7 ObjID 587735742615388296
gmw_00013595.overview: Galaxy 119 is a blue early-type galaxy that is gravitationally interacting with a blue companion galaxy [arXiv:1510.01931]. Both galaxies exhibit enhanced near-ultraviolet emission, which suggests a burst of intense star formation potentially fueled by an exchange of molecular gas [arXiv:1510.01931].

sources.summary: The paper presents Galaxy 119 as a blue early-type galaxy interacting with a blue companion, with near-UV enhancement suggesting star formation and possible molecular-gas exchange despite no detected near-UV trail.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 119 and the neighbouring galaxy are found to have enhanced emission in near-UV when compared to optical data. This could be due to the sudden burst of intense star formation due to the gravitational interaction between galaxies. We could not detect an interacting trail between the galaxies in the near UV data. However, there can be an exchange of molecular gas (fueling star formation) between the galaxies, which is only detected from radio or sub-mm observations.

Figure 2a shows Galaxy 119 with a blue companion that appears to be disrupted in morphology owing to the blue ETG. The companion galaxy is having ongoing star formation as is evident from the near-UV image (Figure 3a).

gmw_00013596.name: Galaxy 180
aliases_external: Galaxy 180 / S09 object 180 / SDSS DR7 ObjID 588011125186691149
gmw_00013596.overview: Galaxy 180 is an interacting blue early-type galaxy with a disrupted spiral companion [arXiv:1510.01931]. The system features tidal trails connecting the galaxies and exhibits a very high star formation rate of 18 solar masses per year [arXiv:1510.01931].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Galaxy 180 as an interacting blue early-type galaxy with a disrupted spiral companion, tidal trails, near-UV star formation, stripped spiral arms, and a very high star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: Galaxy 180, on the other hand, has interaction trails with the companion, and the blobs of enhanced emission are clearly visible in the near-UV images. This could be due to triggered star formation episodes happening within the interacting trails of the galaxies, or we are witnessing the burst of ongoing star formation in the arms of the spiral galaxy that is being stripped by the blue ETG.

Figure 2b shows Galaxy 180, which hosts a spiral companion with disrupted morphology. The system is interacting, and the blue ETG have a very high star formation rate of 18 M _ /yr. The spiral companion is being stripped of its spiral arms with tidal trails connecting the galaxies, which is the site of ongoing star formation, as seen in the near-UV image (Figure 3b).

gmw_00013597.name: HAWKI0003606
gmw_00013597.overview: HAWKI0003606 is a candidate low-luminosity star-forming galaxy with a very high equivalent width [arXiv:1604.04632]. It is either a very low-luminosity line emitter with no obvious continuum or an artifact, as it does not appear on CANDELS images [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: The paper treats HAWKI0003606 as a possible very low-luminosity line emitter or artifact, noting no CANDELS continuum, high equivalent width, and unresolved future classification.

quotes.quote: HAWKI0003606 does not appear on the CANDELS images, thus, it is an artefact or a very low-luminosity line emitter with no obvious continuum (apparent line flux from the NB image was 4.5 10^ 18 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 ). This last object is an extremely interesting candidate low-luminosity SFG with a very high EW. A more detailed analysis in the future will be needed to reveal its nature.

gmw_00013598.name: CANDELS 12039
gmw_00013598.overview: CANDELS 12039 is a low-mass galaxy characterized by blue colors and a weak or absent 4000 Å break feature [arXiv:1604.04632]. Its photometry beyond the F160W band is close to detection limits, and its extinction has been calculated using synthetic FUV and NUV magnitudes [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: The paper treats CANDELS 12039 as a discrepant CANDELS/3D-HST source with blue colors, low mass, weak 4000 break, difficult photometric redshift, and extinction derived from FUV/NUV.

quotes.quote: Comparing them with the CANDELS data we did not find deviations out of the uncertainties except for two objects: CANDELS 12039 = 3D-HST 23397 and CANDELS 18444 = 3D-HST 3408. We decided to keep the CANDELS values except in the two discrepant cases for which the 3D-HST values appeared more suitable to the colours and morphologies these galaxies show in their postage stamps. Actually, these two systems are characterised by blue colours and low masses, properties that make the photo- z estimation more difficult. In particular, they show none or a small 4000 break feature and the photometry beyond the F160W band is close to detection limits.
In the case of CANDELS 12039 the synthetic TIR luminosity was not available and we calculated the extinction employing FUV and NUV synthetic magnitudes from the Rainbow Database.
Once we have the IRX ratio from Eq. eq:tirfuv , we can substitute it in Eq. eq:fuv_ext and apply a Calzetti law (Eq. eq:ha_ext ) to obtain the extinction for this object.

gmw_00013599.name: CANDELS 18444
gmw_00013599.overview: CANDELS 18444 is a high-redshift galaxy with a photometric redshift greater than 2.5 [arXiv:1604.04632]. It is characterized by a low mass, blue colors, and a small or absent 4000 Å break feature [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: CANDELS 18444 is a discrepant CANDELS/3D-HST source whose 3D-HST values better matched its blue, low-mass morphology; the paper also lists it above photometric redshift 2.5.

quotes.quote: Comparing them with the CANDELS data we did not find deviations out of the uncertainties except for two objects: CANDELS 12039 = 3D-HST 23397 and CANDELS 18444 = 3D-HST 3408. We decided to keep the CANDELS values except in the two discrepant cases for which the 3D-HST values appeared more suitable to the colours and morphologies these galaxies show in their postage stamps. Actually, these two systems are characterised by blue colours and low masses, properties that make the photo- z estimation more difficult. In particular, they show none or a small 4000 break feature and the photometry beyond the F160W band is close to detection limits.
Finally, two objects (5 ) have photometric redshift z>2.5 (CANDELS 11513 and 18444).

gmw_00013600.name: CANDELS 20901
gmw_00013600.overview: CANDELS 20901 is a galaxy detected in the 4 Ms Chandra X-ray catalogs [arXiv:1604.04632]. However, neither these catalogs nor available spectroscopic data classify the object as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: CANDELS 20901 is the only sample object detected in the 4 Ms Chandra catalogs, but the paper says neither those catalogs nor available spectra classify it as an AGN.

quotes.quote: We only found X-ray detection for the object CANDELS 20901, with absorption-corrected rest-frame 0.5--8 keV luminosity of 3.8 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 . This object is not classified as an AGN by the 4 Ms catalogues. Furthermore, the spectroscopic data available does not indicate an AGN contribution in this galaxy (see Fig. fig:spectra and Sec. sec:spec ).

gmw_00013601.name: CANDELS 17503
gmw_00013601.overview: CANDELS 17503 is a galaxy classified morphologically as unclassifiable [arXiv:1604.04632]. It is characterized by exceptionally low mass, low luminosity, and a low star formation rate, while exhibiting high extinction [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: CANDELS 17503 is marked Unclassifiable and, with 31370, is among the faintest objects, with the lowest masses, luminosities, and star formation rates but high extinction.

quotes.quote: Moreover, the Unclassifiable CANDELS 17503 and 31370 are the two faintest objects, as is expected from their morphological category.

In addition to the two main classes, we find two galaxies marked as Unclassifiable (CANDELS 17503 and 31370). These are very interesting objects as they show the lowest masses, luminosities, and SFRs with the highest extinctions.

gmw_00013602.overview: The galaxy 31370 is categorized as morphologically unclassifiable [arXiv:1604.04632]. It is characterized by exceptionally low mass, luminosity, and star formation rate, while exhibiting high extinction [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: 31370 is marked Unclassifiable and, with CANDELS 17503, is among the faintest objects, with the lowest masses, luminosities, and star formation rates but high extinction.

quotes.quote: Moreover, the Unclassifiable CANDELS 17503 and 31370 are the two faintest objects, as is expected from their morphological category.

In addition to the two main classes, we find two galaxies marked as Unclassifiable (CANDELS 17503 and 31370). These are very interesting objects as they show the lowest masses, luminosities, and SFRs with the highest extinctions.

gmw_00013603.name: CANDELS 15784
gmw_00013603.overview: CANDELS 15784 is classified as an irregular galaxy with a low axis ratio of 0.34 [arXiv:1604.04632]. It has a Sersic index of 2 to 3, and its shape may be disrupted by star formation or a companion [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: The paper contrasts CANDELS 15784's Sersic index with most sample galaxies and describes the irregular object as low-axis-ratio, possibly distorted by star formation or a companion.

quotes.quote: There is a clear contrast between the galaxies CANDELS 15784, 16155, and 19256, which have S e rsic indexes of n 2--3, and the rest, which have n<1.5 .

The Irregular CANDELS 15784 just presents a difference in terms of the axis ratio ( b/a=0.34 0.02 ), compared to Compact(Spheroid) morphologies ( b/a>0.6 ). It could be the star formation or its companion that is disrupting its shape.

gmw_00013604.name: CANDELS 23137
gmw_00013604.overview: CANDELS 23137 is an irregular merger galaxy with structural properties related to a disk morphology [arXiv:1604.04632]. It is characterized as an H II-like galaxy with high excitation, a featureless continuum, and strong emission lines [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: The paper describes CANDELS 23137 as an irregular merger with disk-like structure and as an H II-like example with prominent emission lines, high excitation, and high [O II] equivalent width.

quotes.quote: The last Irregular CANDELS 23137 is a Merger with structural properties more related to Disk morphology, ( M_B=-19.42 , r_ e =0.52 ).

The best example of this class is CANDELS 23137, with prominent emission lines and featureless continuum. Its excitation is high ( ( [ O iii ] /H ) =0.71 ), as is the EW_ rf ( [ O ii ] )=205.3 , which is above the value of the low-mass galaxy 24623.

gmw_00013605.name: CANDELS 24623
gmw_00013605.overview: CANDELS 24623 is a low-mass, low-luminosity star-forming galaxy characterized by a compact morphology and a flat continuum [arXiv:1604.04632]. The galaxy exhibits a strong [O II] emission line with a high equivalent width and is identified as a strong candidate for low metallicity [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: CANDELS 24623 is the only galaxy below log mass 8.5, with prominent [O II], flat continuum, compact morphology, high equivalent width, and the paper's best low-metallicity candidacy.

quotes.quote: In terms of the mass distinction, we find only one galaxy with (M/M_ ) <8.5 , namely CANDELS 24623. This object has a mass (M/M_ ) =7.91 0.08 , which is one of the lowest values in our sample. It is a clear star-forming object with a very prominent [ O ii ] 3727 line and a very flat continuum (see Fig. fig:spectra ). Its Spheroid(Compact) morphology and its high EW_ rf ( [ O ii ] )=96.8 indicate that it could be completely dominated by the starburst. This low-mass, low-luminosity ( M_B=-16.98 ) galaxy is our best low metallicity candidate.

gmw_00013606.name: CANDELS 21046
gmw_00013606.overview: CANDELS 21046 is an emission line galaxy characterized by unresolved emission lines [arXiv:1604.04632]. It exhibits a low excitation state, which is a signature of an older burst in star formation [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: The paper flags CANDELS 21046's excitation as unexpectedly high because sky subtraction affected H-alpha, reports unresolved line widths, and links low excitation to an older starburst.

quotes.quote: CANDELS 21046 has an excitation value that does not correspond to its spectrum. The value that we get is higher than expected. With a detailed examination of its spectrum in Fig. fig:spectra we discovered that a bad sky subtraction has eliminated a pixel in the H line. The FWHM(H )=11.4 is below FWHM( [ O iii ] 5007)=28.3 , which corresponds to the instrument profile width because the emission lines are not resolved. The estimated real value is indicated with an arrow in Fig. fig:diag_el .
CANDELS 21046 and 13781 have the lowest excitation in this category, a signature of an older burst in star formation.

gmw_00013607.name: CANDELS 22420
gmw_00013607.overview: CANDELS 22420 is a disk-like galaxy characterized by a redder disk morphology [arXiv:1604.04632]. Its spectrum exhibits lower excitation with H-alpha stronger than [O III], a clear Balmer break, and absorption lines that indicate an older stellar population [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: CANDELS 22420 is one of the best disk-like cases, with lower excitation, H-alpha stronger than [O III], a clear Balmer break, absorption lines, and redder disk morphology.

quotes.quote: CANDELS 22420 and 23248 are the best cases in this Disk-like category. Their spectra show lower excitation with H > [ O iii ] . In addition, a clear Balmer break appears with absorption lines, indicative of an older stellar population. All of these features are well connected with Disk morphologies and redder postage stamps.

gmw_00013608.overview: The galaxy 23248 is characterized by a disk-like morphology and a redder appearance [arXiv:1604.04632]. Its spectrum exhibits a clear Balmer break with absorption lines, indicating an older stellar population, alongside lower excitation where H-alpha is stronger than [O III] [arXiv:1604.04632].

sources.summary: 23248 is one of the best disk-like cases, with lower excitation, H-alpha stronger than [O III], a clear Balmer break, absorption lines, and redder disk morphology.

quotes.quote: CANDELS 22420 and 23248 are the best cases in this Disk-like category. Their spectra show lower excitation with H > [ O iii ] . In addition, a clear Balmer break appears with absorption lines, indicative of an older stellar population. All of these features are well connected with Disk morphologies and redder postage stamps.

gmw_00013609.name: ID 5
gmw_00013609.overview: ID 5 is a starburst galaxy that exhibits He II 1640 and C III ] 1909 emission lines in its ultraviolet spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0308456]. It is detected as a faint soft X-ray and radio source, indicating a high extinction-corrected star formation rate with no clear signs of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0308456].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID 5 as a starburst with UV emission lines, the sample's highest extinction-corrected SFR, soft X-ray and radio detections, and little evidence for an AGN.

quotes.quote: One galaxy (ID 5) shows HeII 1640 and CIII ] 1909 emission lines.

Interestingly, ID 5 (which has the highest extinction-corrected SFR ) is a faint (soft) X-ray source (XID563 in the Chandra Deep Field South catalog, Giacconi et al. 2002). If completely due to star formation, its X-ray luminosity L_ 2-10 keV ^ rest 2.7 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 corresponds to SFR 500 M _ yr ^ 1 (Nandra et al. 2002). The object is also a faint 1.4 Ghz radio source with a flux density of 103 13 Jy (Kellermann et al. 2003, private comunication), fully consistent with the tight X-ray--radio luminosities correlation shown by Ranalli et al. (2003) for actively star-forming galaxies. We cannot definitively rule out the presence of an obscured AGN, but the lack of AGN signatures in its UV spectrum, showing instead a strong SiIII 1294 photospheric absorption line, the non detection in the Chandra hard band and the low X-ray to optical flux ratio ( log(f_ 0.5-2 kev /f_R) 1.3 ) indicate this is a vigorous star-forming galaxy.

gmw_00013610.name: putative GRB host galaxy
gmw_00013610.overview: The putative GRB host galaxy is located at coordinates R.A. 21h 31m 22.68s, Dec. +00d 42' 28.8" [arXiv:0808.0151]. It has an absolute R-band magnitude of approximately -21.53, making it slightly more luminous than characteristic host galaxies at its redshift [arXiv:0808.0151].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a galaxy coincident with GRB 080514B's optical transient, gives its coordinates and 2.6 kpc offset, and estimates an R-band luminosity slightly above characteristic host galaxies.

quotes.quote: A galaxy underlying the position of the optical transient is detected in all GROND optical bands at 8.9 days, as well as in the deep Keck g and R band images obtained 24.13 days post-burst. Using the stacked GROND g''i'z' images, the coordinates of this galaxy are R.A., Dec. (J2000) = 21^ h 31^ m 22.^ s 68 , +00^ 42'28 8 , which is 0 3 0 2 off from the position of the optical afterglow. Assuming a cosmological model with H_0=71 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _ M = 0.27, _ =0.73 (Spergel et al. ), for a redshift of 1.8 the offset of the optical transient from the center of its likely host galaxy is 2.6 1.7 kpc.

Assuming a power-law spectrum for the putative host galaxy of the form F_ ^ _ gal , its absolute R_C band magnitude is M_R = m_R - k , where =45.70 mag is the distance modulus and k is the cosmological k correction, k= -2.5 (1- _ gal ) (1+z ). Hence, M_R = -21.53 + 1.12 (1- _ gal ) 0.3 , which for _ gal =0.45, as it follows from the third epoch GROND g''i'z' data, makes this galaxy approximately 0.5 mag more luminous than the characteristic magnitude of the Schechter function describing the band luminosity function of galaxies in the Las Campanas redshift survey (Lin et al. 1996). Its R band magnitude matches well into the distribution of host magnitudes of long bursts at this redshift (Guziy et al. 2005; Savaglio et al. 2008).

gmw_00013611.name: b02
gmw_00013611.overview: The object b02 is a broad-absorption line quasar with an adopted redshift of 2.64 [arXiv:2404.07726]. Although it shows no carbon IV absorption, it displays potential absorption in aluminum III and magnesium II, indicating characteristics of a low-ionization broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:2404.07726].

sources.summary: The paper treats b02 as a problematic BAL-quasar source: it adopts z=2.64, finds no C IV absorption, notes possible Al III/Mg II BALs, and lacks GMRT data because of RFI.

quotes.quote: Subsequently, we identified a discrepancy in the redshift of b02 (100424.88 + 122922.2; z=2.64 ) reported by and , with the latter presenting a value of z=4.66 . Further analysis, based on a broader SDSS spectrum obtained during a different epoch 2020ApJS..250....8L , suggested a redshift closer to that of 2007AJ....134..102S , which we ultimately adopted.
