Assuming the redshift of z=2.64 , we detected no C 4 absorption from this source; hence, the BI and BI _0 presented in Table tbl:BALsample should be 0. However, the source displayed potential BAL in Al 3 and Mg 2 , indicating characteristics of a LoBAL quasar. Despite this issue, we could not acquire GMRT observational data for this source due to radio frequency interference (RFI).

gmw_00013612.name: n24
gmw_00013612.overview: The object n24 is a source that displays an aluminum III broad absorption line [arXiv:2404.07726]. It has also been identified as a candidate for radio-flux variability [arXiv:2404.07726].

sources.summary: The paper flags n24 as an anomalous non-BAL-sample source with an Al III BAL and significant radio-flux variability, so analyses include and exclude it.

quotes.quote: We note, however, that we inadvertently incorporated a source, n24 (J141031.00 + 614136.9), that displays a BAL in Al 3 at 1857.4 in our sample. We will present both analyses, incorporating and excluding this object.

We have identified two candidates, b04 (J104452.41 + 104005.9) and n24 (J141031.00 + 614136.9), representing significant variability of V > 3 , with V = 3.57 and 3.52, respectively. While we acknowledge that only two epochs of observations may not be sufficient to provide conclusive evidence regarding the stability or variability of the targets, we will present both analyses, encompassing and omitting these candidates for variable sources.

gmw_00013613.name: DEEP2 04
gmw_00013613.overview: DEEP2 04 is a very bright galaxy [arXiv:1910.06975]. It lacks optical measurements in the DEEP2 catalogs, which limits its photometric dataset and prevents well-constrained spectral energy distribution modeling [arXiv:1910.06975].

sources.summary: The paper excludes DEEP2 04 from its SED results because its photometric dataset is poorly constrained; it describes the galaxy as very bright and lacking optical measurements in DEEP2 catalogs.

quotes.quote: We note that the SED results for DEEP2 04 and 14 are not used. The SED models for these galaxies are not well constrained due to their limited photometric dataset. DEEP2 04 is a very bright galaxy that its optical measurements are not available in the DEEP2 catalogs.

gmw_00013614.name: DEEP2 14
gmw_00013614.overview: DEEP2 14 is a galaxy with a limited photometric dataset consisting of only three bands, specifically B, R, and I [arXiv:1910.06975]. The galaxy was not detected by Spitzer/IRAC, leaving it with only an upper limit at 3.6 microns [arXiv:1910.06975].

sources.summary: The paper excludes DEEP2 14 from SED results because Spitzer/IRAC did not detect it, leaving only BRI bands and a 3.6 micron upper limit that produces a poor fit.

quotes.quote: We note that the SED results for DEEP2 04 and 14 are not used. The SED models for these galaxies are not well constrained due to their limited photometric dataset.

DEEP2 14 was not detected by Spitzer /IRAC, thus limiting its photometric dataset to three bands ( BRI ) and an upper limit at 3.6 m. The upper limit is far below the BRI photometry, causing a poor fit ( ^2_ = 5.7 ).

gmw_00013615.name: SDSS J030907.49+002419.0
gmw_00013615.overview: SDSS J030907.49+002419.0 is a quasar located at a redshift of z = 2.083 [arXiv:1407.3025]. The object exhibits a mild positive correlation between its spectral index and continuum, with a possible but uncertain contribution from its host galaxy [arXiv:1407.3025].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J030907.49+002419.0 as a quasar with mild positive spectral-index/continuum correlation and a seeing correlation that leaves host contribution possible but uncertain.

quotes.quote: In contrast, none of sources have significant positive correlations, and only three quasars have mild positive correlations (SDSS J022518.36-001332.3 at z = 3.63 , SDSS J030907.49+002419.0 at z = 2.08 , and SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 at z = 0.65 , see Tables table:2 table:3 ).

We checked the correlations between the spectral index and seeing for three RWB sources with mild correlations. With available seeing, we indeed found correlations at similar confidence level as RWB correlations in two sources, SDSS J030907.49+002419.0 ( z=2.083 ), and SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 ( z=0.656 ). Therefore, the effects of host galaxy contribution cannot be completely ruled out, although it is hard to quantitatively evaluate this possibility, and the contribution of host galaxy may not be significant at rest frame wavelength for these two objects.

gmw_00013616.name: SDSS J031022.10+004130.0
gmw_00013616.overview: SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 is a quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.656 [arXiv:1407.3025]. It is classified as a redder-when-brighter source that exhibits mild positive color variability [arXiv:1407.3025].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 as an RWB quasar with mild positive colour variability and a seeing correlation that makes host contribution possible but uncertain.

quotes.quote: In contrast, none of sources have significant positive correlations, and only three quasars have mild positive correlations (SDSS J022518.36-001332.3 at z = 3.63 , SDSS J030907.49+002419.0 at z = 2.08 , and SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 at z = 0.65 , see Tables table:2 table:3 ). The correlations are illustrated in Fig. fig:indexgood with two examples, SDSS J030639.57+000343.1 (BWB, r_ s =-0.89 at >99.5 ), and SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 (RWB, r_ s =0.67 at 91 ).

We checked the correlations between the spectral index and seeing for three RWB sources with mild correlations. With available seeing, we indeed found correlations at similar confidence level as RWB correlations in two sources, SDSS J030907.49+002419.0 ( z=2.083 ), and SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 ( z=0.656 ). Therefore, the effects of host galaxy contribution cannot be completely ruled out, although it is hard to quantitatively evaluate this possibility, and the contribution of host galaxy may not be significant at rest frame wavelength for these two objects.

gmw_00013617.name: SDSS J031027.82-004950.7
gmw_00013617.overview: SDSS J031027.82-004950.7 is a quasar and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1407.3025]. The source exhibits a Baldwin-effect anti-correlation and a weak anti-correlation between its broad-line width and luminosity [arXiv:1407.3025].

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J031027.82-004950.7 as an example quasar showing Baldwin-effect anti-correlation behavior and weak broad-line width/luminosity anti-correlation above 90 percent confidence.

quotes.quote: The examples of anti-correlation and positive correlation are shown in Fig. fig:baldgood for SDSS J031027.82-004950.7 and SDSS J030639.57+000343.1, respectively.

Whereas, anti-correlations are usually weak, with only three sources at >90 confidence level ( in SDSS J022230.28+001844.5, in SDSS J030911.64+002358.8, and in SDSS J031027.82-004950.7, see Tables table:1 table:3 ). In Fig. fig:FWHMgood , the positive and anti-correlation are illustrated with two examples, SDSS J031131.41-002127.4 and SDSS J031027.82-004950.7, for broad , and , respectively.

gmw_00013618.name: SDSS J113627.76+181747.3
gmw_00013618.overview: SDSS J113627.76+181747.3 is a small dwarf galaxy with a diameter of approximately 2 kpc [arXiv:1712.00027]. It has an absolute magnitude of -15.1 mag in the g-band and an estimated oxygen abundance of 8.0 dex [arXiv:1712.00027].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J113627.76+181747.3 as SN 2017dio's host, reports faint photometry and M_g=-15.1 mag, and describes a small dwarf about 2 kpc across with hidden nebular lines and estimated oxygen abundance near 8.0 dex.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy, SDSS J113627.76+181747.3, has u=23.36 , g=21.12 , =20.67 , i=20.55 , and z=20.12 mag. Considering the distance modulus and foreground reddening, the absolute magnitude is M_g = -15.1 mag.

The galaxy appears to be small without a discernible shape. Its diameter of 3 " corresponds to 2 kpc at the distance of 160 Mpc. This suggests that the host is a dwarf galaxy smaller and fainter than the Small Magellanic Cloud M_V = -16.8 mag, mcconachie12 .

In the spectra of SN 2017dio, the interstellar emission lines from the galaxy are hidden by the SN. Therefore, no strong-line analyses such as estimates of star formation rate or metallicity can be done. Employing the galaxy luminosity-metallicity relation of tremonti04 yields an estimate of 12+log(O/H) 8.0 dex for the galaxy.

gmw_00013619.name: PMN J1726+0639
gmw_00013619.overview: PMN J1726+0639 is a compact radio-source active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2204.05847]. It exhibits large intra-hour flux density variations caused by highly anisotropic interstellar scintillation, which may be associated with a nearby local plasma cloud [arXiv:2204.05847].

sources.summary: The paper treats PMN J1726+0639 as a compact radio-source AGN with large intra-hour flux variations, a repeated anisotropic scintillation cycle, and a possible association with a nearby local plasma cloud.

quotes.quote: To test the proposed association between interstellar scintillation and plasma around hot foreground stars, we undertook a survey with the Australia Telscope Compact Array to search for more intra-hour scintillating AGN among 506 compact radio sources. Among them, PMN J1726+0639 Griffith95 was found to show large intra-hour variations in its flux density. In this paper we report the results of tracking the rate of flux density variations in PMN J1726+0639 for just over two years, from February 2018 to March 2020.

Monitoring of PMN J1726+0639 has revealed a strong and repeated annual cycle in the rate of its scintillations. The cycle, which includes two standstills, is consistent with a highly anisotropic model of the scintillation. The position angle of the anisotropy, and the component of transverse scattering screen velocity perpendicular to that position angle, are well determined from the annual cycle. Those determinations show that the scintillation of PMN J1726+0639 is not caused by radially oriented plasma filaments around a nearby hot star, as has been previously suggested for three other IHV sources. We speculate that it may instead be associated with a known, local plasma cloud (within 15 pc ) that has been previously discovered spectroscopically.

gmw_00013620.name: J0103 1104
gmw_00013620.overview: J0103 1104 is a quasar characterized as a low-equivalent-width source [arXiv:2208.02387]. It exhibits strong emission, and its spectrum is inconsistent with the extreme accretion spectral type [arXiv:2208.02387].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0103 1104 as an outlying low-equivalent-width source with strong emission whose spectrum is inconsistent with the xA spectral type.

quotes.quote: The outlying sources identified were J0103 1104, a low EW source with a strong \ emission, and J1231+0725, a BC-dominated spectrum with a faint BLUE.

The vast majority of our sample are xA quasars except for the two sources, J0103 1104 and J1231 + 0726 that were previously identified as outlying sources. They show a spectra not consistent with the xA spectral type and will not be considered for further statistic analysis.

gmw_00013621.name: J1609+0654
gmw_00013621.overview: J1609+0654 is classified as a type-1 quasar [arXiv:2208.02387]. The object exhibits weak broad emission components, which are affected by absorption and a low signal-to-noise ratio [arXiv:2208.02387].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1609+0654 as a type-1 QSO with weak broad components; absorption, low signal-to-noise, and underestimated emission affect its measurements.

quotes.quote: The distribution of the data points involving \ (Fig. fig:redmix left panel) shows a small dispersion from the sample mean with the exception of J1609+0654, a type-1 QSO with weak broad components especially for \ which for this case was more likely underestimated due to absorptions and low S/N.

BC ratios black dots (e.g. J0123+0329, J1545+0156, J1609+0654): this is due to deficient measurements of the \ emission which tend to be underestimated beside the \ feature. The specfit modeling can compensate the surrounding narrow or broad absorptions while the profile measurements take the median intensity over a selected range directly from the spectra and can return close to zero or negative results for low S/N spectra.
The reverse case J1609+0654 is associated with absorption and low S/N at the \ rest frame that effects both the specfit results and the profile ratios.

gmw_00013622.name: J2116+0441
gmw_00013622.overview: J2116+0441 is a quasar classified as an outlying or borderline source in spectral analyses [arXiv:2208.02387]. It exhibits weak blue component emission surrounded by absorptions and affected by poor signal-to-noise, alongside relatively strong emission that may be caused by a higher ionization parameter [arXiv:2208.02387].

sources.summary: The paper flags J2116+0441 as an outlying or borderline source with weak BLUE emission, absorptions, poor signal-to-noise, and relatively prominent emission.

quotes.quote: For most of the sources, the + \ BLUE component emission is slightly weaker than \ BLUE component, resulting in well-behaved distributions with the exception of J2116+0441 and J2145 0758 that show weak + \ BLUE emission surrounded by absorptions and affected by poor S/N.
However, J2116+0441 and J2145-0758 show relatively prominent \ and lower , probably as an effect of a higher ionization parameter.
Sources that have prominent \ BC with respect to CIV BLUE are present in our sample but are labeled as outliers or borderline sources. Two cases in point are J1231+0725 and J2116+0441.

gmw_00013623.name: J2145 0758
gmw_00013623.overview: The quasar J2145 0758 exhibits weak emission in its blue component surrounded by strong absorptions [arXiv:2208.02387]. It also shows relatively strong emission lines and lower values that probably indicate a higher ionization parameter [arXiv:2208.02387].

sources.summary: The paper describes J2145 0758 as a source with weak BLUE emission, absorptions, poor signal-to-noise, BLUE-ratio discrepancies, and relatively prominent emission.

quotes.quote: For most of the sources, the + \ BLUE component emission is slightly weaker than \ BLUE component, resulting in well-behaved distributions with the exception of J2116+0441 and J2145 0758 that show weak + \ BLUE emission surrounded by absorptions and affected by poor S/N.
BLUE ratios black dots (e.g J1231+0725, J0034 0326, J2145 0758): what causes the discrepancies for the BLUE ratios is the strong absorptions in these three features.
However, J2116+0441 and J2145-0758 show relatively prominent \ and lower , probably as an effect of a higher ionization parameter.

gmw_00013624.name: J0946 0124
gmw_00013624.overview: J0946 0124 is classified as a quasar [arXiv:2208.02387]. Spectral analysis supports its classification as an extreme accretion source, although it lies near the borderline of this parameter region [arXiv:2208.02387].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0946 0124 as a borderline object outside the xA parameter region, but its errors and spectrum still support xA classification.

quotes.quote: On the converse, the borderline object J0946 0124 is outside the xA part of the parameter plane. However, its error bars indicate that the criteria are still satisfied within 1 confidence level. The overall appearance of the spectrum (Fig. fig:11 ) supports the classification of J0946 0124 as an xA source.

gmw_00013625.name: J0034 0326
gmw_00013625.overview: J0034 0326 is a quasar characterized by strong spectral features [arXiv:2208.02387]. It exhibits strong absorptions that blur out small radial velocity sections, though its metallicity remains consistent with the sample median [arXiv:2208.02387].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0034 0326 as having strong spectral features and absorptions that distort selected regions, while its metallicity remains near the sample median.

quotes.quote: The values derived from the BC / \ relation are the ones with higher dispersion from the general median especially those with a strong \ feature such as J0034 0326 (Fig. fig:02 ) and J0903+0708 (Fig. fig:16 ).
BLUE ratios black dots (e.g J1231+0725, J0034 0326, J2145 0758): what causes the discrepancies for the BLUE ratios is the strong absorptions in these three features.

J0034 0326 is an example where absorptions blur out small radial velocity sections in both the aforementioned spectral regions. The metallicity of these sources remains consistent with the median value of the sample.

gmw_00013626.name: C49
gmw_00013626.overview: C49 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 3 [arXiv:1404.0125]. Although it was initially observed to have a strong Lyman continuum signal, subsequent observations revealed that this apparent flux actually arises from a lower-redshift contaminating object [arXiv:1404.0125].

sources.summary: The paper treats C49 as an exceptional z ~3 LBG whose apparent strong LyC signal dominated a previous average, but later work found the LyC flux came from lower-redshift contamination, implying little or no escape.

quotes.quote: These results include two exceptional cases, F_ 1500 /F_ 900 (C49) = 12.7 1.8 ( \ = 65 percent) and F_ 1500 /F_ 900 (D3) = 7.5 1.0 ( \ 100 percent), that contribute nearly all of the LyC flux to the average. Subsequent observations, however, reveal that in one case the observed LyC flux may be line-of-sight contamination and in the other case the result of scattered light The apparent LyC flux from C49 has been found to arise from a lower redshift object contaminating the field nestor13 . Thus it is likely that this object has little to no escaping LyC flux.

gmw_00013627.name: D3
gmw_00013627.overview: D3 is a Lyman-break galaxy located at a high redshift of approximately z ~ 3 [arXiv:1404.0125]. Although early spectroscopy suggested near-total Lyman continuum escape, subsequent observations detected no Lyman continuum flux from the galaxy [arXiv:1404.0125].

sources.summary: The paper treats D3 as an exceptional z ~3 LBG with apparent near-total LyC escape in earlier spectroscopy, but later data found no LyC flux and left the earlier signal unresolved, possibly from a nearby bright object.

quotes.quote: These results include two exceptional cases, F_ 1500 /F_ 900 (C49) = 12.7 1.8 ( \ = 65 percent) and F_ 1500 /F_ 900 (D3) = 7.5 1.0 ( \ 100 percent), that contribute nearly all of the LyC flux to the average. Subsequent observations, however, reveal that in one case the observed LyC flux may be line-of-sight contamination and in the other case the result of scattered light The apparent LyC flux from C49 has been found to arise from a lower redshift object contaminating the field nestor13 . Thus it is likely that this object has little to no escaping LyC flux. nestor13 detect no LyC flux from object D3 to the limits of their data. The source of the observed LyC flux in the deep spectroscopy is still unresolved but may be caused by a nearby bright object as discussed in shapley06 .

gmw_00013628.name: UVQSJ015454.68 071222.2
gmw_00013628.overview: UVQSJ015454.68 071222.2 is a luminous quasar at a redshift of z=1.289 [arXiv:1602.06255]. It was historically cataloged as PHL 1228 and initially identified as a candidate faint blue halo star [arXiv:1602.06255].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UVQSJ015454.68 071222.2 as a luminous z=1.289 UVQS quasar with FUV=17.07 mag, previously cataloged as PHL 1228 and treated as a faint blue halo-star candidate.

quotes.quote: One of the most luminous quasars from our survey, UVQSJ015454.68 071222.2 ( z=1.289 , FUV=17.07 mag; Figure  fig:ex_spec ), has an interesting history worth relating. This source was cataloged in 1962 by Haro Luyten as PHL 1228 haro62 . Based on its color and coordinates, those authors identified the source as a candidate faint blue halo star towards the South Galactic pole.

gmw_00013629.name: EDCSNJ1138096 1135223
gmw_00013629.overview: EDCSNJ1138096 1135223 is a faint galaxy that was previously misclassified as an early-type candidate [arXiv:1007.1425]. It is not an elliptical galaxy, displaying a very perturbed morphology and a color that is substantially bluer than the red-sequence [arXiv:1007.1425].

sources.summary: The paper identifies EDCSNJ1138096 1135223 as a misclassified early-type candidate: it is not elliptical, has perturbed morphology, is blue and faint, and was removed from the EDisCS early-type galaxy sample.

quotes.quote: The first one, EDCSNJ1138096 1135223, is clearly not an elliptical and shows a very perturbed morphology.

The last two lines of Table  outsiders show some of the properties of these two galaxies and their HST F814W images are shown in the bottom row of Figure  mosaic . It is not surprising that both galaxies are substantially bluer than the red-sequence. They are also quite faint, where visual classification is, perforce, less reliable. These two misclassified galaxies were removed from our sample and will not be discussed further.

gmw_00013630.name: EDCSNJ1138127 1134190
gmw_00013630.overview: EDCSNJ1138127 1134190 is a faint, blue galaxy located in a dense group of probably interacting galaxies [arXiv:1007.1425]. It is positioned very close to a brighter elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1007.1425].

sources.summary: The paper says EDCSNJ1138127 1134190 was misclassified in a dense, probably interacting group near a brighter elliptical; it is blue, faint, and was removed from the early-type sample.

quotes.quote: The second one, EDCSNJ1138127 1134190, is in a dense group of (probably) interacting galaxies and there is a brighter elliptical very close to the position of this object. It is obvious that the wrong galaxy was classified.

The last two lines of Table  outsiders show some of the properties of these two galaxies and their HST F814W images are shown in the bottom row of Figure  mosaic . It is not surprising that both galaxies are substantially bluer than the red-sequence. They are also quite faint, where visual classification is, perforce, less reliable. These two misclassified galaxies were removed from our sample and will not be discussed further.

gmw_00013631.name: G1 galaxy
gmw_00013631.overview: The G1 galaxy is an emission-line galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.554 [arXiv:2211.05810]. It is identified as the most likely host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst GRB 180618A, which occurred in its outskirts at an offset of approximately 10 kpc [arXiv:2211.05810].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G1 as the most likely GRB 180618A host, reporting emission-line redshift z=0.554, low accidental-alignment probability, and a roughly 10 kpc offset from the burst.

quotes.quote: Among all three candidate galaxies detected in the LBT images, G1 is the most likely host galaxy of GRB 180618A given its proximity (see Figure fig:fov ). In G1 galaxy spectrum (see Figure fig:GRB18_redshiftspec ), we detect two emission lines at _ obs, OII =5794.8 and _ obs, OIII =7784.0 , corresponding to the unresolved [ OII ] = 3726-3729 doublet and the [ OIII ] = 5007 line, respectively. That implies a spectroscopic redshift z_ G1 =0.554 0.001 for the G1 galaxy. This value is consistent with and estimates of photometric redshifts z=0.4^ +0.2 _ 0.1 and z=0.52^ +0.09 _ 0.11 , respectively. We find that the probability of an accidental alignment 2002AJ....123.1111B,2010ApJ...722.1946B,2013ApJ...776...18F of the GRB 180618A and G1 galaxy is low, with p_ d 0.02 .
The GRB 180618A lies in the outskirts of its host, at 10 kpc from the centre of the galaxy (see also 2022arXiv220409059O,2022arXiv220601763F ).

gmw_00013632.name: G3 galaxy
gmw_00013632.overview: The G3 galaxy is cataloged by the Sloan Digital Sky Survey and has a photometric redshift of approximately 0.54 [arXiv:2211.05810]. It is located near two other galaxies, G1 and G2, which share a similar redshift [arXiv:2211.05810].

sources.summary: The paper lists G3 among three galaxies near GRB 180618A, noting its SDSS cataloging, photometric redshift around 0.54, r/z-band brightness, and similar redshift to G1 and G2.

quotes.quote: In both images, we found three galaxies (G1, G2, and G3; see Figure fig:fov ) at a projected angular distance of d_ G1 =1.6 arcsec, d_ G2 = 3.7 arcsec, and d_ G3 = 7.7 arcsec from the UVOT sub-arcsec localization of the GRB 180618A 2018GCN.22810....1S . The G3 galaxy was already catalogued by the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS; 2015ApJS..219...12A ), with a photometric redshift of z_ G3 =0.54 0.03 , and brightness r_ band, G3 = 22.4 0.3 mag and z_ band, G3 = 21.2 0.6 mag.
Therefore, the LBT spectroscopic analysis suggests that all three galaxies (G1, G2, and G3) are at a similar redshift.

gmw_00013633.name: J0041 0947
gmw_00013633.overview: J0041 0947 is an active galactic nucleus that hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1610.04221]. The galaxy exhibits approximately 30 percent variability between optical and infrared observations, and its continuum is best modeled with a spinning black hole parameter of 0.9 [arXiv:1610.04221].

sources.summary: The paper reports optical/IR variability in J0041 0947, scales its GNIRS spectrum to SDSS for SED fitting, adopts a spinning-BH continuum model, and finds a significantly improved fit at a_* = 0.9.

quotes.quote: We concluded that in one object (J0041 0947) there was evidence for 30 per cent variability between optical/IR observations. In this case, we used the GNIRS spectrum as observed for estimating _BH, but scaled it to the level of SDSS for SED fitting.

An exception is J0041 0947, for which we adopt the model with a BH spin parameter of a_*=0.9 . As noted in Section sec:sedmodel , this is the one object in our sample where we see a significant improvement in the continuum fit when we introduce a spinning BH ( _ red = 4.16, 0.92 for a_* = 0, 0.9 ).
This is perhaps clearest in J0839 + 5754, but also J0041 0947 and J0118 0052 show only small changes in , despite a 0.4 dex change in _BH from largest to smallest values.

gmw_00013634.name: J1350 + 2652
gmw_00013634.overview: The active galactic nucleus J1350 + 2652 has a low reddening-related uncertainty of approximately 0.03 dex [arXiv:1610.04221]. It exhibits a 2200 Å extinction feature that is best fitted by a Milky Way reddening curve [arXiv:1610.04221].

sources.summary: The paper reports that J1350 + 2652 has low reddening-related uncertainty, a 2200 feature better fitted by a MW reddening curve, and reasonable or slightly improved high-spin SED fits.

quotes.quote: Typical values range from 0.03 dex (J1350 + 2652) to 0.16 dex (J2328 + 1500).

Three objects, J0041 0947, J1350 + 2652 and J2328 + 1500, show an improvement in the \ fitting statistic for the a_*= 0.9 model compared with a_*= 0 , however the difference for the latter two is slight.

Whilst some objects (such as J1350 + 2652) show evidence for a 2200 \ feature that is better fit by a MW reddening curve (Paper I; a similar finding is shown in capellupo15 , their Fig. \ 7), J1044 + 2128 lacks this feature and shows a much improved extinction correction with the SMC curve.

gmw_00013635.name: J2328 + 1500
gmw_00013635.overview: J2328 + 1500 is an active galactic nucleus that hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1610.04221]. It is characterized by strong narrow emission lines, a low accretion rate, and contamination of its spectral energy distribution by the host galaxy [arXiv:1610.04221].

sources.summary: The paper treats J2328 + 1500 as the least luminous, most massive-BH AGN in the sample, with reddening/accretion degeneracy, host-galaxy contamination, and strong narrow emission lines.

quotes.quote: The intrinsic reddening value is consistent in all models, with all but J2328 + 1500 showing very little variation in optical/IR continuum slope. Degeneracy between the accretion rate and intrinsic reddening is evident in J2328 + 1500 however, with the _BH 2 models showing convergence to different optimum values of E ( B-V ) _ int (evinced by the lower SED for these models).

In general, for AGN at z 0.5 the host galaxy flux is assumed to be insignificant ( shen11 ), and indeed we concluded in Paper I that for our least luminous source (J2328 + 1500), the maximum possible contribution to the SED peak (at 2000 A ) was 1 per cent, which increased to 50 per cent at a wavelength of 1 m. Since this object also hosts the most massive BH of our sample, it is expected to exhibit the largest contamination by the host galaxy, based on the _BH--bulge mass relationship (see Section sec:intro ).
In general, the lowest accretion rate objects show the strongest narrow emission lines (J0839 + 5754 and J2328 + 1500 are the clearest examples).

gmw_00013636.name: J1044 + 2128
gmw_00013636.overview: J1044 + 2128 is an active galactic nucleus that hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1610.04221]. Unlike the most massive objects in its class, this lower-mass system is incompatible with hosting a highly spinning black hole, and its extinction correction is improved by using a Small Magellanic Cloud curve [arXiv:1610.04221].

sources.summary: The paper uses J1044 + 2128 as a case where moderate spin worsens the X-ray SED fit, the SMC extinction curve improves the correction, and high spin remains constrained.

quotes.quote: In 2/3 of the sample we find that the moderate spin states ( a_*= 0.8, 0.9 ) do not provide as good a fit to the data as the low spin states ( a_*=0, 0.5 ), exhibited by the optical--NIR spectra ( J0839 + 5754) or by the X-ray spectra ( J1044 + 2128).

Whilst some objects (such as J1350 + 2652) show evidence for a 2200 \ feature that is better fit by a MW reddening curve (Paper I; a similar finding is shown in capellupo15 , their Fig. \ 7), J1044 + 2128 lacks this feature and shows a much improved extinction correction with the SMC curve.

We can still conclude that the most massive AGN in our sample are all compatible with hosting highly spinning BHs, whereas the least massive (J1044 + 2128) is not, but reiterate that there are several sources of degeneracy in the models.

gmw_00013637.name: J0043 + 0114
gmw_00013637.overview: J0043 + 0114 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1610.04221]. The galaxy exhibits weak emission-line components, and its spectral energy distribution shows model differences that are confined mainly to the red part of the spectrum [arXiv:1610.04221].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0043 + 0114 as an AGN whose outer-disc-radius model differences appear mainly in the red spectrum, with weak emission-line components and acceptable high-spin fits.

quotes.quote: In the majority of cases, the model differences are confined mainly to the red part of the spectrum, (see J0043 + 0114 in Fig. \ fig:seds_rout ) as this is where the emission corresponding to the outer AD emerges.
We fit each member with two Gaussians, or one Gaussian for objects showing particularly weak emission (J0043 + 0114, J0157 0048, J1021 + 1315, J1044 + 2128 and J1240 + 4740).

In half of the sample, this produces a poorer fit to the data, but this is not true for all objects; J0041 0947, J0043 + 0114, J1240 + 4740, J1350 + 2652, J2328 + 1500 and J2332 + 0000 all show reasonable fits ( <3 ) for the a_* = 0.9 model.

gmw_00013638.name: J1021 + 1315
gmw_00013638.overview: J1021 + 1315 is an active galactic nucleus characterized by a high accretion rate [arXiv:1610.04221]. The galaxy exhibits extremely weak narrow emission lines, and its spectral profile suggests the potential presence of an additional Gaussian component [arXiv:1610.04221].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1021 + 1315 as a high-accretion-rate AGN with weak narrow emission, a possible extra Gaussian spectral component, and a narrow 5000 feature attributed to noise.

quotes.quote: We fit each member with two Gaussians, or one Gaussian for objects showing particularly weak emission (J0043 + 0114, J0157 0048, J1021 + 1315, J1044 + 2128 and J1240 + 4740).

Finally we note that the profile of in J1044 + 2128 (and to a smaller degree J0043 + 0114 and J1021 + 1315, see Figs. \ fig:specdecomp1 and fig:specdecomp2 ) suggests that addition of another Gaussian component may result in an improved fit.

Similarly, the highest accretion rate objects (particularly J0043 + 0114, J1021 + 1315, J1044 + 2128 and J1240 + 4740) show extremely weak narrow . (The narrow feature at 5000 \ in J1021 + 1315 is attributed to noise, as there is a corresponding feature in the error array.)

gmw_00013639.name: J1240 + 4740
gmw_00013639.overview: J1240 + 4740 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1610.04221] that exhibits extremely weak narrow emission lines [arXiv:1610.04221]. This weak emission is associated with a high accretion rate [arXiv:1610.04221].

sources.summary: The paper mentions J1240 + 4740 in three analysis results: weak modeled emission, reasonable a_* = 0.9 spin fit, and extremely weak narrow emission associated with high accretion rate.

quotes.quote: We fit each member with two Gaussians, or one Gaussian for objects showing particularly weak emission (J0043 + 0114, J0157 0048, J1021 + 1315, J1044 + 2128 and J1240 + 4740).

In half of the sample, this produces a poorer fit to the data, but this is not true for all objects; J0041 0947, J0043 + 0114, J1240 + 4740, J1350 + 2652, J2328 + 1500 and J2332 + 0000 all show reasonable fits ( <3 ) for the a_* = 0.9 model.
Similarly, the highest accretion rate objects (particularly J0043 + 0114, J1021 + 1315, J1044 + 2128 and J1240 + 4740) show extremely weak narrow .

gmw_00013640.name: J100234.8+024253.2
gmw_00013640.overview: J100234.8+024253.2 is a compact or dwarf galaxy with a star-forming spectrum [arXiv:1403.3615]. Its spectrum and X-ray luminosity suggest that the galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1403.3615].

sources.summary: The paper flags this source as a total-SDSS SFR outlier, notes its star-forming spectrum and compact or dwarf appearance, and says its spectrum and X-ray luminosity suggest an AGN.

quotes.quote: J100234.8+024253.2: ID 118584: Another outlier in Fig. total_SDSS . Spectrally classified as SF, it was not morphologically classified, visually it looks like a compact or dwarf galaxy. The spectrum suggests to host an active nuclei and its X-ray luminosity is typical of an AGN (L _ x = 2.56 10^ 42 erg/s).

gmw_00013641.name: J100229.04+023245.8
gmw_00013641.overview: J100229.04+023245.8 is a distant galaxy spectrally classified as star-forming [arXiv:1403.3615]. Morphologically, it is classified as an Sab galaxy, though visual inspection suggests it could also be an S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1403.3615].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this source as a distant H-alpha SFR outlier, classifies it spectrally as star-forming and morphologically as Sab, and finds no unusual spectrum explaining the discrepancy.

quotes.quote: J100229.04+023245.8: ID 118599: This is one of the most distant outliers in Fig. total_Ha . Spectrally classified as SF. It is morphologically classified as Sab, although visually could also be an S0. Nothing strange in its spectrum which could a priori explain the discrepancy between the SFRs.

gmw_00013642.name: J100116.79+021712.1
gmw_00013642.overview: J100116.79+021712.1 is a star-forming galaxy that is classified as an H-alpha star formation rate outlier [arXiv:1403.3615]. Optical imaging reveals a very bright structure near the galactic bulge, which may be an H II region or a nearby contaminating source [arXiv:1403.3615].

sources.summary: The paper marks this source as an H-alpha SFR outlier with a star-forming classification, an unusual line-ratio spectrum, and a bright bulge-region structure possibly related to HII emission or contamination.

quotes.quote: J100116.79+021712.1: ID 118280: Outlier in Fig. total_Ha . Spectrally classified as SF, has an interesting spectrum with / [ NII ] ratio almost 1:1 and in absorption. Besides, the image shows a very bright structure near the bulge of the galaxy (HII region or powerful contaminating source near the galactic centre?).

gmw_00013643.name: J105150.4+573906.1
gmw_00013643.overview: J105150.4+573906.1 is a star-forming galaxy morphologically classified as Scd, though it visually resembles an S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1403.3615]. Its spectrum exhibits a weak starburst, characterized by absorption features and intense emission at 410 nm [arXiv:1403.3615].

sources.summary: The paper treats this source as a total-UV SFR outlier, calls it a star-forming Scd galaxy that could visually be S0, and reports weak-starburst spectral features.

quotes.quote: J105150.4+573906.1: ID 313631: Outlier in Fig. total_UV . SF galaxy morphologically classified as Scd but which visually could be an S0. Its spectrum shows a weak starburst, in absorption and an intense emission at 410 nm.

gmw_00013644.name: J095852.79+022603.1
gmw_00013644.overview: J095852.79+022603.1 is a massive lenticular galaxy with an S0 morphology [arXiv:1403.3615]. It exhibits a post-starburst-like spectrum with weak emission lines, low star formation, and far-infrared and far-ultraviolet fluxes that may be influenced by old stellar populations [arXiv:1403.3615].

sources.summary: The paper describes this total-UV outlier as an unclassified S0 galaxy with a post-starburst-like spectrum, low star formation, and FIR/FUV fluxes possibly affected by old stars.

quotes.quote: J095852.79+022603.1: ID 118534: Outlier in Fig. total_UV , unclassified spectral galaxy with S0 morphology. Its spectrum looks like a post-starburst (weak emission line, in absorption and no X-ray detection). It is a massive galaxy with low star formation, its FIR and FUV fluxes could be affected by old stellar populations.

gmw_00013645.name: J100322.08+025001.1
gmw_00013645.overview: J100322.08+025001.1 is an unclassified galaxy with an elliptical morphology [arXiv:1403.3615]. It exhibits a typical early-type galaxy spectrum with no evidence of active star formation [arXiv:1403.3615].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this total-UV outlier as an unclassified E-morphology galaxy with an early-type spectrum lacking star formation, noting possible FUV contamination affecting SFR estimates.

quotes.quote: J100322.08+025001.1: ID 118620: Outlier in Fig. total_UV , unclassified spectral galaxy with E morphology. Typical ETGs spectrum with no evidence of star formation. It has been mentioned in the article the possibility of FUV flux contamination for the ETGs galaxies which could affect the SFR _ UV estimation.

gmw_00013646.name: A514S
gmw_00013646.overview: The galaxy A514S is the host of the active galactic nucleus PKS 0446-20 [arXiv:2310.00914]. It exhibits extended, bent radio emission originating from two radio lobes that form a bridge, arc, and tail structure [arXiv:2310.00914].

sources.summary: The paper treats A514S as the host galaxy of AGN PKS 0446-20 and describes unusually long bent radio emission, with bridge, arc, and tail structures whose flux and spectrum vary with distance.

quotes.quote: The radio emission originates from the two radio lobes of the AGN located near the center of the southern subcluster and extends towards the southern outskirts with multiple bends. Its peculiar morphology is characterized by a 400 kpc ``bridge'', a 300 kpc ``arc'', and a 400 kpc ``tail'', which together contribute to its largest linear size of 0.7 Mpc. We find that both the flux and spectral features of the emission change with the distance from the AGN.

In this letter, we focus on the southern radio emission originating from the AGN PKS 0446-20 (hereafter A514S), which exhibits a remarkably peculiar morphology. 2001A&A...379..807G have reported two radio lobes with a hint of extended radio emission towards the south. We found that this radio feature extends much longer than what has been reported.
One possible source of jet redistribution is the motion of the host galaxy A514S. If the host galaxy falls into the cluster with a high velocity, its jet will lag behind it.

gmw_00013647.name: QSO J0633 2333
gmw_00013647.overview: QSO J0633 2333 is a quasar [arXiv:2003.07472]. High-resolution imaging of the source reveals a pointlike structure with almost unit image coherence [arXiv:2003.07472].

sources.summary: The paper uses QSO J0633 2333 as a Band 8 imaging target to compare B2B and in-band phase referencing, finding nearly unit coherence and pointlike images.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:10 shows a subset of three out of the four Band 8 images from the consecutive experiments on 2017 July 18 targeting QSO J0633 2333 using B2B (left) and in-band (right) phase referencing with the switching cycle time of 60 s obtained by culling phase calibrator scans.

The top row images of Figure fig:10 indicates that the peak flux densities are almost identical for B2B and in-band phase referencing---that is, the image coherence is almost unity---and that the image structure is pointlike with few defects, although the B2B phase referencing image noise is higher. The increased image noise is due to inaccuracies in the DGC. After self-calibration, the image noise of the B2B and in-band phase referencing are equivalent, indeed confirming that the residual offsets have been fully corrected.

gmw_00013648.name: QSO J2228 0753
gmw_00013648.overview: QSO J2228 0753 is a quasar observed as a continuum point-source target [arXiv:2003.07472]. High-resolution observations of the source were conducted in ALMA Bands 7 and 8, achieving high image coherence [arXiv:2003.07472].

sources.summary: The paper observes QSO J2228 0753 as a continuum point-source target in ALMA Bands 7 and 8, achieving high-resolution images and strong B2B coherence.

quotes.quote: The QSO J2228 0753 was observed as a continuum point-source target in Bands 7 and 8, while QSO J2229 0832, with a separation angle of 0.^ 7 , was observed as a phase calibrator at an LF. We selected a bright QSO, J2253 + 1608, as a DGC source located 25^ away from the target. The high LO1 frequencies are 289 (Band 7) and 405 (Band 8) GHz, while the corresponding low LO1 frequencies are 96 (Band 3) and 135 (Band 4) GHz, respectively.

The left and right panels of Figure fig:13 show the synthesized images in Bands 7 and 8, respectively, using a Briggs robust 0.5 weighting in CASA CLEAN when combining two EBs taken in the middle of October and the beginning of 2017 November. The achieved beam sizes are 19 16 and 16 12 mas in Bands 7 and 8, respectively. Further details of the observations and data analysis results are described in another paper Asaki2019 . We also performed phase self-calibration with a solution interval of the target scan length and obtained the images free from the atmospheric and instrumental phase errors in order to investigate the image coherence. We obtained high image coherences of 94 and 84 in Bands 7 and 8, respectively, so that B2B phase referencing works effectively in those experiments.

gmw_00013649.name: Source ID 382
gmw_00013649.overview: Source ID 382 is a normal high-redshift galaxy with a photometric redshift of 6.14 [arXiv:2208.06364]. It has a delensed stellar mass of 3e8 solar masses and a best-fit dust extinction of A_V = 0.4 [arXiv:2208.06364].

sources.summary: The paper presents Source ID 382 as a z_phot=6.14 normal high-redshift galaxy detected at 7.7 and 3.6 m, with good SED fitting, A_V=0.4, and delensed stellar mass 3e8 solar masses.

quotes.quote: Examples of 7.7 m sources at z>6 Fig. fig:seds shows two examples of 7.7 m normal galaxies at z>6 . The postage stamps show their clear detection at both 7.7 and 3.6 m . In both cases, the resulting SED fitting is of very good quality. Even the 7.7 m photometric point, which was not used for the SED fitting, is in excellent agreement with the extrapolation of the best-fit template at observed wavelengths >5 m . Source ID 382 at z_ phot = 6.14 has a best-fit dust extinction A_V = 0.4 and delensed stellar mass of 3 10^ 8 M_ .

gmw_00013650.name: ID 399
gmw_00013650.overview: ID 399 is a normal high-redshift galaxy with a photometric redshift of 6.86 [arXiv:2208.06364]. It is clearly detected at both 7.7 and 3.6 micrometers, exhibiting a delensed stellar mass of 2e10 solar masses and a best-fit dust extinction of zero [arXiv:2208.06364].

sources.summary: The paper presents ID 399 as a z_phot=6.86 normal high-redshift galaxy detected at 7.7 and 3.6 m, with high-quality SED fitting, A_V=0, and delensed stellar mass 2e10 solar masses.

quotes.quote: Examples of 7.7 m sources at z>6 Fig. fig:seds shows two examples of 7.7 m normal galaxies at z>6 . The postage stamps show their clear detection at both 7.7 and 3.6 m . In both cases, the resulting SED fitting is of very good quality. Even the 7.7 m photometric point, which was not used for the SED fitting, is in excellent agreement with the extrapolation of the best-fit template at observed wavelengths >5 m .
The other source, ID 399 at z_ phot = 6.86 , instead, has a best-fit dust extinction A_V =0 and a delensed stellar mass of 2 10^ 10 M_ .
